etymology.story: late 14c., "cross out with lines," from Anglo-French canceler, from Latin cancellare "to make resemble a lattice," which in Late Latin took on a sense "cross out something written" by marking it with crossed lines, from cancelli, plural of cancellus "lattice, grating," diminutive of cancer "crossed bars, lattice," a variant of carcer "prison" (see incarceration).

meanings.definitionCn: 爆炸的；爆炸性的；爆发性的

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + 爆发/鼓掌 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: 1660s, "tending to explode," from Latin explos-, past participle stem of explodere "drive out, reject" (see explosion) + -ive.

morphology.mnemonic: rise high and roll forcefully + on

meanings.definitionCn: 生硬的；突然的；唐突的；陡峭的

etymology.story: 1580s, from Latin abruptus "broken off, precipitous, disconnected," past participle of abrumpere "break off," from ab- "off" (see ab-) + rumpere "break" (see rupture (n.)).
mid-15c., discoragen, from Middle French descourager, from Old French descoragier, from des- "away" (see dis-) + corage (see courage).

meanings.definitionCn: 自发的；自然的；无意识的

etymology.story: 1650s, "occurring without external stimulus," from Late Latin spontaneus "willing, of one's free will," from Latin (sua) sponte "of one's own accord, willingly;" of uncertain origin.

meanings.definitionCn: 破坏；失事；残骸；失去健康的人

etymology.story: late 14c., "young of the coney," from Walloon robète or a similar French dialect word, diminutive of Flemish or Middle Dutch robbe "rabbit," of unknown origin.
1580s, probably from gloom (n.) even though that word is not attested as early as this one.
mid-14c., reprochen "to rebuke, reproach," from  Anglo-French repruchier, Old French reprochier "upbraid, blame, accuse, speak ill of," from reproche (see reproach (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 根据；证明；正当理由；委任状

etymology.story: late 13c., "to keep safe from danger," from Old North French warantir "safeguard, protect; guarantee, pledge" (Old French garantir), from warant (see warrant (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 目的；利益；理由；日本米酒

etymology.story: early 14c., concelen, from Old French conceler "to hide, conceal, dissimulate," from Latin concelare "to hide," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + celare "to hide," from PIE root *kel- (2) "to cover, conceal" (see cell).

meanings.definitionCn: 吸，闻；嗤之以鼻；气味；以鼻吸气；吸气声
叙事的，叙述的；叙事体的

etymology.story: "a tale, story," 1560s, from Middle French narrative and from narrative (adj.)..

meanings.definitionCn: 有能力的，能胜任的；足够的

etymology.story: late 14c., "suitable," from Old French competent "sufficient, appropriate, suitable," from Latin competentem (nominative competens), present participle of competere "coincide, agree" (see compete).

morphology.mnemonic: in + vest + 结果/过程/状态

etymology.story: 1590s, "act of putting on vestments" (a sense now found in investiture); later "act of being invested with an office, right, endowment, etc." (1640s); and "surrounding and besieging" of a military target (1811); from invest + -ment.

meanings.definitionCn: 同时发生；巧合；一致；相符；

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 落入/巧合

etymology.story: 1705, "be identical in substance or nature," but from 1640s as a verb in English in Latin form, "occupy the same space, agree in position," from Medieval Latin coincidere (used in astrology), literally "to fall upon together," from Latin com- "together" (see co-) + incidere "to fall upon" (in- "upon" + cadere "to fall;" see case (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 打草稿用的；凑合的；碰巧的

morphology.mnemonic: ring环，圆 + 与…有关的/像…的
共同/联合 + 工作 + 使…/具有…的 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: early 15c., from Old French triple or directly from Latin triplus (see triple (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 脾气；韧度vt.调和，使缓和；使回火
向旁边(的),侧身,横着(的),斜着(的)

etymology.story: 1570s, from side (n.) + way (n.), with adverbial genitive.

late 13c., from Old French statut, estatut "(royal) promulgation, (legal) statute," from Late Latin statutum "a law, decree," noun use of neuter past participle of Latin statuere "enact, establish," from status "condition, position" (see status)..

Old English clif "rock, promontory, steep slope," from Proto-Germanic *kliban (source also of Old Saxon clif, Old Norse klif, Middle Dutch klippe, Dutch klip, Old High German klep, German Klippe "cliff, promontory, steep rock").

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + 结束/限制 + ite

etymology.story: late 14c., "eternal, limitless," also "extremely great in number," from Old French infinit "endless, boundless" and directly from Latin infinitus "unbounded, unlimited, countless, numberless," from in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + finitus "defining, definite," from finis "end" (see finish (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 充足，足够；vt.(食物等)使(某人)满足
值得注意的，显著的；著名的

etymology.story: mid-14c., from Old French notable "well-known, notable, remarkable" (13c.) and directly from Latin notabilis "noteworthy, extraordinary," from notare "to note" (see note (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 承认；容许；(比赛结束前)认输；退让

etymology.story: 1630s, from Middle French concéder or directly from Latin concedere "give way, yield, go away, depart, retire," figuratively "agree, consent, give precedence," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + cedere "to go, grant, give way" (see cede).

meanings.definitionCn: 选举,推选;选择,作出选择

etymology.story: early 15c., of action, "voluntary;" of persons, "taken in preference to others," especially "chosen by God for some special purpose," from Latin electus, past participle of eligere "to pick out, choose" (see election).
1630s, from self- + -ish.

meanings.definitionCn: 蒸发,挥发;沉淀;发射;消失

morphology.mnemonic: 出/从 + vapor + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: early 15c., "draw off (humors or spirits) as vapor," from Late Latin evaporatum, past participle of evaporare "disperse in vapor" (see evaporation).

meanings.definitionCn: 委员会；佣金；犯；委任；委任状

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 发送 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: also stair-case, 1620s, originally the enclosure of the stairs, from stair + case (n.2) in its sense "frame;" compare former window-case, door-case..

late 14c., "pertaining to reason;" mid-15c., "endowed with reason," from Old French racionel and directly from Latin rationalis "of or belonging to reason, reasonable," from ratio (genitive rationis) "reckoning, calculation, reason" (see ratio)..

meanings.definitionCn: 再继续,重新开始;再用;恢复

etymology.story: also résumé, 1804, "a summary," from French résumé, noun use of past participle of Middle French resumer "to sum up," from Latin resumere (see resume (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 职业;召唤;天命;天职;才能

etymology.story: early 15c., "spiritual calling," from Old French vocacion "call, consecration; calling, profession" (13c.) or directly from Latin vocationem (nominative vocatio), literally "a calling, a being called" from vocatus "called," past participle of vocare "to call" (see voice (n.)).

"lame," in Old English lemphalt "limping," from Proto-Germanic *haltaz (source also of Old Saxon, Old Frisian halt, Old Norse haltr, Old High German halz, Gothic halts "lame"), from PIE *keld-, from root *kel- "to strike, cut," with derivatives meaning "something broken or cut off" (source also of Russian koldyka "lame," Greek kolobos "broken, curtailed").
late 14c., from sun (n.) + set (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 明显的；显著的；平淡无奇的

etymology.story: 1580s, "frequently met with," from Latin obvius "that is in the way, presenting itself readily, open, exposed, commonplace," from obviam (adv.) "in the way," from ob "against" (see ob-) + viam, accusative of via "way" (see via).

meanings.definitionCn: 外科；外科手术；手术室；诊疗室

morphology.mnemonic: 暴力/违反 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: mid-14c., from Old French violent or directly from Latin violentus, related to violare (see violation).

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 太阳/独自

etymology.story: 1560s, "to stand in front of," from Middle French confronter (15c.), from Medieval Latin confrontare "assign limits, adjoin," from Latin com- "together" (see com-) + frontem (nominative frons) "forehead" (see front (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 守信的， 忠实的； 如实的

etymology.story: early 14c., "sincerely religious, devout, pious," especially in reference to Christian practice; mid-14c., "loyal (to a lord, friend, spouse, etc.); true; honest, trustworthy," from faith + -ful.

meanings.definitionCn: 生殖;翻版;繁殖;复制,仿造

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 向前/支持 + 引导

etymology.story: 1610s, "to produce again," from re- "again" + produce (v.), probably on model of French reproduire (16c.).

meanings.definitionCn: 把挤在一起；使缩成一团；草率了事

etymology.story: 1570s, "to heap or crowd together," probably from Low German hudern "to cover, to shelter" (of hens on chicks or nurses with children), from Middle Low German huden "to cover up," which is probably a frequentative form from Proto-Germanic *hud-, from PIE *keudh-, extended form of the root *(s)keu- "to cover, conceal" (see hide (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 骨架，骨骼；纲要；骨瘦如柴的人
骨骼的；骨瘦如柴的；概略的

etymology.story: 1570s, from Modern Latin sceleton "bones, bony framework of the body," from Greek skeleton soma "dried-up body, mummy, skeleton," from neuter of skeletos "dried-up" (also, as a noun, "dried body, mummy"), from skellein "dry up, make dry, parch," from PIE root *skele- "to parch, wither" (see sclero-).

meanings.definitionCn: 文盲的；不识字的；没受教育的

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + 石 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: early 15c., "uneducated, unable to read and write" (originally meaning Latin), from Latin illiteratus "unlearned, unlettered, ignorant; without culture, inelegant," from assimilated form of in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + literatus "educated," literally "furnished with letters" (see literate).

meanings.definitionCn: 交叉；十字路口；交集；交叉点

morphology.mnemonic: 之间/相互 + cut切割 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1550s, "act or fact of crossing," from Middle French intersection (14c.) and directly from Latin intersectionem (nominative intersectio) "a cutting asunder, intersection," noun of action from past participle stem of intersecare "intersect, cut asunder," from inter- "between" (see inter-) + secare "to cut" (see section (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: school + 与…有关的/像…的 + 身份/状态/技能

etymology.story: 1530s, "status of a scholar," from scholar + -ship.

late 14c., from Latin praepositionem (nominative praepositio) "a putting before, a prefixing," noun of action from past participle stem of praeponere "put before," from prae "before" (see pre-) + ponere "put, set, place" (past participle positus; see position (n.)).
late 14c., from Latin dispersus, past participle of dispergere "to scatter," from dis- "apart, in every direction" (see dis-) + spargere "to scatter" (see sparse).

early 14c., "illusory appearance," from Old French fantaisie, phantasie "vision, imagination" (14c.), from Latin phantasia, from Greek phantasia "power of imagination; appearance, image, perception," from phantazesthai "picture to oneself," from phantos "visible," from phainesthai "appear," in late Greek "to imagine, have visions," related to phaos, phos "light," phainein "to show, to bring to light" (see phantasm).

meanings.definitionCn: 心智健全的,神志清醒的,明智的,稳健的

etymology.story: 1721, back-formation from sanity or else from Latin sanus "sound, healthy," in figurative or transferred use, "of sound mind, rational, sane," also, of style, "correct;" of uncertain origin.
mid-15c., from Latin frustratus, past participle of frustrari "to deceive, disappoint, make vain," from frustra (adv.) "in vain, in error," related to fraus "injury, harm" (see fraud).

morphology.mnemonic: 出/从 + 咬，啮 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1540s, from Middle French erosion (16c.), from Latin erosionem (nominative erosio) "a gnawing away," noun of action from past participle stem of erodere "gnaw away" (see erosion).
late 14c., from Latin ascendere "to climb up, mount, ascend," figuratively "to rise, reach," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + scandere "to climb" (see scan (v.)).
Old English hearmian "to hurt, injure," from the noun (see harm (n.)).
"with condition that," early 15c., conjunction use of past participle of provide.

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + 分配/免除 + able

etymology.story: 1530s, "not subject to dispensation," from Medieval Latin *indispensabilis, from in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + dispensabilis, from Latin dispensare "disburse, administer, distribute (by weight)" (see dispense).
1690s, "glass to regulate light rays," from Latin lens (genitive lentis) "a lentil," on analogy of the double-convex shape.

meanings.definitionCn: 体现，使具体化；具体表达

etymology.story: 1540s, in reference to a soul or spirit invested with a physical form; from 1660s of principles, ideas, etc.; from em- (1) "in" + body (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 任何地方都不， 没有地方 <

etymology.story: Old English nahwær "nowhere, not at all;" see no + where.
late 14c., from Old French motif "moving" or directly from Medieval Latin motivus "moving, impelling," from past participle stem of movere "to move" (see move (v.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 浪漫的,传奇式的;不切实际的,好幻想的

etymology.story: 1778, "look in the face of," from French envisager "look in the face of," from en- "in" (see en- (1)) + visage "face" (see visage).

meanings.definitionCn: 促动；激励，鼓励，作为…的动机

etymology.story: 1863, "to stimulate toward action," from motive + -ate (2); perhaps modeled on French motiver or German motivieren.

meanings.definitionCn: 乘；使增加；使繁殖；使相乘

etymology.story: mid-12c., multeplier, "to cause to become many," from Old French multiplier, mouteplier (12c.) "increase, get bigger; flourish; breed; extend, enrich," from Latin multiplicare "to increase," from multiplex (genitive multiplicis) "having many folds, many times as great in number," from comb.

late 14c., of animals, "to graze;" early 15c., of humans, "to lead to pasture, to feed by putting in a pasture," from Old French pasturer (12c., Modern French pâturer, from pasture (see pasture (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1680s, from approve + -al (2).
1640s, "involving many parts," from French multiple (14c.), from Late Latin multiplus "manifold," from Latin multi- "many, much" (see multi-) + -plus "-fold" (see -plus).

meanings.definitionCn: 通用的;多才多艺的,多方面的
磁铁；[电磁] 磁体；磁石

etymology.story: also exposé, "display of discreditable information," 1803, initially as a French word; noun use of past participle of French exposer "lay open" (see expose (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 集中；浓缩；全神贯注；聚集

root.meaning: to rest as at a center

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + to rest as at a center + 使…/具有…的
出/向外/前任 + 爆发/鼓掌 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1620s, "action of driving out with violence and noise," from French explosion, from Latin explosionem (nominative explosio) "a driving off by clapping," noun of action from past participle stem of explodere "drive out by clapping" (see explode for origin and sense evolution).
1590s, "unite or consolidate by hammering or compression, often after softening by heating," alteration of well (v.) "to boil, rise;" influenced by past participle form welled.
1840, "right to the present time," from phrase up to date, probably originally from bookkeeping.

mid-14c., "removal of a saint's body or relics to a new place," also "rendering of a text from one language to another," from Old French translacion "translation" of text, also of the bones of a saint, etc.
1837, from French personnel (a contrastive term to matériel), noun use of personnel (adj.) "personal," from Old French personel (see personal)..

Old English cancer "spreading sore, cancer" (also canceradl), from Latin cancer "a crab," later, "malignant tumor," from Greek karkinos, which, like the Modern English word, has three meanings: crab, tumor, and the zodiac constellation (late Old English), from PIE root *qarq- "to be hard" (like the shell of a crab); source also of Sanskrit karkatah "crab," karkarah "hard;" and perhaps cognate with PIE root *qar-tu- "hard, strong," source of English hard.

morphology.mnemonic: 超过/外部 + appearance of a person

etymology.story: "mental view or survey," 1742, from out (adv.) + look (v.).
late 14c., "recite a narrative," from Old French romancier "narrate in French; translate into French," from romanz (see romance (n.)).

1530s, from Middle French porcelaine and directly from Italian porcellana "porcelain" (13c.), literally "cowrie shell," the chinaware so called from resemblance of its lustrous transparency to the shiny surface of the shells.

meanings.definitionCn: 神话；虚构的人，虚构的事

etymology.story: 1830, from French Mythe (1818) and directly from Modern Latin mythus, from Greek mythos "speech, thought, story, myth, anything delivered by word of mouth," of unknown origin.General sense of "untrue story, rumor" is from 1840..

meanings.definitionCn: 奇异的， 幻想的， 异想天开的；极好的

morphology.mnemonic: to stir up air + ic + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: late 15c., from fantastic + -al (1).

meanings.definitionCn: 含义；暗示；牵连，卷入；可能的结果，影响

morphology.mnemonic: in + 折叠/交织 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: early 15c., "action of entangling," from Latin implicationem (nominative implicatio) "an interweaving, an entanglement," noun of state from past participle stem of implicare "involve, entangle; embrace; connect closely, associate," from assimilated form of in- "into, in, on, upon" (see in- (2)) + plicare "to fold" (see ply (v.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 凶猛的,残忍的;狂热的,强烈的

etymology.story: mid-13c., "proud, noble, bold, haughty," from Old French fers, fiers, nominative form of fer, fier "strong, overwhelming, violent, fierce, wild; proud, mighty, great, impressive" (Modern French fier "proud, haughty"), from Latin ferus "wild, untamed, uncultivated; waste, desert;" figuratively "wild, uncultivated, savage, cruel," from PIE root *ghwer- "wild; wild animal" (source also of Greek ther, Old Church Slavonic zveri, Lithuanian zveris "wild beast").

root.meaning: self-imposed restraint on freedom of words or actions; habit of keeping back the feelings

morphology.mnemonic: self-imposed restraint on freedom of words or actions; habit of keeping back the feelings + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "act of reserving," from Old French reservation (14c.) and directly from Late Latin reservationem (nominative reservatio), noun of action from past participle stem of Latin reservare (see reserve (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 成批的，大块的；交通堵塞的

etymology.story: "obstruct," 1590s, from French bloquer "to block, stop up," from Old French bloc (see block (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 补品；兴奋剂；甜香酒，甘露酒

morphology.mnemonic: 心 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: late 14c., "of the heart," from Middle French cordial, from Medieval Latin cordialis "of or for the heart," from Latin cor (genitive cordis) "heart," from PIE root *kerd- (1) "heart" (see heart (n.)).
1540s, from Middle French timide "easily frightened, shy" (16c.) and directly from Latin timidus "fearful, afraid, cowardly," from timere "to fear," of uncertain origin.

meanings.definitionCn: 提议,建议;提名,推荐;求婚

etymology.story: mid-14c., from Old French proposer "propose, advance, suggest" (12c.), from pro- "forth" (see pro-) + poser "put, place" (see pose (v.1)).

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 形状/形态

etymology.story: late 13c., from Old French raseor "a razor" (12c.), from raser "to scrape, shave" (see rase).

meanings.definitionCn: 客车， 长途汽车， 大客车；教练； 辅导员， 私人教师
接替的人或事物，继任者

etymology.story: "one who comes after," late 13c., from Anglo-French successor and Old French successour "successor, heir" (12c., Modern French successeur), from Latin successor "follower, successor," agent noun from past participle stem of succedere "to come after" (see succeed)..
Old English pinian "to torture, torment, afflict, cause to suffer," from *pine "pain, torture, punishment," possibly ultimately from Latin poena "punishment, penalty," from Greek poine (see penal).

meanings.definitionCn: 品脱；一品脱的量；一品脱牛奶或啤酒

etymology.story: mid-14c., from Old French pinte "liquid measure, pint" (13c.), probably from Vulgar Latin *pincta (source of Old Provençal, Spanish, Italian pinta), altered from Latin picta "painted," fem.

mid-15c., "walk unsteadily, reel" (intransitive), altered from stakeren (early 14c.), from a Scandinavian source (compare Old Danish stagra, Old Norse stakra "to push, shove, cause to reel," also "to stumble, stagger," perhaps literally "hit with a stick," from Proto-Germanic *stakon- "a stake," from PIE *steg- (1) "pole, stick." Cognate with Dutch staggelen "to stagger," German staggeln "to stammer." Transitive sense of "bewilder, amaze" first recorded 1550s; that of "arrange in a zig-zag pattern" is from 1856.

meanings.definitionCn: 讽刺；反语；具有讽刺意味的事

morphology.mnemonic: shackle with irons + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: "of or resembling iron," late 14c., from iron (n.) + -y (2)..

root.meaning: to affix an ID band to (a wild animal, etc.)

morphology.mnemonic: to affix an ID band to (a wild animal, etc.) + 行为/结果/集合

meanings.definitionCn: 结合；[语] 连接词；同时发生

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 连接 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., originally of planets, from Old French conjonction "union, joining, sexual intercourse" (12c.), from Latin coniunctionem (nominative coniunctio), from past participle stem of coniugare "join together" (see conjugal).

"a number of symptoms occurring together," 1540s, from medical Latin, from Greek syndrome "concurrence of symptoms, concourse of people," from syndromos "place where several roads meet," literally "a running together," from syn- "with" (see syn-) + dromos "a running, course" (see dromedary).

meanings.definitionCn: 整齐的,有秩序的,有条理的

root.meaning: to give orders for or to

morphology.mnemonic: to give orders for or to + 以…方式/副词化

etymology.story: "arranged in order," 1570s, from order (n.) + -ly (1)..

meanings.definitionCn: 拖；麻的粗纤维；拖曳所用之绳

etymology.story: "pull with a rope," Old English togian "to drag, pull," from Proto-Germanic *tugojanan (source also of Old English teon "to draw," Old Frisian togia "to pull about," Old Norse toga, Old High German zogon, German ziehen "to draw, pull, drag"), from PIE root *deuk- "to pull, draw" (source also of Latin ducere "to lead;" see duke (n.)).
Old English mædwe "meadow, pasture," originally "land covered in grass which is mown for hay;" oblique case of mæd (see mead (n.2))..

meanings.definitionCn: 烤，焙；烘，烘烤；暴露于某种热力下以得温暖

etymology.story: early 14c., "meat roasted or for roasting;" see roast (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 拖，拉；用力拖拉；努力得到的结果；捕获物；一网捕获的鱼量；拖运距离

1690s, from sarcasm, perhaps on the model of enthusiastic.

late 14c., "fill with disease, render pestilential; pollute, contaminate; to corrupt morally," from Latin infectus, past participle of inficere "to stain, tinge, dye," also "to corrupt, stain, spoil," literally "to put in to, dip into," from in- "in" (see in- (2)) + facere "to make, do, perform" (see factitious).

early 13c., "ask earnestly, beg," also (c.

early 15c., from Medieval Latin successivus "successive," from success-, stem of Latin succedere "to come after" (see succeed).

1520s, from Latin exterior "outward, outer, exterior," comparative of exterus "on the outside, outward, outer, of another country, foreign," itself a comparative of ex "out of" (see ex-).

late 15c., "likely to happen," from Latin incidentem (nominative incidens), present participle of

etymology.story: early 15c., "military campaign; the act of rapidly setting forth," from Old French expedicion "an expediting, implementation; expedition, mission" (13c.) and directly from Latin expeditionem (nominative expeditio) "an enterprise against an enemy, a military campaign," noun of action from past participle stem of expedire "make ready, prepare" (see expedite).

Influenced in French by cognate Provençal refranhar "singing of birds, refrain." The notion is of something that causes a song to "break off" then resume.

early 15c., from Old French pacte "agreement, treaty, compact" (14c.), from Latin pactum "agreement, contract, covenant," noun use of neuter past participle of pacisci "to covenant, to agree, make a treaty," from PIE root *pag- "fix, join together, unite, make firm" (source also of Sanskrit pasa- "cord, rope," Avestan pas- "to fetter," Greek pegnynai "to fix, make firm, fast or solid," Latin pangere "to fix, to fasten," Slavonic paž "wooden partition," Old English fegan "to join," fon "to catch seize")..

1650s, "freight loaded on a ship," from Spanish cargo "burden," from cargar "to load, impose taxes," from Late Latin carricare "to load on a cart" (see charge (v.)).

1560s, back-formation from exclamation or else from Middle French exclamer (16c.), from Latin exclamare "cry out loud, call out," from ex- "out," or else here as an intensive prefix (see ex-) + clamare "cry, shout, call" (see claim (v.)).
1660s, from French ballette from Italian balletto, diminutive of ballo "a dance" (see ball (n.2)).

late 12c., a shortening of Old English adrædan, contraction of ondrædan "counsel or advise against," also "to dread, fear, be afraid," from on- "against" + rædan "to advise" (see read (v.)).

Old English steorfan "to die" (past tense stearf, past participle storfen), literally "become stiff," from Proto-Germanic *sterban "be stiff" (source also of Old Frisian sterva, Old Saxon sterban, Dutch sterven, Old High German sterban "to die," Old Norse stjarfi "tetanus"), from PIE root *ster- (1) "stiff, rigid" (see stereo-).

1868, "sketch of the plot of a play," from Italian scenario, from Late Latin scenarius "of stage scenes," from Latin scena "scene" (see scene).
late 14c., from Old French divorcer, from divorce (see divorce (n.)).
early 15c., "to deal out or apportion," from Latin distributus, past participle of distribuere "to divide, distribute" (see distribution).

Old English ancleow "ankle," from PIE root *ang-/*ank- "to bend" (see angle (n.)).

1847, "to question," quies, perhaps from Latin qui es? "who are you?," first question in oral exams in Latin in old-time grammar schools.

in early use also engredient, early 15c., "something forming part of a mixture," from Latin ingredientem (nominative ingrediens) "that which enters into" (a compound, recipe, etc.), present participle of ingredi "go in, enter," from in- "in" (see in- (2)) + gradi "to step, go" (see grade (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 渴， 口渴；渴望， 热望

"custodian of a library," 1713; see library + -an.

etymology.story: 1560s, "arrangement for a distribution of prizes by chance," from Italian lotteria, from lotto "lot, portion, share," from same root as Old English hlot (see lot).

Old English clawian "to scratch, claw," from the same root as claw (n.).

"a lyric poem," 1580s, from Middle French lyrique "short poem expressing personal emotion," from Latin lyricus "of or for the lyre," from Greek lyrikos "singing to the lyre," from lyra (see lyre).
Old English clæppan "to throb, beat," common Germanic, echoic (cognate with Old Frisian klapa "to beat," Old Norse klappa, Old High German klaphon, German klappen, Old Saxon klapunga).

morphology.mnemonic: 眼/视 + 与…有关的/形容词

late 14c., "origin, source, beginning; rule of conduct; axiom, basic assumption; elemental aspect of a craft or discipline," from Anglo-French principle, Old French principe "origin, cause, principle," from Latin principium (plural principia) "a beginning, commencement, origin, first part," in plural "foundation, elements," from princeps (see prince).
late 15c., frequentative of Middle English dasen (see daze (v.)).

Old English þeoh, þeh, from Proto-Germanic *theuham (source also of Old Frisian thiach, Old Dutch thio, Dutch dij, Old Norse þjo, Old High German dioh), probably literally "the thick or fat part of the leg," from PIE *teuk- from root *teue- (2) "to swell" (source also of Lithuanian taukas, Old Church Slavonic tuku, Russian tuku "fat of animals;" Lithuanian tukti "to become fat;" Avestan tuma "fat;" Greek tylos "callus, lump," tymbos "burial mound, grave, tomb;" Old Irish ton "rump;" Latin tumere "to swell," tumulus "raised heap of earth," tumidus "swollen;"tumor "a swelling;" Middle Irish tomm "a small hill," Welsh tom "mound")..

mid-13c., from Old English hlid "lid, cover, opening, gate," from Proto-Germanic *khlithan (source also of Old Norse hlið "gate, gap," Swedish lid "gate," Old French hlid, Middle Dutch lit, Dutch lid, Old High German hlit "lid, cover"), from PIE root *klei- "to lean" (see lean (v.)), with here perhaps the sense of "that which bends over." Meaning "eyelid" is from early 13c.
early 15c., from Old French tedieus, from Late Latin taediosus "wearisome, irksome, tedious," from Latin taedium (see tedium).
"obtain from a specified source," 1972, from source (n.).

1560s, from Middle French participant, from Latin participantem (nominative participans), present participle of participare "to share in, partake of" from particeps "sharing, partaking" (see participation)..

early 15c., from Middle French domestique (14c.) and directly from Latin domesticus "belonging to the household," from domus "house," from PIE *dom-o- "house," from root *dem- "house, household" (source also of Sanskrit damah "house;" Avestan demana- "house;" Greek domos "house," despotes "master, lord;" Latin dominus "master of a household;" Old Church Slavonic domu, Russian dom "house;" Lithuanian dimstis "enclosed court, property;" Old Norse topt "homestead").

etymology.story: early 14c., from Old French compiler "compile, collect" (13c.), from Latin compilare "to plunder, rob," probably originally "bundle together, heap up;" hence "to pack up and carry off," from com- "together" (see com-) + pilare "to compress, ram down."

mid-14c., "firmness of resolve, mental equilibrium" (of persons), from Old French stablete, establete "firmness, solidity, stability; durability, constancy" (Modern French stabilité), from Latin stabilitatem (nominative stabilitas) "a standing fast, firmness," figuratively "security, steadfastness," from stabilis "steadfast, firm" (see stable (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 张力，拉力；紧张，不安；电压

morphology.mnemonic: 持有/保持 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1530s, "a stretched condition," from Middle French tension (16c.) or directly from Latin tensionem (nominative tensio) "a stretching" (in Medieval Latin "a struggle, contest"), noun of state from tensus, past participle of tendere "to stretch," from PIE root *ten- "stretch" (see tenet).
13c., from Old French esches "chessmen," plural of eschec "game of chess, chessboard; checkmate" (see check (n.1)), from the key move of the game.

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 弯曲 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., reflexion, in reference to surfaces throwing back light or heat, from Late Latin reflexionem (nominative reflexio) "a reflection," literally "a bending back," noun of action from past participle stem of Latin reflectere "to bend back, bend backwards, turn away," from re- "back" (see re-) + flectere "to bend" (see flexible).

meanings.definitionCn: （大学）校园；大学，大学生活；校园内的草地

etymology.story: "college grounds," 1774, from Latin campus "a field," probably properly "an expanse surrounded" (by woods, higher ground, etc.), from PIE *kampos "a corner, cove," from root *kamp- "to bend" (source also of Lithuanian kampus "corner," Polish kępa "cluster of trees or brush").
1650s, from Late Latin ultimatus, past participle of ultimare "to be final, come to an end," from Latin ultimus (fem.
early 15c., "follow up, pursue" (some course or action), from Latin prosecutus, past participle of prosequi "follow after, accompany; chase, pursue; attack, assail, abuse" (see pursue).

meanings.definitionCn: 用言辞的,用文字的;口头的;动词的

morphology.mnemonic: 词 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: early 15c., "dealing with words" (especially in contrast to things or realities), from Old French verbal (14c.) and directly from Late Latin verbalis "consisting of words, relating to verbs," from Latin verbum "word" (see verb).
1753, from harass + -ment..

1610s, "territory under the jurisdiction of a lord or officer," from French district (16c.), from Medieval Latin districtus "restraining of offenders, jurisdiction," then under the feudal system "area of jurisdiction," noun use of past participle of Latin distringere "hinder, detain" (see distress).

morphology.mnemonic: 过度/超越 + wholly, without limit

etymology.story: "everywhere," Old English ofer eall, from ofer "over" (see over) + eall (see all).

meanings.definitionCn: 假装；假定，设想；承担；呈现，采取

etymology.story: early 15c., assumpten "to receive up into heaven" (especially of the Virgin Mary), also assumen "to arrogate," from Latin assumere, adsumere "to take up, take to oneself, take besides, obtain in addition," from ad- "to, up" (see ad-) + sumere "to take," from sub "under" (see sub-) + emere "to take" (see exempt (adj.)).
mid-14c., from Old French destinée (12c.) "purpose, intent, fate, destiny; that which is destined," noun use of fem.

meanings.definitionCn: 记忆,记忆力;回忆;存储(器)

morphology.mnemonic: memory记忆 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: mid-13c., "recollection (of someone or something); awareness, consciousness," also "fame, renown, reputation," from Anglo-French memorie (Old French memoire, 11c., "mind, memory, remembrance; memorial, record") and directly from Latin memoria "memory, remembrance, faculty of remembering," noun of quality from memor "mindful, remembering," from PIE root *(s)mer- (1) "to remember" (Sanskrit smarati "remembers," Avestan mimara "mindful;" Greek merimna "care, thought," mermeros "causing anxiety, mischievous, baneful;" Serbo-Croatian mariti "to care for;" Welsh marth "sadness, anxiety;" Old Norse Mimir, name of the giant who guards the Well of Wisdom; Old English gemimor "known," murnan "mourn, remember sorrowfully;" Dutch mijmeren "to ponder").
1550s, from Medieval Latin conceptum "draft, abstract," in classical Latin "(a thing) conceived," from concep-, past participle stem of concipere "to take in" (see conceive).

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 站立/设置 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: 1590s, from Middle French désastre (1560s), from Italian disastro "ill-starred," from dis-, here merely pejorative (see dis-) + astro "star, planet," from Latin astrum, from Greek astron (see star (n.)).

late 13c., anhaunsen "to raise, make higher," from Anglo-French enhauncer, probably from Old French enhaucier "make greater, make higher or louder; fatten, foster; raise in esteem," from Vulgar Latin *inaltiare, from Late Latin inaltare "raise, exalt,"  from altare "make high," from altus "high" (see old).

mid-14c., of uncertain origin, probably from Old French paele, paelle "cooking or frying pan, warming pan;" also a liquid measure, from Latin patella "small pan, little dish, platter," diminutive of patina "broad shallow pan, stewpan" (see pan (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 大会,会议;惯例,常规,习俗;公约,协定

etymology.story: early 15c., "a formal agreement," also "a formal meeting or convention" (of rulers, etc.), also "a secret agreement," from Middle French convention and directly from Latin conventionem (nominative conventio) "meeting, assembly, covenant," noun of action from past participle stem of convenire (see convene)..

root.meaning: device that breaks an electrical circuit

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + device that breaks an electrical circuit + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: late 15c., from refuse + -al (2)..
nonmetallic element, 1817, coined by British chemist Thomas Thomson from silica (silicon dioxide), from which it was isolated.

meanings.definitionCn: 重力， 引力；庄重； 严重

etymology.story: 1550s, from Middle French récipient (16c.) and directly from Latin recipientem (nominative recipiens), present participle of recipere (see receive).
late 15c., "public welfare, general good," from common (adj.) + wealth (n.); meaning "the state" is attested from 1510s; applied specifically to the government of England in the period 1649-1660..

meanings.definitionCn: 工作室；[广播][电视] 演播室；画室；电影制片厂

etymology.story: 1819, "work-room of a sculptor or painter," usually one with windows to admit light from the sky, from Italian studio "room for study," from Latin studium (see study (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: swallow hastily + to parry, to fend off

meanings.definitionCn: 卷心菜，甘蓝菜，洋白菜；（俚)脑袋；（非正式、侮辱）植物人（常用于英式英语）；（俚）钱，尤指纸币（常用于美式俚语）

etymology.story: mid-15c., caboge, from Middle French caboche "head" (in dialect, "cabbage"), from Old French caboce "head," a diminutive from Latin caput "head" (see capitulum).

meanings.definitionCn: 调节，规定；控制；校准；有系统的管理

etymology.story: early 15c., "adjust by rule, control," from Late Latin regulatus, past participle of regulare "to control by rule, direct," from Latin regula "rule" (see regular).

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 填充/补充 + …的场所/与…有关

etymology.story: 1620s, "ceremonious," from complement + -ary.

meanings.definitionCn: 引出，预先提出；作为…的前提
前提；上述各项；房屋连地基
付款，支付；报酬，报答；偿还；惩罚，报应

root.meaning: money given for labor or services, wages

morphology.mnemonic: money given for labor or services, wages + 结果/过程/状态

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French paiement (13c.), from paiier (see pay (v.))..
1580s, from Late Latin controversialis "pertaining to controversy," from Latin controversia (see controversy)..
mid-15c., "covered walk or passageway, narrow and partly open passageway along a wall," from Old French galerie "a long portico" (14c.), from Medieval Latin galeria, of unknown origin.

meanings.definitionCn: 承租人；房客；佃户；居住者

morphology.mnemonic: 持有/保持 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: early 14c., "person who holds lands by title or by lease," from Anglo-French tenaunt (late 13c.), Old French tenant "possessor; feudal tenant" (12c.), noun use of present participle of tenir "to hold," from Latin tenere "hold, keep, grasp" (see tenet).
mid-15c., "small metal goods," from hard (adj.) + ware (n.).
late 12c., wearmth, Proto-Germanic *warmitho- (source also of Middle Low German wermede, Dutch warmte), from *warmo- (see warm (adj.); also see -th (2))..

late 13c., from Old French ostage, hostage "kindness, hospitality; residence, dwelling; rent, tribute; compensation; guarantee, pledge, bail; person given as security or hostage" (11c., Modern French ôtage), which is of uncertain origin.
late 14c., "state of being earlier," from Old French priorite (14c.), from Medieval Latin prioritatem (nominative prioritas) "fact or condition of being prior," from Latin prior (see prior (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 引进; 嵌入; 插入;

etymology.story: "narrow opening into a coast, arm of the sea," 1570s, said by old sources to be originally a Kentish term; a special use of Middle English inlate "passage or opening by which an enclosed place may be entered" (c.
1670s, "having the quality of creating," from create + -ive.

meanings.definitionCn: 公正的，明断的；法庭的；审判上的

etymology.story: late 14c., "of or pertaining to a judge; pertaining to the administration of justice," from Latin iudicalis "of or belonging to a court of justice," from iudicium "judgment, decision of a court of justice," also the court itself, from iudex "a judge" (see judge (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 航天飞机；穿梭；梭子；穿梭班机、公共汽车等
使穿梭般来回移动；短程穿梭般运送

etymology.story: Old English scytel "a dart, arrow," from Proto-Germanic *skutilaz (source also of Old Norse skutill "harpoon"), from PIE *skeud- "to shoot, to chase, to throw, to project" (see shoot (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 地方 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1630s, from verbal used of adjective allocate (mid-15c.

meanings.definitionCn: 最初的,初级的;首要的,主要的,基本的

morphology.mnemonic: 第一/首要 + …的场所/与…有关

etymology.story: 1861, American English, short for primary election (1792, with reference to France; in a U.S.

meanings.definitionCn: 喘息；渴望；气喘吁吁地说出某事
事业,企(事)业单位;事业心,进取心

etymology.story: early 15c., "an undertaking," formerly also enterprize, from Old French enterprise "an undertaking," noun use of fem.

morphology.mnemonic: 独自/太阳 + 与…有关的/像…的

etymology.story: mid-15c., "pertaining to the sun," from Latin solaris "of the sun," from sol "sun" (see sol).

meanings.definitionCn: 消耗；吃完，喝光；(with)使着迷；烧毁

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French consumer "to consume" (12c.) and directly from Latin consumere "to use up, eat, waste," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + sumere "to take," from sub- "under" + emere "to buy, take" (see exempt (adj.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 形成;构成;组织;构造;编制;塑造

morphology.mnemonic: 形状/形态 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "vital force in plants and animals;" early 15c., "act of creating or making," from Old French formacion "formation, fashioning, creation" (12c.) or directly from Latin formationem (nominative formatio) "a forming, shaping," noun of action or condition from past participle stem of formare "to form," from forma "form, shape" (see form (n.)).
"round, vaulted roof," 1650s, from French dome (16c.), from Provençal doma, from Greek doma "house, housetop" (especially a style of roof from the east), related to domos "house" (see domestic).

"combustible cord or tube for lighting an explosive device," also fuze, 1640s, from Italian fuso, literally "spindle" (the ignition device so called for its shape, because the originals were long, thin tubes filled with gunpowder), from Latin fusus "a spindle," which is of uncertain origin.

meanings.definitionCn: 额外费用；奖金；保险费;(商)溢价

etymology.story: 1510s, from French prétexte, from Latin praetextum "a pretext, outward display," noun use of neuter past participle of praetexere "to disguise, cover," literally "weave in front" (for sense, compare pull the wool over (someone's) eyes); from prae- "in front" (see pre-) + texere "to weave," from PIE root *teks- "to weave, to make" (see texture (n.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 保证；担保；使确信；弄清楚

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French asseurer (12c., Modern French assurer) "to reassure, calm, protect, to render sure," from Vulgar Latin *assecurar, from Latin ad- "to" (see ad-) + securus "safe, secure" (see secure (adj.)).

1829, from French solidarité "communion of interests and responsibilities, mutual responsibility," a coinage of the "Encyclopédie" (1765), from solidaire "interdependent, complete, entire," from solide (see solid (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 摔倒；倒塌；滚动；打滚；仓惶地行动
请教，向...咨询，找...商量；查阅，查看

etymology.story: 1520s, from Middle French consulter (16c.), from Latin consultare "consult, take the advice of," frequentative of consulere "to take counsel, meet and consider" (see consultation).

meanings.definitionCn: 亲切的,客气的;宽厚的,仁慈的

morphology.mnemonic: to show favor + 具有…性质的
gaudily alluring + 以…方式/副词化

etymology.story: mid-15c., "entirely, purely," from mere (adj.) + -ly (2).

meanings.definitionCn: 小题大作；忙乱；焦燥；焦急；无事自扰
大惊小怪，大惊小怪的人；小题大作；忙乱

etymology.story: "course of action," late 14c., from mean (n.); sense of "wealth" is first recorded c.

morphology.mnemonic: 开始/初始 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French eternel "eternal," or directly from Late Latin aeternalis, from Latin aeternus "of an age, lasting, enduring, permanent, everlasting, endless," contraction of aeviternus "of great age," from aevum "age" (see eon).

meanings.definitionCn: 赤裸裸地,无遮蔽地;仅仅,勉强,几乎没有

etymology.story: Old English bærlice "openly, clear, public;" see bare (adj.) + -ly (2).

Old English clingan "hold fast, adhere closely; congeal, shrivel" (strong verb, past tense clang, past participle clungen), from Proto-Germanic *klingg- (source also of Danish klynge "to cluster;" Old High German klinga "narrow gorge;" Old Norse klengjask "press onward;" Danish klinke, Dutch klinken "to clench;" German Klinke "latch").

meanings.definitionCn: 讽刺；讽刺文学，讽刺作品

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之前/预先 + 强壮/价值 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: early 15c., "having great power or force," from Latin praevalentem (nominative praevalens) "of superior strength; mighty," present participle of praevalere "to be more able" (see prevail).

1560s, from medical Latin diabetes, from late Greek diabetes "excessive discharge of urine" (so named by Aretaeus the Cappadocian, physician of Alexandria, 2c.), literally "a passer-through, siphon," from diabainein "to pass through," from dia- "through" (see dia-) + bainein "to go" (see come).

meanings.definitionCn: （塑料或金属的）夹子；回纹针；修剪；剪报
剪；剪掉；缩短；给…剪毛（或发）用别针别在某物上，用夹子夹在某物上

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + 强壮/价值 + able

etymology.story: 1570s, "above value, too valuable for exact estimate," from in- (1) "not" + value (v.) "estimate the worth of" + -able.
early 15c., from Latin compactus, past participle of compingere "to fasten together" (see compact (adj.)).

"supply land with water," 1610s, from Latin irrigatus, past participle of irrigare "lead water to, refresh, irrigate, flood," from assimilated form of in- "into, in, on, upon" (see in- (2)) + rigare "to water, to moisten," of uncertain origin.

morphology.mnemonic: 向下/移除/取消 + notice, attention, reputation

etymology.story: 1590s, from Middle French dénoter (14c.), from Latin denotare "denote, mark out," from de- "completely" (see de-) + notare "to mark" (see note (v.)).
mid-13c., blomen, from the noun (see bloom (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 威严；最高权威，王权；雄伟；权威

morphology.mnemonic: 狂热 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1620s, from Medieval Latin necessitatus, past participle of necessitare "to render necessary," from Latin necessitas (see necessity).

meanings.definitionCn: 正统的；传统的；惯常的；东正教的

etymology.story: mid-15c., of opinions, faith, from Late Latin orthodoxus, from Greek orthodoxos "having the right opinion," from orthos "right, true, straight" (see ortho-) + doxa "opinion, praise," from dokein "to seem," from PIE root *dek- "to take, accept" (see decent).

meanings.definitionCn: 结合；组合；联合；[化学] 化合

etymology.story: late 14c., combinacyoun, from Old French combination (14c., Modern French combinaison), from Late Latin combinationem (nominative combinatio) "a joining two by two," noun of action from past participle stem of combinare (see combine (v.))..

early 15c., from Middle French obtenir "acquire, obtain" (14c.), from Latin obtinere "hold, hold fast, take hold of, get possession of, acquire," from ob "to" (though perhaps intensive in this case; see ob-) + tenere "to hold" (see tenet).

morphology.mnemonic: beloved person, admired woman + …的场所/与…有关

etymology.story: late 14c., juelrye "precious ornaments, jewel work," from Old French juelerye, from jouel (see jewel).
1590s, after French homme d'état; see state (n.1) + man (n.).

late 14c., "careful attention to detail," also "desire to know or learn" (originally usually in a bad sense), from Old French curiosete "curiosity, avidity, choosiness" (Modern French curiosité), from Latin curiositatem (nominative curiositas) "desire of knowledge, inquisitiveness," from curiosus (see curious).

meanings.definitionCn: (Amiable)人名；(法)阿米亚布勒；(英)阿米娅布尔

etymology.story: mid-14c., from Old French amiable, from Late Latin amicabilis "friendly," from amicus "friend," related to amare "to love" (see Amy).

meanings.definitionCn: [法] 非法的；违法的；违反规则的

etymology.story: 1630s, from French illégal (14c.) or directly from Medieval Latin illegalis, from assimilated form of in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + Latin legalis (see legal).

late 14c., "pertaining to form or arrangement;" also, in philosophy and theology, "pertaining to the form or essence of a thing," from Old French formal, formel "formal, constituent" (13c.) and directly from Latin formalis, from forma "a form, figure, shape" (see form (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 彩虹；五彩缤纷的排列；幻想
使呈彩虹状；如彩虹般装饰

etymology.story: Old English renboga; see rain (n.) + bow (n.).
also loud-speaker, 1884, from loud (adj.) + speaker..

root.meaning: stand in some relation; have reference or respect

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + stand in some relation; have reference or respect

etymology.story: 1742, back-formation from correlation, or else a verbal use of the noun.
mid-15c., from Middle French ample, from Latin amplus "large, spacious," related to ampla "handle, grip.".
1520s, from genitive of sale (compare craftsman, tradesman) + man (n.)..

meanings.definitionCn: 招待,款待;使娱乐;使欢乐;容纳,接受

morphology.mnemonic: 之间/相互 + 持有/保持

etymology.story: late 15c., "to keep up, maintain, to keep (someone) in a certain frame of mind," from Middle French entretenir, from Old French entretenir "hold together, stick together, support" (12c.), from entre- "among" (from Latin inter; see inter-) + tenir "to hold" (from Latin tenere; see tenet).

meanings.definitionCn: 批评的,评论的;危急,紧要的;临界的

root.meaning: one who judges merits of books, plays, etc.

morphology.mnemonic: one who judges merits of books, plays, etc. + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1580s, "censorious," from critic + -al (1).
"in another place, in other places," c.

meanings.definitionCn: 辐射的；容光焕发的；光芒四射的

morphology.mnemonic: 射线/放射 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: "point or object from which light radiates," 1727; see radiant (adj.).

meanings.definitionCn: 穿过， 渗入；看穿， 洞察

etymology.story: 1520s, from Latin penetratus, past participle of penetrare "to put or get into, enter into," related to penitus "within, inmost," penus "innermost part of a temple, store of food," penates "household gods."

meanings.definitionCn: 最重要的,主要的n.负责人,校长;资本,本金

etymology.story: 1610s, "pattern, model," from Latin specimen "indication, mark, example, sign, evidence; that by which a thing is known, means of knowing," from specere "to look at" (see scope (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 异国的；外来的；异国情调的

etymology.story: 1590s, "belonging to another country," from Middle French exotique (16c.) and directly from Latin exoticus, from Greek exotikos "foreign," literally "from the outside," from exo "outside" (see exo-).

meanings.definitionCn: 救济；减轻，解除；安慰；浮雕

etymology.story: 1880, noun and verb, from Irish Land League ostracism of Capt.

meanings.definitionCn: (to)介绍;传入,引进;导言,导论,绪论

morphology.mnemonic: intra + 引导 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "act of bringing into existence," from Old French introduccion (14c.) and directly from Latin introductionem (nominative introductio) "a leading in," noun of action from past participle stem of introducere "to lead in, bring in; introduce; found, establish; bring forward (as an assertion)," from intro- "inward, to the inside" (see intro-) + ducere "to lead" (see duke (n.)).
1540s, from Middle French revenge, back-formation from revengier (see revenge (v.))..

early 15c., "to sign at the bottom of a document," from Latin subscribere "write, write underneath, sign one's name; register," also figuratively "assent, agree to, approve," from sub "underneath" (see sub-) + scribere "write" (see script (n.)).
also distil, late 14c., from Old French distiller (14c.), from Latin distillare "trickle down in minute drops," from dis- "apart" (see dis-) + stillare "to drip, drop," from stilla "drop."

1846, "medical treatment of disease," from Modern Latin therapia, from Greek therapeia "curing, healing, service done to the sick; a waiting on, service," from therapeuein "to cure, treat medically," literally "attend, do service, take care of" (see therapeutic)..

meanings.definitionCn: 急速上升；嗡嗡声；[摄] 变焦摄影

morphology.mnemonic: one of the same kind + 身份/状态/技能

etymology.story: 1530s, from Middle French collègue (16c.), from Latin collega "partner in office," from com- "with" (see com-) + leg-, stem of legare "to choose" (see legate).
1580s, "art of managing a household," perhaps from French économique (see economic); also see -ics.

morphology.mnemonic: 感激/愉悦 + 状态/品质

etymology.story: mid-15c., "good will," from Middle French gratitude (15c.) or directly from Medieval Latin gratitudinem (nominative gratitudo) "thankfulness," from Latin gratus "thankful, pleasing" (see grace (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 之间/相互 + one who goes with another.

etymology.story: formerly also enterfere, mid-15c., "to strike against," from Middle French enterferir "exchange blows, strike each other," from entre- "between" (see entre-) + ferir "to strike," from Latin ferire "to knock, strike," related to Latin forare "to bore, pierce" (see bore (v.), and compare punch (v.), which has both the senses "to hit" and "to make a hole in").

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 结果/过程/状态

etymology.story: 1640s, "private rooms for the use of one person within a house," from French appartement (16c.), from Italian appartimento, literally "a separated place," from appartere "to separate," from a "to" (see ad-) + parte "side, place," from Latin partem (see part (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 忍受/容忍 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1530s, of authorities, "to allow without interference," from Latin toleratus, past participle of tolerare (see toleration).

meanings.definitionCn: 警报声， 警报器 A

etymology.story:
mid-14c., "sea nymph who by her singing lures sailors to their destruction," from Old French sereine (12c., Modern French sirène) and directly from Latin Siren (Late Latin Sirena), from Greek Seiren ["Odyssey," xii.39 ff.], one of the Seirenes, mythical sisters who enticed sailors to their deaths with their songs, also in Greek "a deceitful woman," perhaps literally "binder, entangler," from seira "cord, rope."

Meaning "device that makes a warning sound" (on an ambulance, etc.) first recorded 1879, in reference to steamboats, perhaps from similar use of the French word.

morphology.mnemonic: 不足/在…之下 + one's occupation, branch of business

etymology.story: 1721, "to mark underneath or below with a line," from under + line (v.).
"take (something) ill; be in some degree angry or provoked at," c.

mid-14c., from Old French compassion "sympathy, pity" (12c.), from Late Latin compassionem (nominative compassio) "sympathy," noun of state from past participle stem of compati "to feel pity," from com- "together" (see com-) + pati "to suffer" (see passion).

meanings.definitionCn: 模糊不清的；引起歧义的

etymology.story: 1520s, from Latin ambiguus "having double meaning, shifting, changeable, doubtful," adjective derived from ambigere "to dispute about," literally "to wander," from ambi- "about" (see ambi-) + agere "drive, lead, act" (see act (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 结合；装订；有约束力；过紧
捆绑；困境；讨厌的事情；植物的藤蔓

etymology.story: Old English deaw, from Proto-Germanic *dawwaz (source also of Old Saxon dau, Old Frisian daw, Middle Dutch dau, Old High German tau, German Tau, Old Norse dögg "dew"), from PIE root *dheu- (2) "to flow" (source also of Sanskrit dhavate "flows, runs")..
1812 in modern sense, from rail (n.1) + way (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 使成立方形；使自乘二次；量…的体积
脆的,易碎的;简明扼要的；（空气）清爽的
侧面；轮廓；外形；剖面；简况
冲击， 碰撞；效果； 影响
温和的；轻微的；淡味的；文雅的；不含有害物质的的

etymology.story: Old English milde "gentle, merciful," from Proto-Germanic *milthjaz- (source also of Old Norse mildr, Old Saxon mildi, Old Frisian milde, Middle Dutch milde, Dutch mild, Old High German milti, German milde "mild," Gothic mildiþa "kindness"), from PIE *meldh-, from root *mel- "soft," with derivatives referring to soft or softened materials (source also of Greek malthon "weakling," myle "mill;" Latin molere "to grind;" Old Irish meldach "tender;" Sanskrit mrdh "to neglect," also "to be moist").

meanings.definitionCn: 磨粉机； 磨坊；作坊； 工厂

etymology.story: 1610s, from Latin census "the enrollment of the names and property assessments of all Roman citizens," originally past participle of censere "to assess" (see censor (n.)).

early 15c., in Scottish, from Middle French domaine "domain, estate," from Old French demaine "lord's estate," from Latin dominium "property, dominion," from dominus "lord, master, owner," from domus "house" (see domestic).
1660s, via French attitude (17c.), from Italian attitudine "disposition, posture," also "aptness, promptitude," from Late Latin aptitudinem (nominative aptitudo; see aptitude).

morphology.mnemonic: in + 流动 + ance + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: "powerful, having influence," 1650s, from Medieval Latin influentialis, from influentia (see influence (n.)).

early 14c., from Anglo-Latin biblia, Old French bible (13c.) "the Bible," also any large book generally, from Medieval and Late Latin biblia "the Bible" (neuter plural interpreted as feminine singular), from phrase biblia sacra "holy books," a translation of Greek ta biblia to hagia "the holy books." The Latin word is from Greek biblion "paper, scroll," the ordinary word for "book," originally a diminutive of byblos "Egyptian papyrus."

The Greek word perhaps is from Byblos, the name of the Phoenician port from which Egyptian papyrus was exported to Greece (modern Jebeil, in Lebanon; compare parchment).

meanings.definitionCn: 透明的，透光的；易理解的；明显的

morphology.mnemonic: 穿越/转变 + 平等 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: early 15c., from Medieval Latin transparentem (nominative transparens), present participle of transparere "show light through," from Latin trans- "through" (see trans-) + parere "come in sight, appear" (see appear).

morphology.mnemonic: 日/天 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: mid-14c., "book of church services," from Anglo-French jurnal, from Old French jornel, "a day; time; a day's travel or work" (12c., Modern French journal), properly "that which takes place daily," noun use of adjective meaning "daily, of the day," from Late Latin diurnalis "daily" (see diurnal).

early 14c., "to cover (a street) with stones or other material," from Old French paver "to pave" (12c.), perhaps a back-formation from Old French pavement or else from Vulgar Latin *pavare, from Latin pavire "to beat, ram, tread down," from PIE *pau- "to cut, strike, stamp" (source also of Latin putare "to prune;" Greek paiein "to strike;" Lithuanian piauju "to cut," piuklas "saw").

meanings.definitionCn: 作品,作文,乐曲;作曲;结构,组成,成分

suffix.meaning: thing or animal spoken about before.

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 放置 + thing or animal spoken about before. + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "action of combining," also "manner in which a thing is composed," from Old French composicion (13c., Modern French composition) "composition, make-up, literary work, agreement, settlement," from Latin compositionem (nominative compositio) "a putting together, connecting, arranging," noun of action from past participle stem of componere (see composite).

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + quire + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "act of obtaining," from Old French acquisicion (13c.) or directly from Latin acquisitionem (nominative acquisitio), noun of action from past participle stem of acquirere "get in addition, accumulate," from ad- "extra" (see ad-) + quaerere "to seek to obtain" (see query (v.)).
1610s, from Italian vulcano "burning mountain," from Latin Vulcanus "Vulcan," Roman god of fire, also "fire, flames, volcano" (see Vulcan).
mid-15c., from so (adv.) + past participle of call (v.).
"time limit," 1920, American English newspaper jargon, from dead (adj.) + line (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 严格的；僵硬的，死板的；坚硬的；精确的

etymology.story: early 15c., from Latin rigidus "hard, stiff, rough, severe," from rigere "be stiff," from PIE *reig- "stretch (tight), bind tightly, make fast" (source also of Old Irish riag "torture," Middle High German ric "band, string").
1550s, "pleasing to the mind," also "full of gratitude, disposed to repay favors bestowed," from obsolete adjective grate "agreeable, pleasant," from Latin gratus "pleasing" (see grace (n.)).

"important arrival," 1742, an extended sense of  Advent "season before Christmas" (Old English), from Latin adventus "a coming, approach, arrival," in Church Latin "the coming of the Savior," from past participle stem of advenire "arrive, come to," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + venire "to come" (see venue).

meanings.definitionCn: 中世纪的,中古(时代)的,老式的,原始的

etymology.story: 1827, "pertaining to or suggestive of the Middle Ages," coined in English from Latin medium "the middle" (see medium (n.)) + aevum "age" (see eon)..

morphology.mnemonic: 工作 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1922, "pertaining to operation," from operation + -al (1).

meanings.definitionCn: 卷入,陷入,连累;包含,含有,涉及

etymology.story: late 14c., "envelop, surround; make cloudy or obscure," from Old French involver and directly from Latin involvere "envelop, surround, overwhelm," literally "roll into," from in- "in" (see in- (2)) + volvere "to roll" (see volvox).

meanings.definitionCn: 油轮；运油飞机；油槽车；坦克手

etymology.story: "ship for carrying oil or other liquid cargo," 1900, from tank (n.)..
1640s, "tangled," from past participle adjective from complicate.

meanings.definitionCn: [数] 极大，最大限度；最大量
最高的；最多的；最大极限的

morphology.mnemonic: greatest premise, greatest among propositions + ium

meanings.definitionCn: 宽容的；容忍的；有耐药力的

morphology.mnemonic: 忍受/容忍 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: 1784, "free from bigotry or severity in judging others," from French tolérant (16c.), and directly from Latin tolerantem (nominative tolerans), present participle of tolerare "to bear, endure, tolerate" (see toleration).

mid-15c., from Latin fragrantem (nominative fragrans) "sweet-smelling," present participle of fragrare "smell strongly, emit (a sweet) odor," from Proto-Italic *fragro-, from PIE root *bhrag- "to smell" (source also of Old Irish broimm "break wind," Middle High German bræhen "to smell," Middle Dutch bracke, Old High German braccho "hound, setter;" see brach).

1570s, "form of speech of a region or group," from Middle French dialecte, from Latin dialectus "local language, way of speaking, conversation," from Greek dialektos "talk, conversation, speech;" also "the language of a country, dialect," from dialegesthai "converse with each other," from dia- "across, between" (see dia-) + legein "speak" (see lecture (n.))..

"close-fitting garment covering the foot and lower leg," 1580s, from stock "leg covering, stocking" (late 15c.), from Old English stocu "sleeve," related to Old English stocc "trunk, log" (see stock (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 紧急情况,突然事件,非常时刻

etymology.story: "unforeseen occurrence requiring immediate attention," 1630s, from Latin emergens, present participle of emergere "to rise out or up" (see emerge).

meanings.definitionCn: 暗淡的，昏暗的；模糊的，看不清的；悲观的，怀疑的
使暗淡，使失去光泽；使变模糊

etymology.story: 1804, from French paralyser (16c.), from Old French paralisie "paralysis," from Latin paralysis (see paralysis).

meanings.definitionCn: 下沉，下降；倾斜；浸渍，蘸湿

etymology.story: "stupid person, eccentric person," 1920s slang, perhaps a back-formation from dippy.

late 14c., "ability, opportunity, means, resources," from Old French faculte "skill, accomplishment, learning" (14c., Modern French faculté) and directly from Latin facultatem (nominative facultas) "power, ability, capability, opportunity; sufficient number, abundance, wealth," from *facli-tat-s, from facilis (see facile).

meanings.definitionCn: 活动停止期间；临时解雇；操作停止；失业期

etymology.story: also lay-off, lay off; 1889, "rest, relaxation, respite;" from the verbal phrase; see lay (v.) + off (adv.).
1570s, "marked by deliberation," from Latin consideratus, past participle of considerare (see consider).

meanings.definitionCn: 为…提供线索；为…提供情况
法律的,法定的;合法的,正当的
支架；括号；墙上凸出的托架
括在一起；把…归入同一类；排除
半径，半径范围；[解剖] 桡骨；辐射光线；有效航程

etymology.story: 1590s, "cross-shaft," from Latin radius "staff, stake, rod; spoke of a wheel; ray of light, beam of light; radius of a circle," of unknown origin.
Old English heahweg "main road from one town to another;" see high (adj.) in sense of "main" + way (n.).

morphology.mnemonic: 标记 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: late 14c., a re-Latinizing of earlier ampayre, apeyre "make worse, cause to deteriorate" (c.

meanings.definitionCn: 排出；耗尽；使精疲力尽；彻底探讨

etymology.story: "waste gas," 1848, originally from steam engines, from exhaust (v.).

late 14c., "capable of being dissolved," from Old French soluble "expungable, eradicable" (13c.), from Late Latin solubilis "that may be loosened or dissolved," from stem of Latin solvere "loosen, dissolve" (see solve).
Old English þær æfter; see there + after.

meanings.definitionCn: 葡萄；葡萄酒；葡萄树；葡萄色

etymology.story: mid-13c., "a grape, a berry of the vine," also collective singular, from Old French grape "bunch of grapes, grape" (12c.), probably a back-formation from graper "steal; grasp; catch with a hook; pick (grapes)," from a Frankish or other Germanic word, from Proto-Germanic *krappon "hook," from a group of Germanic words meaning "bent, crooked, hooked" (cognates: Middle Dutch crappe, Old High German krapfo "hook;" also see cramp (n.2)).

meanings.definitionCn: 用带捆绑；用皮条抽打；约束

etymology.story: mid-15c., "the proving of guilt," from Late Latin convictionem (nominative convictio) "proof, refutation," noun of action from past participle stem of convincere (see convince).

meanings.definitionCn: 稻草；吸管；一文不值的东西

etymology.story: Old English streaw (rare) "stems or stalks of certain species of grains," apparently literally "that which is scattered or strewn," related to streowian (see strew), from Proto-Germanic *strawam "that which is scattered" (source also of Old Norse stra, Danish straa, Swedish strå, Old Saxon stro, Old Frisian stre, Old Dutch, Old High German stro, Dutch stroo, German Stroh "straw"), from PIE *stere- "to spread" (see structure (n.)).
1878, shortening of graphic formula (see graphic).

meanings.definitionCn: 接受，接收，验收，接纳；承认，认可

etymology.story: 1570s, from Middle French acceptance, from accepter (see accept).

root.meaning: stand in some relation; have reference or respect

morphology.mnemonic: stand in some relation; have reference or respect + 有…倾向的 + ity

etymology.story: 1834, "fact or condition of being relative" (apparently coined by Coleridge, of God, in "Notes on Waterland's Vindication of Christ's Divinity"), from relative (adj.) + -ity.

late 12c., "Garden of Eden," from Old French paradis "paradise, Garden of Eden" (11c.), from Late Latin paradisus, from Greek paradeisos "park, paradise, Garden of Eden," from an Iranian source similar to Avestan pairidaeza "enclosure, park" (Modern Persian and Arabic firdaus "garden, paradise"), compound of pairi- "around" + diz "to make, form (a wall)."

The first element is cognate with Greek peri- "around, about" (see per), the second is from PIE root *dheigh- "to form, build" (see dough).

meanings.definitionCn: 分散;使分心;打扰;使心情烦乱

etymology.story: mid-14c., "to draw asunder or apart, to turn aside" (literal and figurative), from Latin distractus, past participle of distrahere "draw in different directions," from dis- "away" (see dis-) + trahere "to draw" (see tract (n.1)).

mid-13c., from Old Norse lan, related to lja "to lend," from Proto-Germanic *laikhwniz (source also of Old Frisian len "thing lent," Middle Dutch lene, Dutch leen "loan, fief," Old High German lehan, German Lehn "fief, feudal tenure"), originally "to let have, to leave (to someone)," from PIE *leikw- "to leave" (see relinquish).

meanings.definitionCn: [计] 虚拟的；实质上的，事实上的（但未在名义上或正式获承认）

etymology.story: late 14c., "influencing by physical virtues or capabilities, effective with respect to inherent natural qualities," from Medieval Latin virtualis, from Latin virtus "excellence, potency, efficacy," literally "manliness, manhood" (see virtue).

meanings.definitionCn: 值得的；有价值的；配得上的，相称的；可尊敬的；应…的

etymology.story: mid-13c., "having merit," from worth (n.) + -y (2).

root.meaning: count upon (to do something), trust or rely on

morphology.mnemonic: count upon (to do something), trust or rely on + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1530s, from Middle French expectation (14c.) or directly from Latin expectationem/exspectationem (nominative expectatio/exspectatio) "anticipation, an awaiting," noun of action from past participle stem of expectare/exspectare (see expect).

meanings.definitionCn: 书名； 标题；头衔， 称号
薄雾；视线模糊不清；模糊不清之物

etymology.story: Old English mistian "to become misty, to be or grow misty;" see mist (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 期间；持久， 持续时间

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French duration, from Medieval Latin durationem (nominative duratio), noun of action from past participle stem of Latin durare "harden" (see endure).

meanings.definitionCn: 适用于种植园或热带、亚热带国家的

morphology.mnemonic: to bury + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: mid-15c., "action of planting," from Middle French plantation, from Latin plantationem (nominative plantatio) "a planting," noun of action from past participle stem of plantare "to plant" (see plant).

meanings.definitionCn: （乘渡船）渡过；用渡船运送；空运

etymology.story: early 15c., "a passage over a river," from the verb or from Old Norse ferju-, in compounds, "passage across water," ultimately from the same Germanic root as ferry (v.).
1640s, probably from a Scandinavian source (compare Old Norse dusa "to doze," Danish døse "to make dull," Swedish dialectal dusa "to sleep"); related to Old English dysig "foolish" (see dizzy).

root.meaning: range of something, place or scene of activity

morphology.mnemonic: hemi + range of something, place or scene of activity

etymology.story: late 14c., hemysperie, in reference to the celestial sphere, from Late Latin hemisphaerium, from Greek hemisphairion, from hemi- "half" (see hemi-) + sphaira "sphere" (see sphere).

meanings.definitionCn: 游荡；游手好闲；虚度光阴

etymology.story: 1835, American English, back-formation from loafer (1830).

meanings.definitionCn: 原子能的；[细胞] 细胞核的；中心的；原子核的

etymology.story: 1841, "of or like the nucleus of a cell," from nucleus + -ar, probably by influence of French nucléaire.

meanings.definitionCn: 月亮的，月球的；阴历的；银的；微亮的

morphology.mnemonic: moon月亮 + 与…有关的/像…的

etymology.story: "crescent-shaped," early 15c.; "pertaining to the moon," 1620s, from Old French lunaire (15c.), from Latin lunaris "of the moon," from luna "moon" (with capital L- "moon goddess"); see Luna..

late 14c., "poetry; a poem; ancient literature; poetical works, fables, or tales," from Old French poetrie (13c.), and perhaps directly from Medieval Latin poetria (c.650), from Latin poeta (see poet).

late 14c., from Old French arrogant (14c.), from Latin arrogantem (nominative arrogans) "assuming, overbearing, insolent," present participle of arrogare "to claim for oneself, assume" (see arrogance).

meanings.definitionCn: 放松，休息；松懈，松弛；变从容；休养

etymology.story: late 14c., "to make (something) less compact or dense," from Old French relaschier "set free; soften; reduce" (14c.), from Latin relaxare "relax, loosen, open, stretch out, widen again; make loose," from re- "back" (see re-) + laxare "loosen," from laxus "loose" (see lax).

meanings.definitionCn: [电] 继电器；接替，接替人员；驿马

etymology.story: Old English preost probably shortened from the older Germanic form represented by Old Saxon and Old High German prestar, Old Frisian prestere, all from Vulgar Latin *prester "priest," from Late Latin presbyter "presbyter, elder," from Greek presbyteros (see Presbyterian).
early 15c., from Latin permissionem (nominative permissio), noun of action from past participle stem of permittere (see permit (v.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 盛大的,豪华的;重大的,主要的

etymology.story: 1650s, from Latin permeatus, past participle of permeare "to pass through" (see permeable).

meanings.definitionCn: 安排，计划；编制目录；将……列入计划表

etymology.story: late 14c., "expound the meaning of, render clear or explicit," from Old French interpreter "explain; translate" (13c.) and directly from Latin interpretari "explain, expound, understand," from interpres "agent, translator," from inter- "between" (see inter-) + second element of uncertain origin, perhaps related to Sanskrit prath- "to spread abroad," PIE *per- (5) "to traffic in, sell" (see pornography).

1580s, "the whole world, cosmos, the totality of existing things," from Old French univers (12c.), from Latin universum "all things, everybody, all people, the whole world," noun use of neuter of adjective universus "all together, all in one, whole, entire, relating to all," literally "turned into one," from unus "one" (see one) + versus, past participle of vertere "to turn" (see versus)..

meanings.definitionCn: 毛毯，毯子；毯状物，覆盖层
总括的，全体的；没有限制的

etymology.story: in early use also graunt, early 13c., "to allow, permit (something); consent to (a prayer, request, etc.)," from Old French graanter, variant of creanter "assure, promise, guarantee, swear; confirm, authorize, approve (of)," from Latin credentem (nominative credens), present participle of credere "to believe, to trust" (see credo).

1620s, "reckon as an abatement or deduction," from Old French desconter (13c., Modern French décompter), from Medieval Latin discomputare, from dis- (see dis-) + computare "to count" (see count (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 变换,转换；改变（信仰等）；兑换（钱）
滋味；风味；食欲；开胃小菜；含义；调味品
盼望;期待;享受; 品味；喜爱；给…加佐料
分,分割;部门,科,处;除法;分界线

morphology.mnemonic: watershed, separation between river valleys + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French division, from Latin divisionem (nominative divisio), from divid-, stem of dividere (see divide).

root.meaning: a nation's collective, organized sea power

morphology.mnemonic: a nation's collective, organized sea power + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1530s, from Middle French navigation (14c.) or directly from Latin navigationem (nominative navigatio) "a sailing, navigation, voyage," noun of action from past participle stem of navigare "to sail, sail over, go by sea, steer a ship," from navis "ship" (see naval) + root of agere "to drive" (see act (n.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 变陈旧；撒尿；变得不新鲜
（植物的）茎，秆；（支持叶子、果实和花的）梗，柄；追踪；高视阔步

etymology.story: "stem of a plant," early 14c., probably a diminutive (with -k suffix) of stale "one of the uprights of a ladder, handle, stalk," from Old English stalu "wooden part" (of a tool or instrument), from Proto-Germanic *stalla- (source also of Old English steala "stalk, support," steall "place"), from PIE *stol-no-, suffixed form of *stol-, variant of root *stel- "to put, stand" (see stall (n.1)).

1730, from Spanish cigarro (source also of French cigare), probably from Maya sicar "to smoke rolled tobacco leaves," from si'c "tobacco;" or from or influenced by Spanish cigarra "grasshopper, cicada" (on resemblance of shape), from Vulgar Latin *cicala (source also of French cigale, Italian cigala).

meanings.definitionCn: 沥青；音高；程度；树脂；倾斜；投掷；球场

etymology.story: mid-14c., "where;" early 15c.

mid-14c., "action, deeds," from Anglo-French fet, from Old French fait "action, deed, achievement" (12c.), from Latin factum "thing done," a noun based on the past participle of facere "make, do" (see factitious, and compare fact).

morphology.mnemonic: determine bearings + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French oriental "eastern, from the east" (12c.) and directly from Latin orientalis "of the east," from orientem (see Orient (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 奔流；倾注；迸发；连续不断

meanings.definitionCn: 略读；撇去…的浮物；从…表面飞掠而过；去除；（为逃税而）隐瞒（部分收入）

meanings.definitionCn: (尤指不好的事)一再发生；重现

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 堆积/积累 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1520s, from Latin accumulatus, past participle of accumulare "to heap up" (see accumulation).

mid-13c., "sinfulness," from Old French crimne (12c., Modern French crime), from Latin crimen (genitive criminis) "charge, indictment, accusation; crime, fault, offense," perhaps from cernere "to decide, to sift" (see crisis).

early 14c., leisir, "opportunity to do something," also "free time, time at one's disposal," from Old French leisir, loisir "capacity, ability, freedom (to do something); permission; spare time; free will; idleness, inactivity," noun use of infinitive leisir "be permitted," from Latin licere "to be allowed" (see licence (n.)).

early 15c., "to flood, overflow," from Anglo-French surounder, Middle French soronder "to overflow, abound; surpass, dominate," from Late Latin superundare "overflow," from Latin super "over" (see super-) + undare "to flow in waves," from unda "wave" (see water (n.1); and compare abound).

"a drama sung" [Klein], 1640s, from Italian opera, literally "a work, labor, composition," from Latin opera "work, effort" (Latin plural regarded as feminine singular), secondary (abstract) noun from operari "to work," from opus (genitive operis) "a work" (see opus).

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之前/预先 + sume + able + 具有…特征/名词化

late 14c., ele, "lateral division of a church (usually separated by a row of pillars), from Old French ele "wing (of a bird or an army), side of a ship" (12c., Modern French aile), from Latin ala, related to axilla "wing, upper arm, armpit; wing of an army," from PIE *aks- "axis" (see axis), via a suffixed form *aks-la-.
early 15c., "income from property or possessions," from Middle French revenue, in Old French, "a return," noun use of fem.
"long, narrow, flat piece," mid-15c., "narrow piece of cloth," probably related to or from Middle Low German strippe "strap, thong," and from the same source as stripe (n.1).

"seed, grain; particle, drop; berry; grain as a unit of weight," from Latin granum "seed, a grain, small kernel," from PIE root *gre-no- "grain" (see corn (n.1)).

late 14c., "act of dwelling; dwelling place," from Old French residence, from Medieval Latin residentia, from Latin residentem (nominative residens) "residing, dwelling," present participle of residere "reside" (see reside).

"to endure," Old English brucan "use, enjoy, possess; eat; cohabit with," from Proto-Germanic *bruk- "to make use of, enjoy" (source also of Old Saxon brukan, Old Frisian bruka, Old High German bruhhan, German brauchen "to use," Gothic brukjan), from PIE root *bhrug- "to make use of, have enjoyment of" (source also of Latin fructus).

mid-14c., "authoritative," from Old French autentique (13c., Modern French authentique) "authentic; canonical," and directly from Medieval Latin authenticus, from Greek authentikos "original, genuine, principal," from authentes "one acting on one's own authority," from autos "self" (see auto-) + hentes "doer, being," from PIE *sene- "to accomplish, achieve." Sense of "entitled to acceptance as factual" is first recorded mid-14c.

Old English breost "breast, bosom; mind, thought, disposition," from Proto-Germanic *breustam "breast" (source also of Old Saxon briost, Old Frisian briast, Old Norse brjost, Dutch borst, German brust, Gothic brusts), perhaps literally "swelling" and from PIE root *bhreus- "to swell, sprout" (source also of Middle Irish bruasach "having a broad, strong chest," Old Irish bruinne "breast").

late 12c., "God's forgiveness of his creatures' offenses," from Old French mercit, merci (9c.) "reward, gift; kindness, grace, pity," from Latin mercedem (nominative merces) "reward, wages, pay hire" (in Vulgar Latin "favor, pity"), from merx (genitive mercis) "wares, merchandise" (see market (n.)).
mid-15c., metathesis of crulle (c.

early 14c., "to train or instruct in some specific subject," from Old French informer, enformer "instruct, teach" (13c.) and directly from Latin informare "to shape, give form to, delineate," figuratively "train, instruct, educate," from in- "into" (see in- (2)) + formare "to form, shape," from forma "form" (see form (n.)).

"act of setting out on a journey, business, etc." 1759, from out + set (v.).

late 14c., "breadth," from Old French latitude (13c.) and directly from Latin latitudo "breadth, width, extent, size," from latus "wide, broad, extensive," from Old Latin stlatus, from PIE *stleto-, suffixed form of root *stele- "to spread, to extend" (source also of Old Church Slavonic steljo "to spread out," Armenian lain "broad").

1520s, "outbreak of disorder, revolt, commotion," used by Tindale and later Coverdale as a loan-translation of German Aufruhr or Dutch oproer "tumult, riot," literally "a stirring up," in German and Dutch bibles (as in Acts xxi:38).

etymology.story: Old English buttorfleoge, evidently butter (n.) + fly (n.), but of obscure signification.

meanings.definitionCn: 良性的；和蔼的，亲切的；吉利的

etymology.story: early 14c., from Old French benigne (12c., "kind, benign, merciful, gracious;" Modern French bénin, fem.
