etymology.story: late 14c., in letters patent, literally "open letter," from Old French patente, from Latin patentum (nominative patens) "open, lying open," present participle of patere "lie open, be open," from PIE *pete- "to spread" (see pace (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 确定 + 使…化/造成 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: early 15c., "action of certifying," from French certificat, from Medieval Latin certificatum "thing certified," noun use of neuter past participle of certificare (see certify).

meanings.definitionCn: 混乱；食堂，伙食团；困境；脏乱的东西
酸的；发酵的；刺耳的；酸臭的；讨厌的

etymology.story: late 15c., "destroy, eradicate," from Latin deletus, past participle of delere "destroy, blot out, efface," from delevi, originally perfective tense of delinere "to daub, erase by smudging" (as of the wax on a writing table), from de- "from, away" (see de-) + linere "to smear, wipe" (see lime (n.1)).
early 15c., "a special quality or thing;" mid-15c.

meanings.definitionCn: 改变,变换;变压;转化;改造,改造

morphology.mnemonic: 穿越/转变 + 形状/形态

etymology.story: mid-14c., "change the form of" (transitive), from Old French transformer (14c.), from Latin transformare "change in shape, metamorphose," from trans- "across" (see trans-) + formare "to form" (see form (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: lead + er + 身份/状态/技能

etymology.story: 1821, "position of a leader, command," from leader + -ship.

meanings.definitionCn: 大小；量级；[地震] 震级；重要；光度

etymology.story: 1590s, "natural, not acquired," from Latin genuinus "native, natural, innate," from root of gignere "beget" (see genus), perhaps influenced in form by contrasting adulterinus "spurious." [Alternative etymology is from Latin genu "knee," from a supposed ancient custom of a father acknowledging paternity of a newborn by placing it on his knee.] Meaning "really proceeding from its reputed source" is from 1660s.

meanings.definitionCn: 摇动(液体)；使焦虑不安；困扰；鼓动

etymology.story: 1580s, "to disturb," from Latin agitatus, past participle of agitare "to put in constant motion, drive onward, impel," frequentative of agere "to move, drive" (see act (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 免疫的,有免疫力的;有受影响的;豁免的

etymology.story: mid-15c., "free, exempt" (from taxes, tithes, sin, etc.), from Latin immunis "exempt from public service, untaxed; unburdened, not tributary," from assimilated form of in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + munis "performing services" (compare municipal), from PIE *moi-n-es-, suffixed form of root *mei- (1) "to change" (see mutable).

1748, "pamphlet; short written work stitched together," from French brochure "a stitched work," from brocher "to stitch" (sheets together), from Old French brochier "to prick, jab, pierce," from broche "pointed tool, awl" (see broach (n.))..
1510s, "an interpreter," 1530s in the sense of "person who speaks for another or others." Irregular formation from spoke, past tense of speak (actually a back-formation from spoken) + man (n.).

morphology.mnemonic: equi + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., a term in astrology (from French équation, 14c.); general sense of "action of making equal" is from 1650s, from Latin aequationem (nominative aequatio) "an equal distribution, a sharing in common," noun of state from past participle stem of aequare (see equal (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 感人的；令人钦佩的；给人以深刻印象的

morphology.mnemonic: in + device for pressing cloth + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: 1590s, "capable of being easily impressed" (a sense now rare or obsolete), from impress (v.1) + -ive.
early 13c., "hind part of a ship; steering gear of a ship," probably from a Scandinavian source, such as Old Norse stjorn "a steering," related to or derived from styra "to guide" (see steer (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 旋转式机器；[动力] 转缸式发动机

etymology.story: 1731, from Medieval Latin rotarius "pertaining to wheels," from Latin rota "a wheel, a potter's wheel; wheel for torture," from PIE root *ret- "to run, to turn, to roll" (source also of Sanskrit rathah "car, chariot;" Avestan ratho; Lithuanian ratas "wheel," ritu "I roll;" Old High German rad, German Rad, Dutch rad, Old Frisian reth, Old Saxon rath, Old Irish roth, Welsh rhod "carriage wheel").

meanings.definitionCn: [化学] 化合物；混合物；复合词
合成；混合；恶化，加重；和解，妥协

etymology.story: late 14c., originally compouned, past participle of compounen (see compound (v.)).
"clear rich-red variety of corundum," c.

meanings.definitionCn: 非常的;格外的;意外的;离奇的;临时的

morphology.mnemonic: extra + 顺序/等级 + …的场所/与…有关

etymology.story: early 15c., from Latin extraordinarius "out of the common order," from extra ordinem "out of order," especially the usual order, from extra "out" (see extra-) + ordinem, accusative of ordo "order" (see order (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: regul + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1670s, "act of regulating; state of being reduced to order," noun of action from regulate.
1620s, "to intertwine" (as a past participle adjective, early 15c.), from Latin complicatus "folded together; confused, intricate," past participle of complicare (see complication).

meanings.definitionCn: 使…困惑；使…为难；苦思而得出

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French evident and directly from Latin evidentem (nominative evidens) "perceptible, clear, obvious, apparent" from ex- "fully, out of" (see ex-) + videntem (nominative videns), present participle of videre "to see" (see vision)..

morphology.mnemonic: 统治 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: "room and provisions, lodging," c.

Old English mere "sea, ocean; lake, pool, pond, cistern," from Proto-Germanic *mari (source also of Old Norse marr, Old Saxon meri "sea," Middle Dutch maer, Dutch meer "lake, sea, pool," Old High German mari, German Meer "sea," Gothic marei "sea," mari-saiws "lake"), from PIE *mori- "sea" (source also of Latin mare, Old Church Slavonic morje, Russian more, Lithuanian mares, Old Irish muir, Welsh mor "sea," Gaulish Are-morici "people living near the sea")..

early 14c., "area outside a town or city," whether agricultural or residential but most frequently residential, from Old French suburbe "suburb of a town," from Latin suburbium "an outlying part of a city" (especially Rome), from sub "below, near" (see sub-) + urbs (genitive urbis) "city" (see urban).
1650s, from French simplifier "to make simpler" (15c.), from Medieval Latin simplificare "to simplify," from Latin simplex "simple" (see simplex) + root of facere "to make" (see factitious).

late 13c., from Anglo-French anuier, Old French enoiier, anuier "to weary, vex, anger; be troublesome or irksome to," from Late Latin inodiare "make loathsome," from Latin (esse) in odio "(it is to me) hateful," ablative of odium "hatred" (see odium).
"join or fasten with glue," late 14c., from Old French gluer, gluier "smear with glue; join together," from glu "glue, birdlime" (see glue (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 反映；反射，照出；表达；显示;反省

etymology.story: late 14c., "turn or bend back;" early 15c., "to divert, to turn aside, deflect," from Old French reflecter (14c.), from Latin reflectere "bend back, turn back" (see reflection).

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 给予/分配 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French contribution and directly from Latin contributionem (nominative contributio), noun of action from past participle stem of contribuere "to bring together, add, contribute," from com- "together" (see com-) + tribuere "to allot, pay" (see tribute)..

morphology.mnemonic: 向前/支持 + 走/让步 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late Old English, "act of marching or proceeding," from Old French procession "procession" (religious or secular), 11c., and directly from Late Latin processionem (nominative processio) "religious procession," in classical Latin "a marching onward, a going forward, advance," noun of action from past participle stem of procedere (see proceed)..
1886, "devotee of the teachings of Marx," from French marxiste, from Karl Marx (1818-1883), German political theorist.

1580s, from Middle French massacre "wholesale slaughter, carnage," from Old French macacre, macecle "slaughterhouse, butchery," of unknown origin; perhaps related to Latin macellum "provisions store, butcher shop.".

late 14c., "lacking wisdom or knowledge; unaware," from Old French ignorant (14c.), from Latin ignorantem (nominative ignorans) "not knowing, ignorant," present participle of ignorare "not to know, to be unacquainted; mistake, misunderstand; take no notice of, pay no attention to," from assimilated form of in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + Old Latin gnarus "aware, acquainted with" (source also of Classical Latin noscere "to know," notus "known"), from Proto-Latin suffixed form *gno-ro-, suffixed form of PIE root *gno- "to know" (see know).

meanings.definitionCn: 可忽略不计的，微不足道的

etymology.story: "capable of being neglected," 1819, from negligence + -ible.
also set-back, 1670s, "reversal, check to progress," from set (v.) + back (adv.).

"sprinkle liquid in drops," 1520s, from Middle Dutch sprayen, from Proto-Germanic *sprewjan (source also of German sprühen "to sparkle, drizzle," Spreu "chaff," literally "that which flies about"), from extended form of PIE root *sper- (4) "to sow, scatter" (see sprout (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 区分；辨别；使杰出，使表现突出

etymology.story: 1560s, from Middle French distinguiss-, stem of distinguer, or directly from Latin distinguere "to separate between, keep separate, mark off, distinguish," perhaps literally "separate by pricking," from dis- "apart" (see dis-) + -stinguere "to prick" (compare extinguish and Latin instinguere "to incite, impel").
early 13c., houlen, probably ultimately of imitative origin; similar formations are found in other Germanic languages.

morphology.mnemonic: in + 伸展/趋向 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., entencioun, "purpose, design, aim or object; will, wish, desire, that which is intended," from Old French entencion "intent, purpose, aspiration; will; thought" (12c.), from Latin intentionem (nominative intentio) "a stretching out, straining, exertion, effort; attention," noun of action from intendere "to turn one's attention," literally "to stretch out" (see intend).

meanings.definitionCn: 粗鲁的；无礼的；狂暴的；未开化的

etymology.story:
late 13c., "coarse, rough" (of surfaces), from Old French ruide (13c.) or directly from Latin rudis "rough, crude, unlearned," a word of uncertain etymology, related to rudus "rubble." The usual preferred derivation is that it is from the same source as Latin rufus "red" (see rufous) via a notion of raw ("red") meat, but de Vaan points out "there is not a shimmer of a meaning 'red' in rudis or in rudus 'rubble', so that the supposed shift from 'crude (meat)' > 'crude' rests in the air."

Sense of "ill-mannered, uncultured; uneducated, uncultured" is from mid-14c.

meanings.definitionCn: (疾病的)症状；(不好事情的)征兆,表征

etymology.story: 1540s, re-Latinized from sinthoma (late 14c.), from Medieval Latin sinthoma "symptom of a disease," altered from Late Latin symptoma, from Greek symptoma "a happening, accident, disease," from stem of sympiptein "to befall, happen; coincide, fall together," from assimilated form of syn- "together" (see syn-) + piptein "to fall," from PIE *pi-pt-, reduplicated form of root *pet- "to rush; to fly" (see petition (n.)).
Old English þærbig "thus, by means of or because of that;" see there + by.

meanings.definitionCn: 灾难性的；损失惨重的；悲伤的

etymology.story: 1580s, "ill-starred," from French désastreux (16c.), from désastre (see disaster) or from Italian desastroso.

late 14c., "pertaining to the burial of the dead," mid-15c., from Medieval Latin funeralia "funeral rites," originally neuter plural of Late Latin funeralis "having to do with a funeral," from Latin funus (genitive funeris) "funeral, funeral procession, burial rites; death, corpse," a word of uncertain origin, perhaps ultimately from PIE root *dheu- (3) "to die." Singular and plural used interchangeably in English until c.

meanings.definitionCn: 冲动；[电子] 脉冲；刺激；神经冲动；推动力

etymology.story: early 15c., "an act of impelling, a thrust, push," from Latin impulsus "a push against, pressure, shock," figuratively "incitement, instigation," past participle of impellere "to strike against, push against" (see impel).

meanings.definitionCn: 传染的,传染性的,有感染力的

morphology.mnemonic: in + 做/制造 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: "catching, having the quality of spreading from person to person, communicable by infection," 1540s of diseases, 1610s of emotions, actions, etc.; see infection + -ous.

meanings.definitionCn: 突出的，显著的；杰出的；卓越的

etymology.story: mid-15c., "projecting, jutting out," from Latin prominentem (nominative prominens) "prominent," present participle of prominere "jut or stand out, be prominent, overhang," from pro- "before, forward" (see pro-) + -minere "project, jut out," which is related to mons "hill" (see mount (n.1)).
late 15c., from superior (adj.) + -ity, or directly from Medieval Latin superioritatem (nominative superioritas), from superior..

late 13c., "article of value used for adornment," from Anglo-French juel, Old French jouel "ornament; present; gem, jewel" (12c.), which is perhaps [Watkins] from Medieval Latin jocale, from Latin jocus "pastime, sport," in Vulgar Latin "that which causes joy" (see joke (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 基础 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: mid-15c., "primary, original, pertaining to a foundation," modeled on Late Latin fundamentalis "of the foundation," from Latin fundamentum "foundation" (see fundament).

morphology.mnemonic: globe-shaped glass vessel + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1670s, "spherical," from globe + -al (1).

morphology.mnemonic: 超过/外部 + one's occupation, branch of business

meanings.definitionCn: [电影][电视] 淡出；[电影][电视] 淡入
名义上的;(金额,租金)微不足道的 name

morphology.mnemonic: name名 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: early 15c., "pertaining to nouns," from Latin nominalis "pertaining to a name or names," from nomen (genitive nominis) "name," cognate with Old English nama (see name (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + 走/让步

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French exces (14c.) "excess, extravagance, outrage," from Latin excessus "departure, a going beyond the bounds of reason or beyond the subject," from stem of excedere "to depart, go beyond" (see exceed).

meanings.definitionCn: 节俭；节约；[植] 海石竹
透视画法,透视图;远景,前途;观点,看法

morphology.mnemonic: 通过/彻底 + 看/观察 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: late 14c., "science of optics," from Old French perspective and directly from Medieval Latin perspectiva ars "science of optics," from fem.

meanings.definitionCn: 银河；[天] 星系；银河系；一群显赫的人

etymology.story: late 14c., from French galaxie or directly from Late Latin galaxias "the Milky Way" as a feature in the night sky (in classical Latin via lactea or circulus lacteus)from Greek galaxias (adj.), in galaxias kyklos, literally "milky circle," from gala (genitive galaktos) "milk" (see lacto-).

meanings.definitionCn: 同等;平等;相等;等式;等同性

morphology.mnemonic: equi + 与…有关的/形容词 + ity

etymology.story: late 14c., "evenness, smoothness, uniformity;" c.

morphology.mnemonic: 说/讲 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1650s, from Late Latin dictationem (nominative dictatio), noun of action from past participle stem of dictare (see dictate (v.))..

root.meaning: supporter of the American cause in the War of Independence

morphology.mnemonic: supporter of the American cause in the War of Independence + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: "war waged against a government by some portion of its subjects," mid-14c., from Old French rebellion (14c.) and directly from Latin rebellionem (nominative rebellio) "rebellion, revolt; renewal of war," from rebellis (see rebel (adj.))..

"enter in a hostile manner," late 15c., from Latin invadere "to go, come, or get into; enter violently, penetrate into as an enemy, assail, assault, make an attack on," from in- "in" (see in- (2)) + vadere "go, walk" (see vamoose).

meanings.definitionCn: 疲倦的；厌烦的；令人厌烦的

etymology.story: Old English wergian "to be or become tired" (intransitive), gewergian "to exhaust, to make tired" (transitive), from the source of weary (adj.).

meanings.definitionCn: 彻底的， 完全的；细心的； 认真的； 周到的

etymology.story: 1570s, from Middle French démocratie (14c.), from Medieval Latin democratia (13c.), from Greek demokratia "popular government," from demos "common people," originally "district" (see demotic), + kratos "rule, strength" (see -cracy)..
1530s, from Latin pæninsula "a peninsula," literally "almost an island," from pæne "almost" + insula "island" (see isle).

meanings.definitionCn: 离弃，丢弃；遗弃，抛弃；放弃

etymology.story: "a letting loose, surrender to natural impulses," 1822, from a sense in French abandon (see abandon (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 全家人，一家人；(包括佣工在内的)家庭，户

etymology.story: late 14c., "members of a family collectively (including servants)," also "furniture and articles belonging to a house;" see house (n.) + hold (n.1).

meanings.definitionCn: 逆的，相反的；敌对的；不利的；有害的

etymology.story: late 14c., "contrary, opposing," from Old French avers (13c., Modern French adverse) "antagonistic, unfriendly, contrary, foreign" (as in gent avers "infidel race"), from Latin adversus "turned against, turned toward, fronting, facing," figuratively "hostile, adverse, unfavorable," past participle of advertere, from ad- "to" (see ad-) + vertere "to turn" (see versus).

1765, from French bulletin (16c.), modeled on Italian bulletino, diminutive of bulletta "document, voting slip," itself a diminutive of Latin bulla (see bull (n.2)) with equivalent of Old French -elet (see -let).

1630s, "reason, rationale," from Latin ratio "reckoning, numbering, calculation; business affair, procedure," also "reason, reasoning, judgment, understanding," from rat-, past participle stem of reri "to reckon, calculate," also "think" (see reason (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 连接，接合；交叉点；接合点

morphology.mnemonic: 连接 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1711, "act of joining," from Latin iunctionem (nominative iunctio) "a joining, uniting," noun of action from past participle stem of iungere "to join together" (see jugular).

early 15c., "a prompting" (a sense now obsolete), from Old French instinct (14c.) or directly from Latin instinctus "instigation, impulse, inspiration," noun use of past participle of instinguere "to incite, impel," from in- "into, in, on, upon" (see in- (2)) + stinguere "prick, goad," from PIE *steig- "to prick, stick, pierce" (see stick (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 大炮；加农炮；榴弹炮；机关炮

morphology.mnemonic: after + to parry, to fend off

etymology.story: Old English æftanweard, from æftan "after" (see aft) + -weard suffix indicating direction (see -ward); nautical use as aftward, then expanded by influence of after; variant afterwards shows adverbial genitive..

morphology.mnemonic: 认为/名声 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: mid-14c., "credit, good reputation," from Latin reputationem (nominative reputatio) "consideration, a thinking over," noun of action from past participle stem of reputare "reflect upon, reckon, count over," from re- "repeatedly" (see re-) + putare "to reckon, consider" (see putative)..

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + 向下/移除/取消 + 悬挂/称量/支付 + ance

etymology.story: 1630s, "fact of not depending on others or another, self-support and self-government;" see independent + -ence.
mid-14c., from stem of Old French restreindre "press, push together; curb, bridle; bandage" (12c.), from Latin restringere "draw back tightly, confine, check" (see restriction).

meanings.definitionCn: 尴尬的；笨拙的；棘手的；不合适的

etymology.story: mid-14c., "in the wrong direction," from awk "back-handed" + adverbial suffix -weard (see -ward).

"flesh of sheep used as food," late 13c., from Old French moton "mutton; ram, wether, sheep" (12c., Modern French mouton), from Medieval Latin multonem (8c.), probably from Gallo-Roman *multo-s, accusative of Celtic *multo "sheep" (source also of Old Irish molt "wether," Mid-Breton mout, Welsh mollt); the same word also was borrowed into Italian as montone "a sheep." Transferred slang sense of "food for lust, loose women, prostitutes" (1510s) led to extensive British slang uses down to the present day for woman variously regarded as seeking lovers or as lust objects.

late 14c., from Old French impacient "impatient" (Modern French impatient), from Latin impatientem (nominative impatiens) "that cannot bear, intolerant, impatient," from assimilated form of in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + patiens "bearing, enduring" (see patience).

meanings.definitionCn: 溢出，溅出；溢出量；摔下；小塞子

etymology.story: 1845, originally "a throw or fall from a horse," from spill (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 摇篮；发源地；发祥地；支船架
抚育；把搁在支架上；把放在摇篮内

morphology.mnemonic: 劳动/工作 + …的场所/与…有关

etymology.story: 1620s, from Latin inspectus, past participle of inspicere "look at, observe, view; look into, inspect, examine," from in- "into" (see in- (2)) + specere "to look" (see scope (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 作战；参加竞选；参加活动
生死攸关的,重大的;生命的,生机的

etymology.story: late 14c., "of or manifesting life," from Latin vitalis "of or belonging to life," from vita "life," related to vivere "to live," from PIE root *gweie- (1) "to live" (see bio-).
early 15c., "armed encounter, battle," from Old French conflit and directly from Latin conflictus (see conflict (v.)).
1944, in reference to information, 1952 in reference to persons, from up (adv.) + date (v.1).
early 15c., from Latin detectus, past participle of detegere "uncover, expose," figuratively "discover, reveal, disclose," from de- "un-, off" (see de-) + tegere "to cover" (see stegosaurus).

meanings.definitionCn: 爱好者；业余爱好者；外行

etymology.story: 1784, "one who has a taste for (something)," from French amateur "lover of," from Latin amatorem (nominative amator) "lover," agent noun from amatus, past participle of amare "to love" (see Amy).

early 14c., condempner "to blame, censure," from Old French condamner "to condemn" (11c.), from Latin condemnare "to sentence, doom, blame, disapprove," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + damnare "to harm, damage" (see damn).
1530s, from Middle French commerce (14c.), from Latin commercium "trade, trafficking," from com- "together" (see com-) + merx (genitive mercis) "merchandise" (see market (n.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 协作; 援助; 帮助

etymology.story: early 15c., "act of helping or aiding," from Middle French assistance, from assister (see assist (v.))..

late 14c., "profound depth," from Old French golf "a gulf, whirlpool," from Italian golfo "a gulf, a bay," from Late Latin colfos, from Greek kolpos "bay, gulf of the sea," earlier "trough between waves, fold of a loose garment," originally "bosom," the common notion being "curved shape." This is from PIE *kwelp- "to arch, to vault" (compare Old English hwealf, a-hwielfan "to overwhelm").

meanings.definitionCn: 定义,解释;(轮廓影像等的)清晰度;阐明;

etymology.story: late 14c., "decision, setting of boundaries," from Old French definicion, from Latin definitionem (nominative definitio), noun of action from past participle stem of definire (see define).

meanings.definitionCn: 刮脸，剃胡子；修面；<口>侥幸逃过，幸免；剃刀，刮刀

etymology.story: Old English sceafan (strong verb, past tense scof, past participle scafen), "to scrape, shave, polish," from Proto-Germanic *skaban (source also of Old Norse skafa, Middle Dutch scaven, German schaben, Gothic skaban "scratch, shave, scrape"), from PIE *skabh-, collateral form of root *(s)kep- "to cut, to scrape, to hack" (see scabies).

morphology.mnemonic: 拿/消耗 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: "a summary statement or account," c.

whitish metal element, 1755, coined in 1754 by Swedish mineralogist Axel von Cronstedt (1722-1765) from shortening of Swedish kopparnickel "copper-colored ore" (from which it was first obtained), a half-translation of German Kupfernickel, literally "copper demon," from Kupfer (see copper) + Nickel "demon, goblin, rascal" (a pet form of masc.
early 15c., from Old French acostumer (12c., Modern French accoutumer), from à "to" (see ad-) + costume (see costume (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 使需要，必需；承担；遗传给；蕴含

etymology.story: mid-14c., "convert (an estate) into 'fee tail' (feudum talliatum)," from en- (1) "make" + taile "legal limitation," especially of inheritance, ruling who succeeds in ownership and preventing the property from being sold off, from Anglo-French taile, Old French taillie, past participle of taillier "allot, cut to shape," from Late Latin taliare "to split" (see tailor (n.)).

1718, "committee of cardinals in charge of Catholic missionary work," short for Congregatio de Propaganda Fide "congregation for propagating the faith," a committee of cardinals established 1622 by Gregory XV to supervise foreign missions.

meanings.definitionCn: 差的；自卑的；下级的，下等的

etymology.story: "shelter or protection from danger or distress," late 14c., from Old French refuge "hiding place" (12c.), from Latin refugium "a taking refuge; place to flee back to," from re- "back" (see re-) + fugere "to flee" (see fugitive (adj.)) + -ium "place for.".

meanings.definitionCn: 戏剧的；急剧的；引人注目的；激动人心的

etymology.story: 1580s, from Late Latin dramaticus, from Greek dramatikos "pertaining to plays," from drama (genitive dramatos; see drama).

mid-14c., comenden, from Latin commendare "to commit to the care or keeping (of someone), to entrust to; to commit to writing;" hence "to set off, render agreeable, praise," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + mandare "to commit to one's charge" (see mandate (n.)).

lute-like musical instrument, 1620s, from French guitare, which was altered by Spanish and Provençal forms from Old French guiterre, earlier guiterne, from Latin cithara, from Greek kithara "cithara," a triangular seven-stringed musical instrument related to the lyre, perhaps from Persian sihtar (see sitar).

1580s, from Spanish mosquito "little gnat," diminutive of mosca "fly," from Latin musca "fly," from PIE root *mu- "gnat, fly," imitative of insect buzzing (compare Sanskrit maksa-, Greek myia, Old English mycg, Modern English midge, Old Church Slavonic mucha), perhaps imitative of the sound of humming insects..

meanings.definitionCn: 人工的，人造的，人为的；虚伪的，做作的

morphology.mnemonic: device, trick + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: late 14c., in the phrase artificial day "part of the day from sunrise to sunset," from Old French artificial, from Latin artificialis "of or belonging to art," from artificium (see artifice).

1530s, "a hanger-on, a toady, person who lives on others," from Middle French parasite (16c.) or directly from Latin parasitus "toady, sponger," and directly from Greek parasitos "one who lives at another's expense, person who eats at the table of another," from noun use of an adjective meaning "feeding beside," from para- "beside" (see para- (1)) + sitos "food," of unknown origin.
1630s, from deficience (mid-15c.) + -cy; or from Late Latin deficientia, from deficientem (see deficient)..

meanings.definitionCn: 虚构的，假想的；想像的；虚数的

etymology.story: "not real, existing only in fancy," late 14c., ymaginaire, from imagine + -ary; or else from Late Latin imaginarius "seeming, fancied," also literal, "pertaining to an image," from Latin imaginari "picture to oneself." Imaginary friend (one who does not exist) attested by 1789..

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + act of moving + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1590s; see remove (v.) + -al (2)..

meanings.definitionCn: 注释； 评论； 意见

etymology.story: early 15c., from Middle French commenter (15c.), from Latin commentari, from commentum (see comment (n.)).

mid-14c., "escape inclusion in; lie beyond the scope of," from Old French transcendre "transcend, surpass," and directly from Latin transcendere "climb over or beyond, surmount, overstep," from trans- "beyond" (see trans-) + scandere "to climb" (see scan (v.)).
late 14c., from Old French desirable (12c.), from dasirer (see desire (v.)).

1610s, from Italian balcone, from balco "scaffold," from a Germanic source (perhaps Langobardic *balko- "beam," cognate with Old English balca "beam, ridge;" see balk (n.)) + Italian augmentative suffix -one.

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 站立/设置

etymology.story: 1520s, from Middle French consister (14c.) or directly from Latin consistere "to stand firm, take a standing position, stop, halt," from com- "together" (see com-) + sistere "to place," causative of stare "to stand, be standing" (see stay (v.)).

late 14c., "act of preparing," from Latin praeparationem (nominative praeparatio) "a making ready," noun of action from past participle stem of praeparare "prepare," from prae "before" (see pre-) + parare "make ready" (see pare).
Old English prician "to prick, pierce, prick out, sting," from West Germanic *prikojan (source also of Low German pricken, Dutch prikken "to prick"), of uncertain origin.

meanings.definitionCn: 兼容的；能共处的；可并立的

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Middle French compatible (15c.), from Medieval Latin compatibilis, from Late Latin compati (see compassion).

late 14c., "Latin grammar, rules of Latin," from Old French gramaire "grammar; learning," especially Latin and philology, also "(magic) incantation, spells, mumbo-jumbo" (12c., Modern French grammaire), an "irregular semi-popular adoption" [OED] of Latin grammatica "grammar, philology," perhaps via an unrecorded Medieval Latin form *grammaria.

1580s, "one who passes judgment," from Middle French critique (14c.), from Latin criticus "a judge, literary critic," from Greek kritikos "able to make judgments," from krinein "to separate, decide" (see crisis).

meanings.definitionCn: 使能够，使成为可能；授予权利或方法

morphology.mnemonic: 使成为/放入 + 有能力的，能干的

etymology.story: early 15c., "to make fit;" mid-15c., "to make able to," from en- (1) "make, put in" + able.

meanings.definitionCn: 依靠的；从属的；取决于…的

morphology.mnemonic: 向下/移除/取消 + 悬挂/称量/支付 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: 15c., variant spelling of dependant, now mostly restricted to adjectival use; see -ance.

meanings.definitionCn: 职业，专业；声明，宣布，表白

morphology.mnemonic: declare openly + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1550s, "to give up as hopeless," from French déplorer (13c.), from Latin deplorare "deplore, bewail, lament, give up for lost," from de- "entirely" (see de-) + plorare "weep, cry out," which is of unknown origin.

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + device for pressing cloth
向/朝/在 + a piece of rising ground

etymology.story: Old English arisan "to get up, rise; spring from, originate; spring up, ascend" (cognate with Old Saxon arisan, Gothic urreisan), from a- (1) "of" + rise (v.).

early 14c., cherischen, from Old French cheriss-, present participle stem of chierir "to hold dear" (12c., Modern French chérir), from chier "dear," from Latin carus "dear, costly, beloved" (see whore).

meanings.definitionCn: [心理] 想象力；空想；幻想物

etymology.story: "faculty of the mind which forms and manipulates images," mid-14c., ymaginacion, from Old French imaginacion "concept, mental picture; hallucination," from Latin imaginationem (nominative imaginatio) "imagination, a fancy," noun of action from past participle stem of imaginari (see imagine)..

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 站立/设置 + ance

etymology.story: mid-14c., from Old French resistance, earlier resistence, from Late Latin resistentia, from present participle stem of Latin resistere "make a stand against, oppose" (see resist).

early 15c., from Old French éminent "prominent" (13c.) or directly from Latin eminentem (nominative eminens) "standing out, projecting, prominent, high," figuratively "distinguished, distinctive," present participle of eminere "stand out, project; be prominent, be conspicuous," from assimilated form of ex- "out" (see ex-) + -minere, which is related to mons "hill" (see mount (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 在…的下面；在…的形式下；在…的支配下

etymology.story: Old English underneoðan, from under + neoðan "below" (see beneath)..

meanings.definitionCn: 谱，光谱，频谱；范围，幅度，系列

etymology.story: 1610s, "apparition, specter," from Latin spectrum (plural spectra) "an appearance, image, apparition, specter," from specere "to look at, view" (see scope (n.1)).

early 13c., from Old French empereor "emperor, leader, ruler" (11c.; accusative; nominative emperere; Modern French empereur), from Latin imperiatorem (nominative imperiator) "commander, emperor," from past participle stem of imperare "to command" (see empire).

morphology.mnemonic: 向前/支持 + to grow long, lengthen,

etymology.story: early 15c., back-formation from prolongation or else from Old French prolonguer, porloignier (13c.), from Late Latin prolongare "to prolong, extend," from Latin pro- "forth" (see pro-) + longus "long" (adj.); see long (adj.).
late 14c., "one of the halves of a folding door," from Latin valva (plural valvae) "section of a folding or revolving door," literally "that which turns," related to volvere "to roll" (see volvox).

meanings.definitionCn: 特别喜欢的， 中意的

morphology.mnemonic: to resemble, look somewhat like + ite

etymology.story: "person or thing regarded with especial liking," 1580s, from Middle French favorit, perhaps via Italian favorito, noun use of past participle of favorire, from favore, from Latin favorem "inclination, partiality, support" (see favor (n.)).
late 14c., from Old French destresser, from Vulgar Latin *districtiare (see distress (n.)).
late 14c., contraction of remenant (c.

meanings.definitionCn: 翻阅；以拇指拨弄；作搭车手势；笨拙地摆弄
陡峭的；不合理的；夸大的；急剧升降的

etymology.story: "to soak in a liquid," early 14c., of uncertain origin, originally in reference to barley or malt, probably cognate with Old Norse steypa "to pour out, throw" (perhaps from an unrecorded Old English cognate), from Proto-Germanic *staupijanan.

meanings.definitionCn: 古典的；经典的；传统的；第一流的

morphology.mnemonic: class + ic + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1590s, "of the highest rank" (originally in literature), from classic + -al (1).

"one who acts in place of another," early 15c., from Middle French substitut (noun use) and directly from Latin substitutus, past participle of substituere "put in place of another" (see substitution).

meanings.definitionCn: 在树皮上刻路标；公开宣布
火焰，烈火；光辉；情感爆发
再者， 加之， 而且

etymology.story: late 14c., in phrase and yit more ouer "there is more to say;" from more (adv.) + over (adv.).

meanings.definitionCn: 神秘的；不可思议的；难解的

morphology.mnemonic: myst + …的场所/与…有关 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: 1610s, "full of mystery," from Latin mysterium (see mystery (n.1)) + -ous.

morphology.mnemonic: 暴力/违反 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: early 15c., "to break" (an oath, etc.), from Latin violatus, past participle of violare "treat with violence, dishonor, outrage" (see violation).

morphology.mnemonic: paid by the recipient + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: early 15c., from Middle French collectif, from Latin collectivus, from collectus (see collect).

"young ox," Old English steor "bullock," from Proto-Germanic *steuraz (source also of Old Saxon stior, Old Norse stjorr, Swedish tjur, Danish tyr, Middle Dutch, Dutch, German stier, Gothic stiur "bull"), perhaps from PIE *steu-ro-, denoting "larger domestic animal" (see taurus).
Old English undergan "obtain, get; undertake," from under + gan (see go (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/一起 + 感情/疾病 + ize

etymology.story: "have fellow-feeling," c.

meanings.definitionCn: 由铁道运输；铺设铁路；以捏造不实之罪使入狱
代理(处)；代办处；[美](政府的)机关，厅

etymology.story: 1650s, "active operation," from Medieval Latin agentia, noun of state from Latin agentem (nominative agens) "effective, powerful," present participle of agere "to drive, conduct" (see act (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 息票；赠券；联票；[经] 配给券

etymology.story: 1822, "certificate of interest due on a bond" (which could be cut from the bond and presented for payment), from French coupon, literally "piece cut off," from couper "to cut," from coup "a blow" (see coup).

early 14c., from Old French multitude (12c.) and directly from Latin multitudinem (nominative multitudo) "a great number, a crowd; the crowd, the common people," from multus "many, much" (see multi-) + suffix -tudo (see -tude).

late 14c., from Old French enhabiter, enabiter "dwell in, live in, reside" (12c.), from Latin inhabitare "to dwell in," from in- "in" (see in- (2)) + habitare "to dwell," frequentative of habere "hold, have" (see habit (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 围攻；包围；围城；不断袭击；长期努力

etymology.story: early 13c., "a seat" (as in Siege Perilous, early 13c., the vacant seat at Arthur's Round Table, according to prophecy to be occupied safely only by the knight destined to find the Holy Grail), from Old French sege "seat, throne," from Vulgar Latin *sedicum "seat," from Latin sedere "sit" (see sedentary).
1540s, from Latin abstractus or else from the adjective abstract.
1650s, from Latin agenda, literally "things to be done," neuter plural of agendus, gerundive of agere "to do" (see act (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: seem, have a certain appearance + ance

etymology.story: late 14c., "visible state or form, figure; mere show," from Anglo-French apparaunce, Old French aparance "appearance, display, pomp" (13c.), from Latin apparentia, abstract noun from aparentem, past participle of apparere (see appear).

meanings.definitionCn: 液体的；清澈的；明亮的；易变的
匿名的，无名的，姓氏不明的

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 名/法则 + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: of unknown origin; attested in London criminal slang as adjective (1775 "a counterfeit"), verb (1812 "to rob"), and noun (1851, "a swindle;" of persons 1888, "a swindler"), but probably older.
1520s, from fisher + man (n.)..

Old English spannan "to join, link, clasp, fasten, bind, connect; stretch, span," from Proto-Germanic *spannan (source also of Old Norse spenna, Old Frisian spanna, Middle Dutch spannen, Dutch spannan "stretch, bend, hoist, hitch," Old High German spannan, German spannen "to join, fasten, extend, connect"), from PIE root *(s)pen- "to draw, stretch, spin" (source also of Latin pendere "to hang, to cause to hang," pondus "weight" (perhaps the notion is the weight of a thing measured by how much it stretches a cord), pensare "to weigh, consider;" Greek ponos "toil," ponein "to toil;" Lithuanian spendziu "lay a snare;" Old Church Slavonic peti "stretch, strain," pato "fetter," pina "I span;" Old English spinnan "to spin;" for other cognates, see spin (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 奇异的（指态度，容貌，款式等）

etymology.story: 1640s, from French bizarre "odd, fantastic" (16c.), from Italian bizarro "irascible, tending to quick flashes of anger" (13c.), from bizza "fit of anger, quick flash of anger" (13c.).
"head," early 14c., polle "hair of the head; piece of fur from the head of an animal," also "head," from Middle Low German or Middle Dutch pol "head, top." Sense extended early 14c.

meanings.definitionCn: 少数民族；少数派；未成年

root.meaning: secondary subject of study, subject of study with fewer credits than a major

morphology.mnemonic: secondary subject of study, subject of study with fewer credits than a major + ity

etymology.story: 1530s, "condition of being smaller," from Middle French minorité (15c.), or directly from Medieval Latin minoritatem (nominative minoritas), from Latin minor (see minor (adj.)).
mid-15c., "full of (divine) grace," also "pleasant, sweet," from grace (n.) + -ful.

meanings.definitionCn: 更好的，更可取的；更合意的

morphology.mnemonic: to esteem (something) more than others + able

etymology.story: 1640s, from or on model of French préférable, from préfér (see prefer).

meanings.definitionCn: 参考，参照；涉及，提及；参考书目；介绍信；证明书

root.meaning: to commit to some authority for a decision

morphology.mnemonic: to commit to some authority for a decision + ance

etymology.story: mid-14c., from Anglo-French conspiracie, Old French conspiracie "conspiracy, plot," from Latin conspirationem (nominative conspiratio) "agreement, union, unanimity," noun of action from conspirare (see conspire); earlier in same sense was conspiration (early 14c.), from French conspiration (13c.), from Latin conspirationem.

meanings.definitionCn: （身体或器官内的）室，膛；房间；会所
把…关在室内；装填（弹药等）
青蛙；[铁路] 辙叉；饰扣
整理， 排列， 布置 A；安排， 筹备

etymology.story: late 14c., "draw up a line of battle," from Old French arengier (12c.), from a- "to" (see ad-) + rangier "set in a row" (Modern French ranger), from rang "rank," from Frankish *hring (see rank (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 宣传，宣扬；公开；广告；注意

etymology.story: 1791, "condition of being public," from French publicité (1690s), from Medieval Latin publicitatem (nominative publicitas), from Latin publicus (see public (adj.)).

mid-13c., "part from each other," from Old French departir (10c.) "to divide, distribute; separate (oneself), depart; die," from Late Latin departire "divide" (transitive), from de- "from" (see de-) + partire "to part, divide," from pars (genitive partis) "a part" (see part (n.)).
alternative spelling of archaeology.

meanings.definitionCn: 始终如一的， 不变的； 恒定的

etymology.story: early 15c., from Old French invariable (14c.) and directly from Medieval Latin invariabilis, from in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + variabilis "changeable" (see variable).

meanings.definitionCn: 化石；僵化的事物；顽固不化的人

etymology.story:
1610s, "any thing dug up;" 1650s (adj.) "obtained by digging" (of coal, salt, etc.), from French fossile (16c.), from Latin fossilis "dug up," from fossus, past participle of fodere "to dig," from PIE root *bhedh- "to dig, pierce."

Restricted noun sense of "geological remains of a plant or animal" is from 1736 (the adjective in the sense "pertaining to fossils" is from 1660s); slang meaning "old person" first recorded 1859.
"to push, prod, thrust," especially with something pointed, c.

meanings.definitionCn: 设备；固定装置；固定于某处不大可能移动之物

morphology.mnemonic: dose of narcotic + 行为/过程/结果

etymology.story: 1590s, "act of fixing," perhaps from fix (v.) on model of mixture, or from an assumed Latin *fixitatem.

meanings.definitionCn: 碰撞；冲突；（意见，看法）的抵触；（政党等的）倾轧

etymology.story: early 15c., from Middle French collision (15c.), from Latin collisionem (nominative collisio) "a dashing together," noun of action from collidere (see collide)..

morphology.mnemonic: 狂热 + 行为/过程/结果

etymology.story: early 13c., "character attributed to someone;" late 13c., "celebrity, renown," from Old French fame "fame, reputation, renown, rumor" (12c.), from Latin fama "talk, rumor, report; reputation, public opinion; renown, good reputation," but also "ill-fame, scandal, reproach," from PIE root *bha- (2) "to speak, tell, say" (source also of Sanskrit bhanati "speaks;" Latin fari "to say," fabula "narrative, account, tale, story;" Armenian ban, bay "word, term;" Old Church Slavonic bajati "to talk, tell;" Old English boian "to boast," ben "prayer, request;" Greek pheme "speech, voice, utterance, a speaking, talk," phone "voice, sound," phanai "to speak;" Old Irish bann "law").

1670s, earlier slogorne (1510s), "battle cry," from Gaelic sluagh-ghairm "battle cry used by Scottish Highland or Irish clans," from sluagh "army, host, slew," from Celtic and Balto-Slavic *slough- "help, service." Second element is gairm "a cry" (see garrulous).

meanings.definitionCn: 胸；胸怀；中间；胸襟；内心；乳房；内部

etymology.story: Old English bosm "breast; womb; surface; ship's hold," from West Germanic *bosm- (source also of Old Frisian bosm, Old Saxon bosom, Middle Dutch boesem, Dutch boezem, Old High German buosam, German Busen "bosom, breast"), perhaps from PIE root *bhou- "to grow, swell," or *bhaghus "arm" (in which case the primary notion would be "enclosure formed by the breast and the arms").

mid-15c., "thick slice of meat cut for roasting," probably from a Scandinavian source such as Old Norse steik "roast meat," related to steikja "to roast on a spit," and ultimately meaning "something stuck" (on a spit), from Proto-Germanic *staiko-, from PIE *steig- "to stick; pointed" (see stick (v.))..
silver-white fluid metallic element, late 14c., from Medieval Latin mercurius, from Latin Mercurius (see Mercury).
"act on each other, act reciprocally," 1805, from inter- + act (v.).

root.meaning: one who has been elected to parliament

morphology.mnemonic: one who has been elected to parliament + 身份/状态/技能

etymology.story: 1640s, "state of being a member," from member + -ship.

meanings.definitionCn: 粉；粉末；[化工][军] 火药；尘土
错误， 过失； 误差

etymology.story: also, through 18c., errour; c.

meanings.definitionCn: 妒忌的；猜疑的；唯恐失去的；戒备的

morphology.mnemonic: to make one; + 以…方式/副词化
to pay attention + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: late 14c., "solicitous, attentive," see attendant (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 痕迹，踪迹；微量；[仪] 迹线；缰绳

etymology.story: late 14c., "follow (a course); draw a line, make an outline of something," also figurative; "ponder, investigate," from Old French tracier "look for, follow, pursue" (12c., Modern French tracer), from Vulgar Latin *tractiare "delineate, score, trace" (source also of Spanish trazar "to trace, devise, plan out," Italian tracciare "to follow by foot"), a frequentative form from Latin tractus "track, course," literally "a drawing out," from past participle stem of trahere "to pull, draw" (see tract (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 数组，阵列；排列，列阵；大批，一系列；衣服

etymology.story: mid-14c., "order, arrangement," from Anglo-French arrai, Old French aroi, from areer "to put in order" (see array (v.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 努力， 尽力； 力图
努力， 尽力； 力图
区别,辨别;有差别地对待,歧视

morphology.mnemonic: 不/分离 + 判定/控告 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1620s, from Latin discriminatus, past participle of discriminare "to divide, separate," from discrimen (genitive discriminis) "interval, distinction, difference," derived noun from discernere (see discern).

root.meaning: money given for labor or services, wages

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + money given for labor or services, wages

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Old French repaier "pay back, give in return," from re- "back" (see re-) + payer "to pay" (see pay (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 警告/提醒 + …的场所/与…有关

etymology.story: "pertaining to money," 1802, from Late Latin monetarius "pertaining to money," originally "of a mint," from Latin moneta "mint, coinage" (see money).
Old English inn "lodging, dwelling, house," probably from inne (adv.) "inside, within" (see in).

meanings.definitionCn: 履行,执行;表演,演出;性能,特性;成绩

etymology.story: late a5c., "accomplishment" (of something), from perform + -ance.

meanings.definitionCn: 连续的；连载的；分期偿还的
电视连续剧；[图情] 期刊；连载小说
大使；特使，(派驻国际组织的)代表

etymology.story: late 14c., also embassador, from Middle French ambassadeur, from Old French embassator, via Provençal or Old Spanish from Latin ambactus "a servant, vassal," from Celtic amb(i)actos "a messenger, servant," from PIE *ambhi- "about" (see ambi-) + *ag- "drive, lead" (see act (v.)).
1550s, "stage in growing," from grow + -th (2), on model of health, stealth, etc.

meanings.definitionCn: 连环漫画；喜剧演员；滑稽人物
教皇，罗马教皇；权威，大师

etymology.story: Old English papa (9c.), from Church Latin papa "bishop, pope" (in classical Latin, "tutor"), from Greek papas "patriarch, bishop," originally "father." Applied to bishops of Asia Minor and taken as a title by the Bishop of Alexandria c.250.

meanings.definitionCn: 不明确的,含糊的,暧昧的

etymology.story: "uncertain as to specifics," 1540s, from Middle French vague "empty, vacant; wild, uncultivated; wandering" (13c.), from Latin vagus "strolling, wandering, rambling," figuratively "vacillating, uncertain," of unknown origin.

Old English faran "to journey, set forth, go, travel, wander, make one's way," also "be, happen, exist; be in a particular condition," from Proto-Germanic *faran "to go" (source also of Old Saxon, Old High German, Gothic faran, Old Norse and Old Frisian fara, Dutch varen, German fahren), from PIE *por- "going, passage," from root *per- (2) "to lead, pass over" (see port (n.1)).
1660s, from accurate + -cy..

meanings.definitionCn: 精神病学；精神病治疗法

morphology.mnemonic: 心理/精神 + ater + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1846, from French psychiatrie, from Medieval Latin psychiatria, literally "a healing of the soul," from Latinized form of Greek psykhe- "mind" (see psyche) + iatreia "healing, care" (see -iatric)..

morphology.mnemonic: determined, decided, absolute, final + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "a breaking into parts," from Old French resolution (14c.) or directly from Latin resolutionem (nominative resolutio) "process of reducing things into simpler forms," from past participle stem of resolvere "loosen" (see resolve).

meanings.definitionCn: 环境；安装；布置；[天] 沉落
放置；沉没；使…处于某位置（set的ing形式）

etymology.story: late 14c., "fact or action of setting (something), putting, placing, planting," verbal noun from set (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 获得；评价；划线，刻划；把…记下

etymology.story: "to cut with incisions or notches," c.

meanings.definitionCn: 大学毕业生,研究生v.大学毕业a.毕了业的

etymology.story: early 15c., "to confer a university degree upon," from Medieval Latin graduatus (see graduate (n.)).
1660s, from Medieval Latin quota, from Latin quota pars "how large a part," from quota, fem.

meanings.definitionCn: 全体一致的；意见一致的；无异议的

etymology.story: 1610s, from Latin unanimus "of one mind, in union," from unus "one" (see one) + animus "mind" (see animus).

morphology.mnemonic: 热 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1756, "having to do with hot springs," from French thermal (Buffon), from Greek therme "heat, feverish heat," from PIE *gwher- "to heat, warm" (source also of Latin fornax "an oven, kiln," formus "warm," Old English wearm; see warm (adj.)).
"scold," 1739, of unknown origin; perhaps related to Danish dialectal rag "grudge."

early 13c., "hardship, suffering, pain, bodily affliction," from Old French grief "wrong, grievance, injustice, misfortune, calamity" (13c.), from grever "afflict, burden, oppress," from Latin gravare "make heavy; cause grief," from gravis "weighty" (see grave (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 惭愧的，感到难为情的；耻于……的

etymology.story: Old English asceamed "feeling shame, filled with shame," past participle of ascamian "to feel shame," from a- intensive prefix + scamian "be ashamed, blush; cause shame" (see shame (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 抢走；轻敲；敲击致使；使着迷
未必的； 不可能的； 靠不住的

morphology.mnemonic: 不/非/否定 + like + 以…方式/副词化

etymology.story: mid-15c., "improbably," from un- (1) "not" + likely (adv.) (see likely (adj.))..

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 悬挂/称量/支付 + ix

etymology.story: 1540s, "subjoined addition to a document or book," from Latin appendix "an addition, continuation, something attached," from appendere (see append).
also hand-writing, "writing with the hand; form of writing peculiar to a person," early 15c., from hand (n.) + writing, translating Latin manuscriptum and equivalent to Greek kheirographia.

meanings.definitionCn: 生的；未加工的；阴冷的；刺痛的；擦掉皮的；无经验的；（在艺术等方面）不成熟的

etymology.story: Old English hreaw "uncooked, raw," from Proto-Germanic *khrawaz (source also of Old Norse hrar, Danish raa, Old Saxon hra, Middle Dutch rau, Dutch rauw, Old High German hrawer, German roh), from PIE root *kreue- (1) "raw flesh" (source also of Sanskrit kravih "raw flesh," krura- "bloody, raw, hard;" Greek kreas "flesh;" Latin crudus "not cooked," cruor "thick blood;" Old Irish cru, Lithuanian kraujas, Old Church Slavonic kruvi "blood;" Old English hrot "thick fluid, serum").
1550s, from court (n.) + yard (n.1)..

root.meaning: large group of people with common ancestry.

morphology.mnemonic: large group of people with common ancestry. + 与…有关的/形容词 + ity

etymology.story: 1690s, "national quality," from national + -ity (in some usages perhaps from French nationalité.

meanings.definitionCn: 市(立,政)的;地方性的,地方自治的

etymology.story: 1540s, from Middle French municipal, from Latin municipalis "of a citizen of a free town, of a free town," also "of a petty town, provincial," from municipium "free town, city whose citizens have the privileges of Roman citizens but are governed by their own laws," from municeps "citizen, inhabitant of a free town." Second element is root of capere "assume, take" (see capable).

meanings.definitionCn: 开始（调查）；制定；创立；提起（诉讼）

etymology.story: 1510s, "purpose, design," from Latin institutum "an ordinance; a purpose; a custom; precedents; principal components," literally "thing set up," noun use of neuter past participle of instituere "to set up, put in place; arrange; found, establish" (see institute (v.)).

late 14c., "to undergo or suffer" (especially without breaking); also "to continue in existence," from Old French endurer (12c.) "make hard, harden; bear, tolerate; keep up, maintain," from Latin indurare "make hard," in Late Latin "harden (the heart) against," from in- (see in- (2)) + durare "to harden," from durus "hard," from PIE *dru-ro-, from root *deru- "be firm, solid, steadfast" (see true).
"bill, placard, thing posted," 1838, from post (v.1).
1660s, from Latinized form of Greek kriterion "means for judging, standard," from krites "judge," from PIE root *krei- (see crisis).

Old English þrote (implied in þrotbolla "the Adam's apple, larynx," literally "throat boll"), related to þrutian "to swell," from Proto-Germanic *thrut- (source also of Old High German drozza, German Drossel, Old Saxon strota, Middle Dutch strote, Dutch strot "throat"), of uncertain origin.
late 14c., essencia (respelled late 15c.

early 15c., "service book used by a priest," from Old French manuel "handbook" (also "plow-handle"), from Late Latin manuale "case or cover of a book, handbook," noun use of neuter of Latin manualis (see manual (adj.)).
1530s, "list of words with explanations," from Medieval Latin vocabularium "a list of words," from Latin vocabulum "word, name, noun," from vocare "to name, call" (see voice (n.)).
1530s, of armies, "to retreat," from Middle French retirer "to withdraw (something)," from re- "back" (see re-) + Old French tirer "to draw" (see tirade).

meanings.definitionCn: 样子, 外表, 面貌, (问题等的)方面

etymology.story: late 14c., an astrological term, "relative position of the planets as they appear from earth" (i.e., how they "look at" one another); later also "way of viewing things," from Latin aspectus "a seeing, looking at, sight, view, countenance, appearance," from past participle of aspicere "to look at," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + specere "to look" (see scope (n.1)).
1839, irregular formation from vegetable (n.) + -arian, as in agrarian, etc.

meanings.definitionCn: 侵略的，好斗的，有进取心的，敢作敢为的

etymology.story: 1791, from Latin aggress-, past participle stem of aggredi "to approach, attack" (see aggression) + -ive.

meanings.definitionCn: 圣人；圣徒；道德崇高的人
分期付款， 分期交付；一集； 一期

morphology.mnemonic: in + stall + 结果/过程/状态

meanings.definitionCn: 表示,意味;要紧,有重要性

etymology.story: late 13c., "be a sign of, indicate, mean," from Old French signifier (12c.), from Latin significare "to make signs, show by signs, point out, express; mean, signify; foreshadow, portend," from significus (adj.), from signum "sign" (see sign (n.)) + root of facere "to make" (see factitious).

meanings.definitionCn: 强迫,迫使;责成;(使)感激,施恩于
摇摆；摆动；秋千；音律；涨落
摇摆；转向；悬挂；大摇大摆地行走
旋转的；悬挂的；强节奏爵士音乐的
骚乱的，混乱的；狂暴的；吵闹的；激流的，湍流的

etymology.story: early 15c., "disorderly, tumultuous, unruly" (of persons), from Middle French turbulent (12c.), from Latin turbulentus "full of commotion, restless, disturbed, boisterous, stormy," figuratively "troubled, confused," from turba "turmoil, crowd" (see turbid).

mid-15c., from Old French invasion "invasion, attack, assaut" (12c.), from Late Latin invasionem (nominative invasio) "an attack, invasion," noun of action from past participle stem of Latin invadere "to go, come, or get into; enter violently, penetrate into as an enemy, assail, assault, make an attack on," from in- "in" (see in- (2)) + vadere "go, walk" (see vamoose)..

early 15c., "case which may be taken as a rule in similar cases," from Middle French precedent, noun use of an adjective, from Latin praecedentum (nominative praecedens), present participle of praecedere "go before" (see precede).

meanings.definitionCn: 吐痰；吐口水；发出劈啪声
搜索，侦察；侦察员；侦察机
登记；注册；记录；挂号邮寄；把…挂号；正式提出
登记；注册；记录；寄存器；登记簿
得不到休息的；不平静的；不安的， 坐立不安的

etymology.story: late 14c., from rest (n.1) + -less.

Old English fleotan "to float; drift; flow, run (as water); swim; sail (of a ship)," from Proto-Germanic *fleutan (source also of Old Frisian fliata, Old Saxon fliotan "to flow," Old High German fliozzan "to float, flow," German fliessen "to flow, run, trickle" (as water), Old Norse fliota "to float, flow"), from PIE root *pleu- "to flow,  swim" (see pluvial).

meanings.definitionCn: 传达,传送;交流;通讯,通话

etymology.story: 1520s, "to impart" (information, etc.), from Latin communicatus, past participle of communicare "impart, inform" (see communication).
Old English sadolian "to put a riding saddle on;" see saddle (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 农村的，乡下的；田园的，有乡村风味的

etymology.story: early 15c., from Old French rural (14c.), from Latin ruralis "of the countryside," from rus (genitive ruris) "open land, country," from PIE *reue- (1) "to open; space" (see room (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 酸,酸性物质a.酸的,酸味的；尖刻的
传动装置，[机] 变速器；传递；传送；播送

etymology.story: 1610s, "conveyance from one place to another," from Latin transmissionem (nominative transmissio) "a sending over or across, passage," noun of action from past participle stem of transmittere "send over or across" (see transmit).

morphology.mnemonic: make short + 行为/结果/集合

etymology.story: 1862, American English, from short + -age..

meanings.definitionCn: 烦人的，无聊的；令人讨厌的
可疑的；暧昧的；无把握的；半信半疑的

etymology.story: 1540s, from Latin dubiosus "doubtful," from dubium "doubt," neuter of dubius "vacillating, moving two ways, fluctuating;" figuratively "wavering in opinion, doubting, doubtful," from duo "two" (see two), with a sense of "of two minds, undecided between two things." Old English also used tweo "two" to mean "doubt." Compare doubt (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 直觉；直觉力；直觉的知识

root.meaning: to perceive directly without reasoning, know by immediate perception

morphology.mnemonic: to perceive directly without reasoning, know by immediate perception + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: mid-15c., intuicioun, "insight, direct or immediate cognition, spiritual perception," originally theological, from Late Latin intuitionem (nominative intuitio) "a looking at, consideration," noun of action from past participle stem of Latin intueri "look at, consider," from in- "at, on" (see in- (2)) + tueri "to look at, watch over" (see tutor (n.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 快的；迅速的；敏捷的；立刻的
有限的；[数]有穷的,限定的

etymology.story: early 15c., "limited in space or time, finite," from Latin finitum, past participle of finire "to limit, set bounds; come to an end" (see finish (v.)).

early 13c., "to free from faults, rectify," from Old French amender (12c.), from Latin emendare "to correct, free from fault," from ex- "out" (see ex-) + menda "fault, blemish," from PIE *mend- "physical defect, fault" (source also of Sanskrit minda "physical blemish," Old Irish mennar "stain, blemish," Welsh mann "sign, mark").
1650s, of diseases, etc., from Latin eruptus, past participle of erumpere "to break out, burst," from assimilated form of ex- "out" (see ex-) + rumpere "to break, rupture" (see rupture (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 感觉/感知

etymology.story: short for mutual fund, 1971; see mutual..

meanings.definitionCn: 剥落；设定命令行解释器的位置

etymology.story: Old English sciell, scill, Anglian scell "seashell, eggshell," related to Old English scealu "shell, husk," from Proto-Germanic *skaljo "piece cut off; shell; scale" (source also of West Frisian skyl "peel, rind," Middle Low German schelle "pod, rind, egg shell," Gothic skalja "tile"), with the shared notion of "covering that splits off," from PIE root *(s)kel- (1) "to cut, cleave" (source also of Old Church Slavonic skolika "shell," Russian skala "bark, rind;" see scale (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 药房；配药学，药剂学；制药业；一批备用药品

etymology.story: late 14c., "a medicine," from Old French farmacie "a purgative" (13c.), from Medieval Latin pharmacia, from Greek pharmakeia "use of drugs, medicines, potions, or spells; poisoning, witchcraft; remedy, cure," from pharmakeus (fem.
Old English þærfore; from there + fore, Old English and Middle English collateral form of for.

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + a different situation

etymology.story: late 15c., from Old French eschangier "exchange, barter" (Modern French échanger), from Vulgar Latin *excambiare (source of Italian scambiare); see exchange (n.).
1620s, from Latin haesitatus, past participle of haesitare "to stick fast; to hesitate; to stammer" (see hesitation).

1775, "portable firearm having a spirally grooved bore," used earlier of the grooves themselves (1751), noun use of rifled (pistol), 1680s, from verb meaning "to cut spiral grooves in" (a gun barrel); see rifle (v.2)..

meanings.definitionCn: 面团，膏；糊状物，[胶粘] 浆糊

etymology.story: "to come up to, to catch in pursuit," early 13c., from over- + take (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 人名地址录,(电话)号码簿

etymology.story: 1540s, "guide, book of rules," from Medieval Latin directorium, noun use of neuter of Latin directorius, from directus (see direct (v.)).
mid-15c., from Middle French monarque (14c.) or directly from Late Latin monarcha, from Greek monarkhes "one who rules alone" (see monarchy).

root.meaning: gang of workers employed after dark

morphology.mnemonic: 过度/超越 + gang of workers employed after dark

etymology.story: early 14c., from over- + night (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 表现；（机器等）运转；举止端正；（事物）起某种作用

etymology.story: early 15c., from be- intensive prefix + have in sense of "to have or bear (oneself) in a particular way, comport" (compare German sich behaben, French se porter).

Old English undon "to unfasten and open" (a window or door), "to unfasten by releasing from a fixed position; to cancel, discharge, abrogate, reverse what has been done, put back in a former condition; bring to ruin, destroy," from un- (2) "opposite of" + do (v.).
1960, acronym for "light amplification by stimulated emission of radiation," on pattern of maser (1955).
early 15c., from Old French misture and directly from Latin mixtura "a mixing," from mixtus (see mix (v.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 范围；余地；视野；眼界；导弹射程

etymology.story: "to view," 1807, from the source of scope (n.2).

morphology.mnemonic: 统治/首领 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1540s, coronell, from Middle French coronel (16c.), modified by dissimilation from Italian colonnella "commander of a column of soldiers at the head of a regiment," from compagna colonella "little column company," from Latin columna "pillar" (see column).

meanings.definitionCn: 录音机；记录器；记录员；八孔直笛

morphology.mnemonic: written account of some event + er

etymology.story: "chief legal officer of a city," early 15c., from Anglo-French recordour (early 14c.), Old French recordeor "witness; storyteller; minstrel," from Medieval Latin recordator, from Latin recordari "remember" (see record (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 识别,鉴别;(with)把…和…看成一样

morphology.mnemonic: 相同/识别 + 使…化/造成

etymology.story: 1640s, "regard as the same," from French identifier, from identité (see identity).
"to affix a label to," c.

meanings.definitionCn: 消息；差使；启示；预言；广告词
报信，报告；[通信] 报文

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French modifier (14c.), from Latin modificare "to limit, measure off, restrain," from modus "measure, manner" (see mode (n.1)) + root of facere "to make" (see factitious).

meanings.definitionCn: 克隆；无性系；无性繁殖；靠营养生殖而由母体分离繁殖的植物
保证，誓言；抵押；抵押品，典当物
保证，许诺；用……抵押；举杯祝……健康

etymology.story: 1680s, "a place where something tends to collect," originally figurative, from French réservoir "storehouse," from Old French reserver "to reserve" (see reserve (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 螃蟹；蟹肉；脾气乖戾的人；起重机
美的；美学的；审美的，具有审美趣味的

etymology.story: 1798, from German Ästhetisch or French esthétique, both from Greek aisthetikos "sensitive, perceptive," from aisthanesthai "to perceive (by the senses or by the mind), to feel," from PIE *awis-dh-yo-, from root *au- "to perceive" (see audience).
mid-13c., arsmetike, from Old French arsmetique (12c.), from Latin arithmetica, from Greek arithmetike (tekhne) "(the) counting (art)," fem.
"containing faults, errors, or defects," late 14c., from fault (n.) + -y (2).

meanings.definitionCn: 变化；[生物] 变异，变种

etymology.story: late 14c., "difference, divergence," from Old French variacion "variety, diversity" and directly from Latin variationem (nominative variatio) "a difference, variation, change," from past participle stem of variare "to change" (see vary).
mid-15c., from Latin eiectus "thrown out," past participle of eicere "throw out, cast out, thrust out; drive into exile, expel, drive away," from ex- "out" (see ex-) + -icere, comb.

morphology.mnemonic: 向下/移除/取消 + 做/制造

etymology.story: 1570s, from Latin defectus, past participle of deficere "to fail, desert" (see defect (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + in + bring about by unusual effort

meanings.definitionCn: 松弛的；疏忽的；不流畅的
随便的；非正式的；临时的；偶然的
便装；临时工人；待命士兵

etymology.story: late 14c., "subject to or produced by chance," from Middle French casuel (15c.), from Late Latin casualis "by chance," from Latin casus "chance, occasion, opportunity; accident, event" (see case (n.1)).

in wagering sense, found first in Shakespeare ("2 Henry IV," 1597), probably from earlier sense of "amount by which one thing exceeds or falls short of another" (1540s), from odd (q.v.), though the sense evolution is uncertain.

morphology.mnemonic: 向下/移除/取消 + 倾斜

etymology.story: early 14c., "deterioration, degeneration," from Old French declin (see decline (v.))..
1856, of mental positions; 1858 in a physical sense, from view + point (n.)..

mid-14c., "of the doctrines of the ancient Church," literally "universally accepted," from French catholique, from Church Latin catholicus "universal, general," from Greek katholikos, from phrase kath' holou "on the whole, in general," from kata "about" + genitive of holos "whole" (see safe (adj.)).
"tubular musical instrument sounded by blowing," Old English hwistle (see whistle (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 处理， 对待； 操纵 A；触， 摸

etymology.story: Middle English hondlen, handlen, "touch with the hands, hold in the hands, fondle, pet," also "to deal with, treat, manhandle," from Old English handlian "to touch or move with the hands," also "deal with, discuss;" formed from hand (n.), perhaps with a frequentative suffix, as fondle from fond.

meanings.definitionCn: 艰苦的，困难的；坚强的，不屈不挠的；坚韧的，牢固的；强壮的，结实的
剧本(原稿)；手稿，原稿；笔迹，手迹

morphology.mnemonic: 旋律/歌曲 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: late 13c., from Old French melodie "music, song, tune" (12c.), from Late Latin melodia, from Greek meloidia "a singing, a chanting, choral song, a tune for lyric poetry," from melos "song, part of song" (see melisma) + oide "song, ode" (see ode)..

meanings.definitionCn: 系统，体系；制度；方法，方式，步聚

etymology.story: 1610s, "the whole creation, the universe," from Late Latin systema "an arrangement, system," from Greek systema "organized whole, a whole compounded of parts," from stem of synistanai "to place together, organize, form in order," from syn- "together" (see syn-) + root of histanai "cause to stand" from PIE root *stā- "to stand" (see stet).

morphology.mnemonic: like + 以…方式/副词化 + hood

etymology.story: late 14c., "resemblance, similarity," from likely + -hood.

meanings.definitionCn: 部分的,不完全的;偏袒的,不公平的,偏爱的

morphology.mnemonic: to separate the hair + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: early 15c., "one-sided, biased," from Old French parcial (14c., Modern French partial), from Medieval Latin partialis "divisible, solitary, partial," from Latin pars (genitive partis) "part" (see part (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: to share a secret with + …的人/处于…状态

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 持有/保持

etymology.story: late 14c., "hold back, restrain;" c.

meanings.definitionCn: [地质] 世；新纪元；新时代；时间上的一点

etymology.story: 1610s, epocha, "point marking the start of a new period in time" (such as the founding of Rome, the birth of Christ, the Hegira), from Medieval Latin epocha, from Greek epokhe "stoppage, fixed point of time," from epekhein "to pause, take up a position," from epi "on" (see epi-) + ekhein "to hold" (see scheme (n.)).

1690s, originally medical, "forceful, vigorous, especially in effect on bowels," from Greek drastikos "effective, efficacious; active, violent," from drasteon "(thing) to be done," from dran "to do, act, perform." Sense of "extreme, severe" is first recorded 1808.

meanings.definitionCn: 熟练的；有技能的；需要技能的

meanings.definitionCn: 气泡，泡沫，泡状物；透明圆形罩，圆形顶

1901, "pertaining to electrons;" see electron + -ic; 1930 as "pertaining to electronics."
1580s, "church of a bishop," from phrase cathedral church (c.
1788, "that which results from something," originally Scottish, from out + come (v.).

etymology.story: 1550s, from Middle French polaire (16c.) or directly from Medieval Latin polaris "of or pertaining to the poles," from Latin polus "an end of an axis" (see pole (n.2)).

提供，偿还；使…变嫩；使…变柔软

etymology.story: "to offer formally," 1540s, from Middle French tendre "to offer, hold forth" (11c.), from Latin tendere "to stretch, extend" (see tenet).

1610s, "deductive reasoning," from Latin synthesis "collection, set, suit of clothes, composition (of a medication)," from Greek synthesis "composition, a putting together," from syntithenai "put together, combine," from syn- "together" (see syn-) + tithenai "put, place" (see theme).

morphology.mnemonic: 落于/发生 + …的人/处于…状态 + 与…有关的/形容词 + 以…方式/副词化

etymology.story: 1520s, "by the way, casually;" see incidental + -ly (2).

late 14c., from Anglo-French vapour, Old French vapor "moisture, vapor" (13c., Modern French vapeur) and directly from Latin vaporem (nominative vapor) "a warm exhalation, steam, heat," of unknown origin.

root.meaning: the recorded events of the past

morphology.mnemonic: the recorded events of the past + 具有…特征/名词化 + …人/与…有关

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Middle French historien (15c.), as if from Medieval Latin *historianus, from Latin historia "narrative of past events; narrative account, report" (see history).

meanings.definitionCn: 课程,(学校等的)全部课程

etymology.story: 1824, from Modern Latin transferred use of classical Latin curriculum "a running, course, career" (also "a fast chariot, racing car"), from currere (see current (adj.)).
1826, "an analogous thing," from French analogue, from Greek analogon (itself used in English from c.

meanings.definitionCn: 想,考虑,(尤指宗教上的)沉思,冥想

etymology.story: 1580s, "to ponder," back-formation from meditation, or else from Latin meditatus, past participle of meditari (see meditation).
also mean time, mid-14c., from mean (adj.2) "middle, intermediate" + time (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 回忆,想起,记起,忆起,记得

etymology.story: "remember, recover knowledge of," 1550s, from Latin recollectus, past participle of recolligere, literally "to collect again," from re- "again" (see re-) + colligere "gather" (see collect).

morphology.mnemonic: 中间/治疗 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: mid-15c., "to summon," from Old French citer "to summon" (14c.), from Latin citare "to summon, urge, call; put in sudden motion, call forward; rouse, excite," frequentative of ciere "to move, set in motion, stir, rouse, call, invite" from PIE root *keie- "to set in motion, to move to and fro" (source also of Sanskrit cyavate "stirs himself, goes;" Greek kinein "to move, set in motion; change, stir up," kinymai "move myself;" Gothic haitan "call, be called;" Old English hatan "command, call").

late 15c., as a hypothetical indivisible body, the building block of the universe, from Latin atomus (especially in Lucretius) "indivisible particle," from Greek atomos "uncut, unhewn; indivisible," from a- "not" + tomos "a cutting," from temnein "to cut" (see tome).
mid-13c., "mourn (someone); regret, bewail;" c.

meanings.definitionCn: 皇家的；盛大的；女王的；高贵的；第一流的
流出；发散， 散发；脱落； 摆脱

etymology.story:
"cast off," Old English sceadan, scadan "to divide, separate, part company; discriminate, decide; scatter abroad, cast about," strong verb (past tense scead, past participle sceadan), from Proto-Germanic *skaithan (source also of Old Saxon skethan, Old Frisian sketha, Middle Dutch sceiden, Dutch scheiden, Old High German sceidan, German scheiden "part, separate, distinguish," Gothic skaidan "separate"), from *skaith "divide, split."

According to Klein's sources, this probably is related to PIE root *skei- "to cut, separate, divide, part, split" (source also of Sanskrit chid-, Greek skhizein, Latin scindere "to split;" Lithuanian skedzu "I make thin, separate, divide;" Old Irish scian "knife;" Welsh chwydu "to break open").

1630s, "a cutting off (mentally), abstraction," from French précision (16c.) and directly from Latin praecisionem (nominative praecisio) "a cutting off," noun of action from past participle stem of praecidere (see precise).

meanings.definitionCn: 通信；符合， 一致；相当于， 对应

etymology.story: 1520s, "to be in agreement, to be in harmony with," from Middle French correspondre (14c.) or directly from Medieval Latin correspondere, from cor- (see com-) "together, with each other" + respondere "to answer" (see respond).
1640s, from Middle French elapser, from Latin elapsus, past participle of elabi "slip or glide away, escape," from ex- "out, away" (see ex-) + labi "to slip, glide" (see lapse (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: sy + 测量 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1560s, "relation of parts, proportion," from Middle French symmétrie (16c.) and directly from Latin symmetria, from Greek symmetria "agreement in dimensions, due proportion, arrangement," from symmetros "having a common measure, even, proportionate," from assimilated form of syn- "together" (see syn-) + metron "meter" (see meter (n.2)).
"to cut grain with a hook or sickle," Old English reopan, Mercian form of ripan "to reap," related to Old English ripe "ripe" (see ripe).

meanings.definitionCn: 自助餐；小卖部；打击；猛烈冲击
假定,假设,认为,揣测,滥用,擅自行动

etymology.story: late 14c., "to take upon oneself, to take liberty," also "to take for granted, presuppose," especially overconfidently, from Old French presumer (12c.) and directly from Latin praesumere "anticipate," in Late Latin, "assume" (see presumption).

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 填充/补充

etymology.story: mid-15c., "to invest with a quality," from Middle French qualifier (15c.) and directly from Medieval Latin qualificare "attribute a quality to; make of a certain quality," from Latin qualis "of what sort?," correlative pronominal adjective (see quality) + facere "to make" (see factitious).

meanings.definitionCn: 交界， 与…接壤； 接近 <

1590s, from Middle French adhérer (15c.) or directly from Latin adhaerare "to stick to" (see adherent (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 全景,全景画,全景摄影,全景照片[装置]

etymology.story: "one who lives at the same time as another," 1630s, originally cotemporary, from co- + temporary; modified by influence of contemporary (adj.).
1833 in the military sense; 1838 as "render capable of movement, bring into circulation," from French mobiliser, from mobile "movable" (see mobile).

meanings.definitionCn: 仿制的，模拟的，虚假的，不诚实的
[电子] 放大器，扩大器；扩音器

etymology.story: 1540s; agent noun from amplify.

etymology.story: "stylish, excellent," British slang, 1951 (popularized c.
1610s, "make easy, render less difficult," from French faciliter "to render easy," from stem of Latin facilis "easy" (see facile).

late 12c., from Old French rober "rob, steal, pillage, ransack, rape," from West Germanic *rauba "booty" (source also of Old High German roubon "to rob," roub "spoil, plunder;" Old English reafian, source of the reave in bereave), from Proto-Germanic *raubon "to rob," from PIE *reup-, *reub- "to snatch" (see rip (v.)).To rob the cradle is attested from 1864 in reference to drafting young men in the American Civil War; by 1949 in reference to seductions or romantic relationships with younger persons.

meanings.definitionCn: 远足， 郊游， 短途旅行

etymology.story: late 14c., "action of going out;" mid-15c., "act of putting out;" verbal noun from out (v.).

Old English rodd "a rod, pole," which is probably cognate with Old Norse rudda "club," from Proto-Germanic *rudd- "stick, club," from PIE *reudh- "to clear land."

As a long, tapering elastic pole for fishing, from mid-15c.

meanings.definitionCn: 哑的，无说话能力的；不说话的，无声音的

etymology.story:
Old English dumb "silent, unable to speak," from PIE *dheubh- "confusion, stupefaction, dizziness," from root *dheu- (1) "dust, mist, vapor, smoke," and related notions of "defective perception or wits."

The Old English, Old Saxon (dumb), Gothic (dumbs), and Old Norse (dumbr) forms of the word meant only "mute, speechless;" in Old High German (thumb) it meant both this and "stupid," and in Modern German this latter became the only sense.

"case for holding arrows," early 14c., from Anglo-French quiveir, Old French quivre, cuivre, probably of Germanic origin, from Proto-Germanic *kukur "container" (source also of Old High German kohhari, German Köcher, Old Saxon kokar, Old Frisian koker, Old English cocur "quiver"); "said to be from the language of the Huns" [Barnhart].

meanings.definitionCn: （表示厌恶、蔑视、烦恼等）胡说；糟了

etymology.story: 1620s (implied in predicted), "foretell, prophesy," a back formation from prediction or else from Latin praedicatus, past participle of praedicere "foretell, advise, give notice," from prae "before" (see pre-) + dicere "to say" (see diction).

垃圾场；仓库；无秩序地累积
普遍的,全体的,通用的;宇宙的,世界的

"domestic fowls," late 14c.

1827, from German Semester "half-year course in a university," from Latin semestris, in cursus semestris "course of six months," from semestris, semenstris "of six months, lasting six months, half-yearly, semi-annual," from sex "six" (see six) + mensis "month" (see moon (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 钝的；迟钝的；无趣的；呆滞的；阴暗的

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 抓住/理解

etymology.story: mid-14c., "to understand," from Latin comprehendere "to take together, to unite; include; seize" (of catching fire or the arrest of criminals); also "to comprehend, perceive" (to seize or take in the mind), from com- "completely" (see com-) + prehendere "to catch hold of, seize" (see prehensile).

meanings.definitionCn: 正确无误的；准确的，精确的

etymology.story: 1610s, "done with care," from Latin accuratus "prepared with care, exact, elaborate," past participle of accurare "take care of," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + curare "take care of" (see cure (n.1)).

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 走/让步 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: mid-15c., from Old French concession (14c.) or directly from Latin concessionem (nominative concessio) "an allowing, conceding," noun of action from past participle stem of concedere (see concede).

early 14c., "narrative dealing with a happening or an event," from Old French legende (12c., Modern French légende) and directly from Medieval Latin legenda "legend, story," especially lives of saints, which were formerly read at matins and in refectories of religious houses, literally "(things) to be read," on certain days in church, etc., from Latin legendus, neuter plural gerundive of legere "to read; to gather, pluck, select" (see lecture (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 相同/识别 + 使…化/造成 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1640s, "treating of a thing as the same as another; act of making or proving to be the same," from French identification, probably from identifier (see identify).

meanings.definitionCn: 使堵塞；挤进，使塞满；混杂；压碎

etymology.story: "to press tightly" (trans.), 1719; "to become wedged" (intrans.), 1706, of unknown origin, perhaps a variant of Middle English cham "to bite upon something; gnash the teeth" (late 14c.; see champ (v.)).

1844, from French protéine, coined 1838 by Dutch chemist Gerhard Johan Mulder (1802-1880), perhaps on suggestion of Berzelius, from Greek proteios "the first quality," from protos "first" (see proto-) + -ine (2).

"to imitate," 1630s, but the notion is implied earlier, as in the phrase play the ape (1570s), Middle English apeshipe "ape-like behavior, simulation" (mid-15c.); and the noun sense of "one who mimics" may date from early 13c.

morphology.mnemonic: 年 + 与…有关的/形容词
