etymology.story: mid-15c., "so as to exclude;" 1560s, "that excludes," from Medieval Latin exclusivus, from exclus-, past participle stem of excludere (see exclude).

"simple earthen or glass cylindrical vessel," early 15c., possibly from Old French jarre "liquid measure smaller than a barrel" (12c.), perhaps from Provençal jarra, from Arabic jarrah "earthen water vessel, ewer" (whence also Spanish jarra, Italian giarra), which is from Persian jarrah "a jar, earthen water-vessel.".
late 12c., from Old French poverte "poverty, misery, wretched condition" (Modern French pauvreté), from Latin paupertatem (nominative paupertas) "poverty," from pauper "poor" (see poor (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 全部节目；计算机指令系统；（美）某人或机器的全部技能

etymology.story: "a stock of plays, songs, etc., which a performer or company has studied and is ready to perform," 1847, from French répertoire, literally "index, list" (14c.), from Late Latin repertorium "inventory" (see repertory)..

meanings.definitionCn: 凭借，手段；度假胜地；常去之地
求助，诉诸；常去；采取某手段或方法

morphology.mnemonic: 见证/证明 + 使…化/造成

late 13c., present participle of lightnen "make bright," extended form of Old English lihting, from leht (see light (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 破旧的；卑鄙的；吝啬的；低劣的

山峰；底座；乘骑用马；攀，登；运载工具；底座

etymology.story: 1590s, "affecting the emotions, exciting the passions," from Middle French pathétique "moving, stirring, affecting" (16c.), from Late Latin patheticus, from Greek pathetikos "subject to feeling, sensitive, capable of emotion," from pathetos "liable to suffer," verbal adjective of pathein "to suffer" (see pathos).
1540s, originally "guinea fowl" (Numida meleagris), a bird imported from Madagascar via Turkey, and called guinea fowl when brought by Portuguese traders from West Africa.

meanings.definitionCn: 魔鬼；撒旦；家伙；恶棍；淘气鬼；冒失鬼
虐待，折磨；（用扯碎机）扯碎；（替作家，律师等）做助手；抹辣味料烤制或煎煮

etymology.story: Old English deofol "evil spirit, a devil, the devil, false god, diabolical person," from Late Latin diabolus (also the source of Italian diavolo, French diable, Spanish diablo; German Teufel is Old High German tiufal, from Latin via Gothic diabaulus).

late 14c., "one who has held an office or position before the present holder," from Old French predecesseor "forebear" and directly from Late Latin praedecessorem (nominative praedecessor), from Latin prae "before" (see pre-) + decessor "retiring official," from decess-, past participle stem of decedere "go away," also "die" (see decease (n.)).
mid-15c., acqueren, from Old French aquerre "acquire, gain, earn, procure," from Vulgar Latin *acquaerere, corresponding to Latin acquirere "to seek in addition to" (see acquisition).

meanings.definitionCn: 手推车；（美）有轨电车（等于trolley car）；（英）无轨电车（等于trolleybus）；空中吊运车

etymology.story: 1823, in Suffolk dialect, "a cart," especially one with wheels flanged for running on a track (1858), probably from troll (v.) in the sense of "to roll." Sense transferred to "device used to transmit electric current to streetcars, consisting of a trolley wheel which makes contact with the overhead wires" (1888), then "streetcar drawing power by a trolley" (1891), which probably is short for trolley-car, attested from 1889..
"self-propelled motor vehicle," 1895, from French automobile, short for véhicule automobile (see automobile (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 富饶的，肥沃的；能生育的

etymology.story: mid-15c., "bearing or producing abundantly," from Middle French fertil (15c.) and directly from Latin fertilis "bearing in abundance, fruitful, productive," from ferre "to bear" (see infer).
late 14c., from Latin notionem (nominative notio) "concept, conception, idea, notice," noun of action from past participle stem of noscere "come to know" (see know).
1856, "something that pulls," from Modern Latin tractor "that which draws," agent noun from past participle stem of Latin trahere "to pull, draw" (see tract (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 优美,文雅;恩惠,恩泽;宽限,缓刑;感恩祷告

etymology.story: late 14c., "imprisoned, enslaved," from Latin captivus "caught, taken prisoner," from captus, past participle of capere "to take, hold, seize" (see capable).

meanings.definitionCn: 红利，股息；回报，效益；被除数

morphology.mnemonic: watershed, separation between river valleys + end

etymology.story: late 15c., from Middle French dividende "a number divided by another," from Latin dividendum "thing to be divided," neuter gerundive of dividere (see divide).

meanings.definitionCn: 恶习；缺点；[机] 老虎钳；卖淫

etymology.story: (also steem, extyme), mid-14c., "account, value, worth," from French estime, from estimer (see esteem (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 持有人；所有人；固定器；（台、架等）支持物

etymology.story: "fold or crease in the extenal body," late 14c.; in cloth or clothing from early 15c., probably from wrinkle (v.).

morphology.mnemonic: own + er + 身份/状态/技能

etymology.story: 1580s, from owner + -ship.
early 15c., from Latin tumor "swelling, condition of being swollen, a tumor," from tumere "to swell" (see tumid)..

root.meaning: clamping tool with two jaws closed by a screw

morphology.mnemonic: 超/在…之上 + clamping tool with two jaws closed by a screw

etymology.story: late 15c., "to look over" (implied in supervising), from Medieval Latin supervisus, past participle of supervidere "oversee, inspect," from Latin super "over" (see super-) + videre "see" (see vision).

meanings.definitionCn: 寓言式的；极为巨大的；极好的

morphology.mnemonic: that which is told, + 具有…性质的

etymology.story: early 15c., "mythical, legendary," from Latin fabulosus "celebrated in fable;" also "rich in myths," from fabula "story, tale" (see fable (n.)).

1520s, from Late Latin dilemma, from Greek dilemma "double proposition," a technical term in rhetoric, from di- "two" + lemma "premise, anything received or taken," from root of lambanein "to take" (see analemma).

root.meaning: attract and occupy the attention of

morphology.mnemonic: attract and occupy the attention of + 结果/过程/状态

etymology.story: 1620s, "formal promise," from engage + -ment.

meanings.definitionCn: 小地毯；毛皮地毯；男子假发

etymology.story: 1550s, "coarse fabric," of Scandinavian origin; compare Norwegian dialectal rugga "coarse coverlet," from Old Norse rogg "shaggy tuft," from Proto-Germanic *rawwa-, perhaps related to rag (n.) and rough (adj.).

meanings.definitionCn: 组织， 机构； 体制， 结构
偶然的；临时的；特殊场合的

etymology.story: late 14c., "occurring now and then," from occasion (n.) + -al (1).
"frequent," 1610s, past participle adjective from repeat (v.).
early 14c., "a quarrel, dispute, disagreement," from Old French debat; see debate (v.).
1928, from space (n.) + craft (n.)..

1540s, from Latin extinguere/exstinguere "quench, put out (what is burning), wipe out, obliterate," from ex- "out" (see ex-) + stinguere "quench," apparently an evolved sense from PIE *steig- "to prick, stick, pierce" (see stick (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 易受攻击的，易受…的攻击；易受伤害的；有弱点的
视野;风景;观察;见解;照片
说教；讲道；鼓吹；传道；反复灌输

etymology.story: at first in late Old English predician, a loan word from Church Latin; reborrowed 12c.

meanings.definitionCn: 喷射，喷嘴；喷气式飞机；黑玉

etymology.story: 1819, back-formation from donation.

meanings.definitionCn: 有帮助的， 有益的， 有用的

etymology.story: late 14c., from help (n.) + -ful.

meanings.definitionCn: 禁忌， 忌讳；禁止 A

etymology.story: also tabu, 1777 (in Cook's "A Voyage to the Pacific Ocean"), "consecrated, inviolable, forbidden, unclean or cursed," explained in some English sources as being from Tongan (Polynesian language of the island of Tonga) ta-bu "sacred," from ta "mark" + bu "especially." But this may be folk etymology, as linguists in the Pacific have reconstructed an irreducable Proto-Polynesian *tapu, from Proto-Oceanic *tabu "sacred, forbidden" (compare Hawaiian kapu "taboo, prohibition, sacred, holy, consecrated;" Tahitian tapu "restriction, sacred;" Maori tapu "be under ritual restriction, prohibited").

meanings.definitionCn: 浇铸， 造型； 塑造， 形成
大胆的，英勇的；黑体的；厚颜无耻的；险峻的

etymology.story: Old English beald (West Saxon), bald (Anglian) "bold, brave, confident, strong," from Proto-Germanic *balthaz (source also of Old High German bald "bold, swift," in names such as Archibald, Leopold, Theobald; Gothic balþei "boldness;" Old Norse ballr "frightful, dangerous"), perhaps from PIE *bhol-to- suffixed form of *bhel- (2) "to blow, swell" (see bole).

meanings.definitionCn: 有意义的;重大的,重要的

morphology.mnemonic: 标记 + 使…化/造成 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: 1570s, "having a meaning," from Latin significantem (nominative significans, present participle of significare "make known, indicate" (see signify).

Old English bledan "to let blood," in Middle English and after, "to let blood from surgically;" also "to emit blood," from Proto-Germanic *blodjan "emit blood" (source also of Old Norse blæða, German bluten), from *bhlo-to- "swell, gush, spurt" (see blood (n.)).
1590s, "to make a catalogue;" see catalogue (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 部分重叠；部分的同时发生

morphology.mnemonic: 不/进入 + 站立/设置 + ity

etymology.story: early 15c., from Old French instabilité "inconstancy" (15c.) or directly from Latin instabilitatem (nominative instabilitas) "unsteadiness," from instabilis "unsteady, not firm, inconstant, fickle," from in- "not, opposite of" (see in- (1)) + stabilis (see stable (adj.))..
1610s, from past participle stem of Medieval Latin assassinare (see assassin).
Old English crawian "make a loud noise like a crow"  (see crow (n.)); sense of "exult in triumph" is 1520s, perhaps in part because the English crow is a carrion-eater.
mid-15c., "neighborly conduct, friendliness," from neighbor (n.) + -hood.

Old English murnan "to mourn, bemoan, long after," also "be anxious about, be careful" (class III strong verb; past tense mearn, past participle murnen), from Proto-Germanic *murnan "to remember sorrowfully" (source also of Old Saxon mornon, Old High German mornen, Gothic maurnan "to mourn," Old Norse morna "to pine away"), probably from PIE root *(s)mer- "to remember" (see memory); or, if the Old Norse sense is the base one, from *mer- "to die, wither."

meanings.definitionCn: 螺栓，螺钉；闪电，雷电；门闩；弩箭;（布的）一匹，一卷
筛选；囫囵吞下；（把门、窗等）闩上；突然说出，脱口说出
突然地；像箭似地；直立地

etymology.story: 1715, from Gaelic uisge beatha "whisky," literally "water of life," from Old Irish uisce "water" (from PIE *ud-skio-, from root *wed- (1) "water, wet;" see water (n.1)) + bethu "life" (from PIE *gwi-wo-tut-, suffixed form of *gwi-wo-, from root *gweie- (1) "to live;" see bio-).

meanings.definitionCn: 新奇；新奇的事物；新颖小巧而廉价的物品

etymology.story: late 14c., "quality of being new," also "a new manner or fashion, an innovation; something new or unusual," from Old French noveleté "newness, innovation, change; news, new fashion" (Modern French nouveauté), from novel "new" (see novel (adj.)).
mid-15c., earliest use was for bees and wasps, probably echoic of humming.

meanings.definitionCn: 缺乏，不存在；缺席，不在；缺席的时间

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French absence (14c.), from Latin absentia, noun of state from absentem (nominative absens), present participle of abesse "be away from, be absent," from ab- "away" (see ab-) + esse "to be" (see essence)..

mid-15c., from Middle French aboliss-, present participle stem of abolir "to abolish" (15c.), from Latin abolere "destroy, cause to die out, retard the growth of," perhaps from ab- "from" (see ab-) + adolere "to grow," from PIE *ol-eye-, causative of root *al- (3) "to grow, nourish" (see old), and perhaps formed as an antonym to adolere.

meanings.definitionCn: 引起的注意(或兴趣等),吸引；引起；激起

etymology.story: early 15c., from Latin attractus, past participle of attrahere "to draw, pull; to attract," from ad- "to" (see ad-) + trahere "draw" (see tract (n.1)).

morphology.mnemonic: 向下/移除/取消 + 写 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French description (12c.) and directly from Latin descriptionem (nominative descriptio) "representation, description, copy," noun of action from past participle stem of describere "write down, transcribe, copy, sketch," from de- "down" (see de-) + scribere "write" (see script (n.))..

morphology.mnemonic: to supervise for publication + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: early 15c., "version, translation, a form of a literary work;" 1550s, "act of publishing," from French édition or directly from Latin editionem (nominative editio) "a bringing forth, producing," also "a statement, account," from past participle stem of edere "bring forth, produce," from ex- "out" (see ex-) + -dere, comb.
1640s, from caution + -ous.

morphology.mnemonic: 不/分离 + seem, have a certain appearance

etymology.story: early 15c., disaperen, from dis- "do the opposite of" + appear.

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 走/让步 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: 1640s, "act of receding, a going back," from French récession "a going backward, a withdrawing," and directly from Latin  recessionem (nominative recessio) "a going back," noun of action from past participle stem of recedere (see recede).

meanings.definitionCn: [数] 负的；消极的；否定的；阴性的

morphology.mnemonic: 否定 + 使…/具有…的 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: late 14c., "a prohibition; absence, nonexistence; opposite," from Old French negatif and directly from Latin negativus (see negative (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 同盟国；伙伴；同盟者；助手
从此， 今后； 因此

etymology.story: "(away) from here," late 13c., hennes, with adverbial genitive -s + Old English heonan "away, hence," from West Germanic *hin- (source also of Old Saxon hinan, Old High German hinnan, German hinnen), from PIE *ki-, variant of root *ko- "this," the stem of the demonstrative pronoun (see here).

meanings.definitionCn: 博学的；有学问的；学术上的

etymology.story: "having knowledge gained by study," mid-14c., past participle adjective from learn (v.) in its former transitive sense.

meanings.definitionCn: 债券；结合；约定；粘合剂；纽带
势头；[物] 动量；动力；冲力

etymology.story: 1690s, scientific use in mechanics, "quantity of motion of a moving body," from Latin momentum "movement, moving power" (see moment).

meanings.definitionCn: 把……作为目标；规定……的指标；瞄准某物

etymology.story: 1610s, a back-formation from obstruction or else from Latin obstructus, past participle of obstruere "to block, to stop up" (see obstruction).

root.meaning: book of information on local sites

morphology.mnemonic: book of information on local sites + ance

etymology.story: 1530s, "the process of directing conduct," hybrid from guide (v.) + -ance; replacing 15c.
1767, from stair + way (n.)..

meanings.definitionCn: 微妙的；精美的，雅致的；柔和的；易碎的；纤弱的；清淡可口的

etymology.story: late 14c., "self-indulgent, loving ease; delightful; sensitive, easily hurt; feeble," from Latin delicatus "alluring, delightful, dainty," also "addicted to pleasure, luxurious, effeminate;" which is of uncertain origin; related by folk etymology (and perhaps genuinely) to deliciae "a pet," and delicere "to allure, entice" (see delicious).

meanings.definitionCn: 想象的；奇特的；昂贵的；精选的

etymology.story: "take a liking to," 1540s, a contraction of fantasien "to fantasize (about)," from fantasy (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 饰以薄纱；用化妆纸揩去

etymology.story: mid-14c., "band or belt of rich material," from Old French tissu "a ribbon, headband, belt of woven material" (c.

morphology.mnemonic: to undo, untie, unfasten + 使变成/变得

etymology.story: late 14c., losnen, later lousen (early 15c.), from loose (v.) + -en (1).
mid-15c., "to rent out property, grant possession and enjoyment of in exchange for a consideration paid," from Old French renter "pay dues to," or from rent (n.1).
1530s, "the giving of thanks," from thanks (n.) + present participle of give (v.).

1540s, probably from -cy + Latin proficientem (nominative proficiens), present participle of proficere "accomplish, make progress; be useful, do good; have success, profit," from pro- "forward" (see pro-) + root of facere "to make, do" (see factitious)..

meanings.definitionCn: 投，抛；计算；浇铸；投射（光、影、视线等）
投掷，抛；铸件，[古生] 铸型；演员阵容；脱落物

etymology.story: 1922, American English, perhaps from high(way) + jacker "one who holds up" (agent noun from jack (v.)).

root.meaning: free or at liberty in a general way (without particulars)

meanings.definitionCn: 切下；把…分成部分；将…切成薄片

etymology.story: late 14c., "woman of royal or noble birth; daughter or wife of a ruler or prince; female ruler," a native formation, or else from Old French princesse, fem.

meanings.definitionCn: 举起,提拔,振奋,提升...的职位

etymology.story: late 15c., "to raise above the usual position," from Latin elevatus, past participle of elevare "lift up, raise," figuratively, "to lighten, alleviate," from ex- "out" (see ex-) + levare "lighten, raise," from levis "light" in weight (see lever).
As a verb, from 1791, from designate (adj.) or else a back-formation from designation.
1530s, a back-formation from humiliation or else from Late Latin humiliatus.

meanings.definitionCn: 用帆布覆盖，用帆布装备

etymology.story: "sturdy cloth made from hemp or flax," mid-14c., from Anglo-French canevaz, Old North French canevach, Old French chanevaz, literally "made of hemp, hempen," noun use of Vulgar Latin adjective *cannapaceus "made of hemp," from Latin cannabis, from Greek kannabis "hemp," a Scythian or Thracian word (see cannabis).

meanings.definitionCn: 条款，项目；一则；一件商品（或物品）

etymology.story: late 14c., originally an adverb, "moreover, in addition," from Latin item (adv.) "likewise, just so, moreover," probably from ita "thus," id "it" (see id) + adverbial ending -tem (compare idem "the same").
1911, from German Gen, coined 1905 by Danish scientist Wilhelm Ludvig Johannsen (1857-1927), from Greek genea "generation, race," from PIE root *gene- (see genus).
small wild plant with purplish-blue flowers, c.

meanings.definitionCn: 空的,中空的;空洞的,空虚的
树干；躯干；象鼻；汽车车尾的行李箱
节约的；挽救的；补偿的；保留的

etymology.story: late 14c., from safe (adj.); see save (prep.)..

early 14c., "to gather, assemble" (transitive and intransitive), from Old French relier "assemble, put together; fasten, attach, rally, oblige," from Latin religare "fasten, bind fast," from re-, intensive prefix (see re-), + ligare "to bind" (see ligament).

meanings.definitionCn: 滑动；滑落；不知不觉陷入

etymology.story: early 14c., stile, "writing instrument, pen, stylus; piece of written discourse, a narrative, treatise;" also "characteristic rhetorical mode of an author, manner or mode of expression," and "way of life, manner, behavior, conduct," from Old French stile, estile "style, fashion, manner; a stake, pale," from Latin stilus "stake, instrument for writing, manner of writing, mode of expression," perhaps from the same source as stick (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + 走/让步 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French excessif "excessive, oppressive," from Latin excess-, past participle stem of excedere "to depart, go beyond" (see exceed).
1520s, "to render (a hunting animal) eager for prey by rewarding it with flesh from a kill," with figurative extensions, from flesh (n.).

1776, "dense growth of trees and other tangled vegetation," such as that of the swampy regions at the base of the Himalayas in India, from Hindi jangal "desert, forest, wasteland, uncultivated ground," from Sanskrit jangala-s "arid, sparsely grown with trees," a word of unknown origin.
early 15c., from Middle French asauter, assauter, from Vulgar Latin *assaltare (see assault (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 樱桃；樱桃树；如樱桃的鲜红色；处女膜，处女
女性的；妇女（似）的；阴性的；娇柔的

etymology.story: mid-14c., "of the female sex," from Old French femenin (12c.) "feminine, female; with feminine qualities, effeminate," from Latin femininus "feminine" (in the grammatical sense at first), from femina "woman, female," literally "she who suckles," from root of felare "to suck, suckle" (see fecund).

meanings.definitionCn: 提高；增高；加强；使更显著

morphology.mnemonic: distance from bottom to top + 使变成/变得

etymology.story: mid-15c., heightenen, transitive, "to exalt, to honor or raise to high position," from height + -en (1).

[车辆] 车辆；工具；交通工具；运载工具；传播媒介；媒介物

etymology.story: 1610s, "a medium through which a drug or medicine is administered," also "any means of conveying or transmitting," from French véhicule (16c.), from Latin vehiculum "means of transport, vehicle, carriage, conveyance," from vehere "to bear, carry, convey," from PIE *wegh- "to go, transport in a vehicle" (source also of Old English wegan "to carry;" Old Norse vegr, Old High German weg "way;" Middle Dutch wagen "wagon;" see wagon).

meanings.definitionCn: 阴冷的；荒凉的，无遮蔽的；黯淡的，无希望的；冷酷的；单调的
垫子；起缓解作用之物；（猪等的）臀肉；银行储蓄
给…安上垫子；把…安置在垫子上；缓和…的冲击

etymology.story: late 13c., from Anglo-French glorious, Old French glorieus "glorious, blessed" (12c., Modern French glorieux), from Latin gloriosus "full of glory, famous," from gloria (see glory (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 建筑，建筑学；建筑式样或风格，建筑物

morphology.mnemonic: 统治/首领 + 行为/过程/结果

etymology.story: 1560s, from Middle French architecture, from Latin architectura, from architectus "architect" (see architect)..

meanings.definitionCn: 压力；强调；紧张；重要性；重读
强调；使紧张；加压力于；用重音读
信赖；信心；受信赖的人或物
多余的；不必要的；奢侈的

morphology.mnemonic: 超/在…之上 + 流动 + 具有…性质的

meanings.definitionCn: 管理者；售票员；领队，指挥；导体

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 引导 + …者/…器

etymology.story: 1520s, "one who leads or guides," from Middle French conductour (14c., Old French conduitor), from Latin conductor "one who hires, contractor," in Late Latin "a carrier," from conductus, past participle of conducere (see conduce).

1825, from French acrobate (14c.), "tightrope-walker," and directly from Greek akrobates "rope dancer, gymnastic performer," which is related to akrobatos "going on tip-toe, climbing up high," from akros "topmost, at the point end" (see acrid) + stem of bainein "walk, go" (see come)..

meanings.definitionCn: 混乱,杂乱;骚乱;失调,疾病

root.meaning: to give orders for or to

morphology.mnemonic: 不/分离 + to give orders for or to

etymology.story: 1520s, "make heavy, burden down," from past participle adjective aggravate "burdened; threatened" (late 15c.), from Latin aggravatus, past participle of aggravare "to render more troublesome," literally "to make heavy" (see aggravation).

meanings.definitionCn: 奶制品；乳牛；制酪场；乳品店；牛奶及乳品业
乳品的；牛奶的；牛奶制的；产乳的

etymology.story: late 13c., "building for making butter and cheese; dairy farm," formed with Anglo-French -erie affixed to Middle English daie (in daie maid "dairymaid"), from Old English dæge "kneader of bread, housekeeper, female servant" (see dey (n.1)).

morphology.mnemonic: 穿越/转变 + 站立/设置 + …者/…器

etymology.story: small electronic device, 1948, from transfer + resistor, so called because it transfers an electrical current across a resistor.

meanings.definitionCn: 坚持；不屈不挠；固执己见（在辩论中）

meanings.definitionCn: 成熟的；充分考虑的；到期的；成年人的

etymology.story: mid-15c., "ripe," also "careful, well-considered," from Latin maturus "ripe, timely, early" (see mature (v.))..

morphology.mnemonic: 船 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: early 14c., "fleet of ships," especially for purposes of war, from Old French navie "fleet; ship," from Latin navigia, plural of navigium "vessel, boat," from navis "ship" (see naval).
late 15c., from Middle French allevier or directly from Late Latin alleviatus, past participle of alleviare "to lighten," from Latin ad- "to" (see ad-) + levis "light" in weight (see lever).

meanings.definitionCn: 影响；势力；感化；有影响的人或事

etymology.story: late 13c., "inclination, disposition, will, desire," from Old French talent (12c.), from Medieval Latin talenta, plural of talentum "inclination, leaning, will, desire" (11c.), in classical Latin "balance, weight; sum of money," from Greek talanton "a balance, pair of scales," hence "weight, definite weight, anything weighed," and in later times sum of money," from PIE *tele- "to lift, support, weigh," "with derivatives referring to measured weights and thence money and payment" [Watkins]; see extol.
1789, from percent + -age.
"to communicate in search of mutual agreement," 1590s, back-formation from negotiation, or else from Latin negotiatus, past participle of negotiari.
1570s, from Italian violino, diminutive of viola (see viola).

meanings.definitionCn: 包，包裹；套装软件，[计] 程序包

morphology.mnemonic: 和平 + 行为/结果/集合

etymology.story: early 15c., from Latinized form of Greek krisis "turning point in a disease" (used as such by Hippocrates and Galen), literally "judgment, result of a trial, selection," from krinein "to separate, decide, judge," from PIE root *krei- "to sieve, discriminate, distinguish" (source also of Greek krinesthai "to explain;" Old English hriddel "sieve;" Latin cribrum "sieve," crimen "judgment, crime," cernere (past participle cretus) "to sift, separate;" Old Irish criathar, Old Welsh cruitr "sieve;" Middle Irish crich "border, boundary").

mid-15c., "bud, sprout;" 1640s, "rudiment of a new organism in an existing one," from Middle French germe "germ (of egg); bud, seed, fruit; offering," from Latin germen (genitive germinis) "spring, offshoot; sprout, bud," which is of uncertain origin, perhaps from PIE root *gen-, *gene- "to beget, bear" (see german (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 粉碎；打碎；破坏；破掉；使散开

etymology.story: early 14c., transitive, probably a variant of Middle English scateren (see scatter (v.)).

late 14c., "action of inducing (someone) to believe (something); argument to persuade, inducement," from Old French persuasion (14c.) and directly from Latin persuasionem (nominative persuasio) "a convincing, persuading," noun of action from past participle stem of persuadere "persuade, convince," from per- "thoroughly, strongly" (see per) + suadere "to urge, persuade," from PIE *swad- "sweet, pleasant" (see sweet (adj.)).

Old English meos "moss," related to mos "bog," from Proto-Germanic *musan (source also of Old High German mios, Danish mos, German Moos), also in part from Old Norse mosi "moss, bog," and Medieval Latin mossa "moss," from the same Germanic source, from PIE *meus- "damp," with derivatives referring to swamps and swamp vegetation (source also of Latin muscus "moss," Lithuanian musai "mold, mildew," Old Church Slavonic muchu "moss").
early 13c., "footprint," from foot (n.) + step (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 老兵；老手；富有经验的人；老运动员
短语, 习语, 措辞, 乐句
措词, 将(乐曲)分成乐句

etymology.story: "to put into a phrase," 1560s; see phrase (n.).

meanings.definitionCn: 爱，喜爱；爱慕之情；感情；疾病，不适

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 做/制造 + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: early 13c., "an emotion of the mind, passion, lust as opposed to reason," from Old French afection (12c.) "emotion, inclination, disposition; love, attraction, enthusiasm," from Latin affectionem (nominative affectio) "a relation, disposition; a temporary state; a frame, constitution," noun of state from past participle stem of afficere "to do something to, act on" (see affect (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 转向,转移;牵制;解闷;娱乐

morphology.mnemonic: different in character or quality. + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: early 15c., "diverse condition;" c.

meanings.definitionCn: 省略， 删去；遗漏， 忽略 <

etymology.story: early 15c., from Latin omittere "let go, let fall," figuratively "lay aside, disregard," from assimilated form of ob (here perhaps intensive) + mittere "let go, send" (see mission).
also centimetre, 1801, from French centimètre (18c.), coined from Latin centum "hundred" (see hundred) + French mètre (see meter (n.2))..

late 14c., from Old French erroneus and directly from Latin erroneus "vagrant, wandering" (in Late Latin "erroneous"), from erronem (nominative erro) "a wanderer, vagabond," from past participle stem of errare "to wander; to err" (see err).
late 14c., from Old French limitacion and directly from Latin limitationem (nominative limitatio), noun of action from past participle stem of limitare (see limit (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 土地/地球 + 使…化/造成

etymology.story: 1570s, from Latin terrificare "to frighten, make afraid," from terrificus "causing terror" (see terrific).

meanings.definitionCn: 慢跑；轻推；蹒跚行进；使颠簸

etymology.story: early 15c., "to inform, to give assurance," from Anglo-French acerteiner, Old French acertener "to assure, certify" (13c.), from a "to" (see ad-) + certain "sure, assured" (see certain).

meanings.definitionCn: 手推车， 小车；〈英〉无轨电车； 〈美〉有轨电车
插入物；管芯；镶块；[机械]刀片
禁止；不准；不允许；〈正式〉严禁

etymology.story: Old English forbeodan "forbid, prohibit" (past tense forbead, plural forbudon, past participle forboden), from for- "against" + beodan "to command" (see bid (v.)).

morphology.mnemonic: in + 呼吸 + 状态/行为/过程

meanings.definitionCn: 拒绝,抵制,丢弃,排斥,退掉
绝对的；透明的；峻峭的；纯粹的

etymology.story: 1620s, "deviate from course" (of a ship), of obscure origin, perhaps from Dutch scheren "to move aside, withdraw, depart," originally "to separate" (see shear (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 奇迹,令人惊奇的人(或事)

etymology.story: mid-12c., "a wondrous work of God," from Old French miracle (11c.) "miracle, story of a miracle, miracle play," from Latin miraculum "object of wonder" (in Church Latin, "marvelous event caused by God"), from mirari "to wonder at, marvel, be astonished," figuratively "to regard, esteem," from mirus "wonderful, astonishing, amazing," earlier *smeiros, from PIE *smei- "to smile, laugh" (source also of Sanskrit smerah "smiling," Greek meidan "to smile," Old Church Slavonic smejo "to laugh;" see smile (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 恭维；称赞；问候；致意；道贺

etymology.story: 1610s, from French complimenter, from compliment (see compliment (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 模仿/复制 + 使…/具有…的 + 状态/行为/过程

root.meaning: to set or place in position

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + to set or place in position

etymology.story: early 14c., "to fulfill, carry out," from Old French compli, past participle of complir "to accomplish, fulfill, carry out," from Vulgar Latin *complire, from Latin complere "to fill up" (see complete (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 原始的；最初的；独创的；新颖的

morphology.mnemonic: 起源/开始 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: "original text," late 14c., from Medieval Latin originale (see original (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 尼龙，[纺] 聚酰胺纤维；尼龙袜

etymology.story: 1938, coined, according to DuPont, from random generic syllable nyl- + -on, common ending in fiber names (compare rayon), said to be ultimately from cotton.

morphology.mnemonic: sand spout, dust storm + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1630s, from Late Latin deviatus, past participle of deviare "to turn out of the way" (see deviant).

meanings.definitionCn: 相簿；唱片集；集邮簿；签名纪念册

etymology.story: 1650s, from Latin album "white color, whiteness," neuter of albus "white" (see alb).

meanings.definitionCn: 分配，指派；(指定的)作业，任务

morphology.mnemonic: to fix, settle, determine, appoint + 结果/过程/状态

etymology.story: late 14c., "order, request, directive," from Old French assignement "(legal) assignment (of dower, etc.)," from Late Latin assignamentum, noun of action from Latin assignare (see assign).
past tense of see; from Old English plural sawon..

meanings.definitionCn: 封建制度的；领地的；世仇的

etymology.story: 1610s, "pertaining to feuds," estates of land granted by a superior on condition of services to be rendered to the grantor, from Medieval Latin feudalis, from feudum "feudal estate, land granted to be held as a benefice," of Germanic origin (cognates: Gothic faihu "property," Old High German fihu "cattle;" see fee).
Old English cyningdom; see king (n.) + -dom.
"doll moved by strings or wires" (later applied to puppets in glove form), 1530s, later form of Middle English popet "doll" (c.
Old English lætra "slower," comparative of læt "late" (see late (adj.)).

early 15c., from Middle French equivalent and directly from Late Latin aequivalentem (nominative aequivalens) "equivalent," present participle of aequivalere "be equivalent," from Latin aequus "equal" (see equal (adj.)) + valere "be well, be worth" (see valiant).

meanings.definitionCn: 楔子；楔形物；导致分裂的东西
使疏远， 使格格不入；使不友好； 离间A

etymology.story: 1540s, "make estranged" (in feelings or affections), from Latin alienatus, past participle of alienare "to make another's, estrange," from alienus "of or belonging to another person or place," from alius "(an)other" (see alias (adv.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 瘟疫；灾祸；麻烦；讨厌的人

etymology.story: late 15c., from Middle Dutch plaghen, from plaghe (n.) "plague" (see plague (n.)).

mid-14c., from Old French perpetuel "without end" (12c.) and directly from Latin perpetualis "universal," in Medieval Latin "permanent," from perpetuus "continuous, universal," from perpetis, genitive of Old Latin perpes "lasting," probably from per- "through" + root of petere "to seek, go to, aim at" (see petition (n.)).

Old English bannan "to summon, command, proclaim," from Proto-Germanic *bannan "proclaim, command, forbid" (source also of Old High German bannan "to command or forbid under threat of punishment," German bannen "banish, expel, curse"), originally "to speak publicly," from PIE root *bha- (2) "to speak" (source also of Old Irish bann "law," Armenian ban "word;" see fame (n.)).

1590s, "bewitch, enchant," from Middle French fasciner (14c.), from Latin fascinatus, past participle of fascinare "bewitch, enchant, fascinate," from fascinus "a charm, enchantment, spell, witchcraft," which is of uncertain origin.

meanings.definitionCn: 外交的；外交上的；老练的

etymology.story: 1711, "pertaining to documents, texts, charters," from Medieval Latin diplomaticus, from diplomat-, stem of diploma (see diploma).

mid-13c., "allow to occur or continue, permit, tolerate, fail to prevent or suppress," also "to be made to undergo, endure, be subjected to" (pain, death, punishment, judgment, grief), from Anglo-French suffrir, Old French sofrir "bear, endure, resist; permit, tolerate, allow" (Modern French souffrir), from Vulgar Latin *sufferire, variant of Latin sufferre "to bear, undergo, endure, carry or put under," from sub "up, under" (see sub-) + ferre "to carry" (see infer).

morphology.mnemonic: 向前/支持 + 投/掷 + …者/…器

etymology.story: 1590s, "one who forms a project," agent noun in Latin form from project (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 邻居； 四邻； 街道， 住宅区
汇票；草稿；选派；（尤指房间、烟囱、炉子等供暖系统中的）（小股）气流
初步画出或（写出）的；（设计、草图、提纲或版本）正在起草中的，草拟的；以草稿形式的；草图的
复杂的；合成的， 综合的

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 折叠/交织

etymology.story: late 14c., "state or quality of being humid," from Old French humidité, umidité "dampness, humidity," from Latin humiditatem (nominative humiditas), from humidus "moist, wet" (see humid).

meanings.definitionCn: 流动的；流畅的；不固定的

etymology.story: "small, unbound treatise," late 14c., from Anglo-Latin panfletus, popular short form of "Pamphilus, seu de Amore" ("Pamphilus, or about Love"), a short 12c.

morphology.mnemonic: after + to parry, to fend off

etymology.story: late 14c., from Anglo-French arterie, Old French artaire (13c.; Modern French artère), and directly from Latin arteria, from Greek arteria "windpipe," also "an artery," as distinct from a vein; related to aeirein "to raise" (see aorta).

morphology.mnemonic: 不足/在…之下 + high regard + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1812, "to estimate at too low an amount," from under + estimate (v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 塑料的， 塑胶的；可塑的， 塑性的

etymology.story: late 14c., "a large mass;" mid-15c., "spherical solid body, a sphere," from Middle French globe (14c.) and directly from Latin globus "round mass, sphere, ball" (also, of men, "a throng, crowd, body, mass"), which is related to gleba "clod, lump of soil" (see glebe) and perhaps glomus "a ball, ball of yarn," but de Vaan says the last two probably are non-IE loan-words.

meanings.definitionCn: 瞬变现象；过往旅客；候鸟
打听， 询问；调查， 研究

etymology.story: alternative spelling of inquiry.
early 15c., from Latin applausus, past participle of applaudere "approve by clapping hands" (see applaud)..

meanings.definitionCn: 院长；系主任；教务长；主持牧师

etymology.story: early 14c., from Old French deien (12c., Modern French doyen), from Late Latin decanus "head of a group of 10 monks in a monastery," from earlier secular meaning "commander of 10 soldiers" (which was extended to civil administrators in the late empire), from Greek dekanos, from deka "ten" (see ten).
mid-15c., "improper practice," from Old French abus (14c.), from Latin abusus (see abuse (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 隔离,孤立;使绝缘,使绝热

etymology.story: 1530s, "make into an island," from Late Latin insulatus "made like an island," from insula "island" (see isle).

late 14c., "mental state," from Latin noun use of affectus "furnished, supplied, endowed," figuratively "disposed, constituted, inclined," past participle of afficere "to do; treat, use, manage, handle; act on; have influence on, do something to," a verb of broad meaning, from ad- "to" (see ad-) + facere (past participle factus) "to make, do" (see factitious).

Old English deaf "deaf," also "empty, barren," specialized from Proto-Germanic *daubaz (source also of Old Saxon dof, Old Norse daufr, Old Frisian daf, Dutch doof "deaf," German taub, Gothic daufs "deaf, insensate"), from PIE dheubh-, which was used to form words meaning "confusion, stupefaction, dizziness" (source also of Greek typhlos "blind," typhein "to make smoke;" Old English dumb "unable to speak;" Old High German tumb).

meanings.definitionCn: 排水；下水道，排水管；消耗
娱乐；消遣；使发笑；使愉快

etymology.story: late 15c., "to divert the attention, beguile, delude," from Middle French amuser "divert, cause to muse," from a "at, to" (but here probably a causal prefix) + muser "ponder, stare fixedly" (see muse (v.)).
"to set or place apart, to detach so as to make alone," by 1786, a back-formation from isolated (q.v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 在内的， 里面的； 内心的 <

morphology.mnemonic: 完整 + to parry, to fend off

etymology.story: Old English inweard "inmost; sincere; internal, intrinsic; deep," from Proto-Germanic *inwarth "inward" (source also of Old Norse innanverðr, Old High German inwart, Middle Dutch inwaert), from root of Old English inne "in" (see in (adv.)) + -weard (see -ward).
"condition of being responsible," 1787, from responsible + -ity.
alternative form of inquire, according to OED mainly used in sense of "to ask a question."

meanings.definitionCn: 公民的；民间的；文职的；有礼貌的；根据民法的

etymology.story: late 14c., "relating to civil law or life; pertaining to the internal affairs of a state," from Old French civil "civil, relating to civil law" (13c.) and directly from Latin civilis "relating to a citizen, relating to public life, befitting a citizen," hence by extension "popular, affable, courteous;" alternative adjectival derivation of civis "townsman" (see city).

"following in time, later," mid-15c., from Middle French subsequent (14c.) and directly from Latin subsequentem (nominative subsequens), present participle of subsequi "come after in time, follow closely," figuratively "imitate, conform to," from sub "closely, up to" (see sub-) + sequi "follow" (see suit (n.)).

1650s, "to point out," back-formation from indication (q.v.) or else from Latin indicatus, past participle of indicare "to point out, show, indicate." Especially "to give suggestion of, be reason for inferring" (1706).

meanings.definitionCn: 包含，容纳；容忍，抑制；可被...除尽

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 持有/保持

etymology.story: late 13c., from Old French contein-, stem of contenir, from Latin continere (transitive) "to hold together, enclose," from com- "together" (see com-) + tenere "to hold" (see tenet).

meanings.definitionCn: 垃圾；轿，担架；一窝（动物的幼崽）；凌乱
乱丢；给…垫褥草；把…弄得乱七八糟

etymology.story: Old English siwian "to stitch, sew, mend, patch, knit together," earlier siowian, from Proto-Germanic *siwjanan (source also of Old Norse syja, Swedish sy, Danish sye, Old Frisian sia, Old High German siuwan, Gothic siujan "to sew"), from PIE root *syu- "to bind, sew" (source also of Sanskrit sivyati "sews," sutram "thread, string;" Greek hymen "thin skin, membrane," hymnos "song;" Latin suere "to sew, sew together;" Old Church Slavonic šijo "to sew," šivu "seam;" Lettish siuviu, siuti "to sew," siuvikis "tailor;" Russian švec "tailor").
1560s, from Middle French ovalle "oval figure," from Medieval Latin ovalis (see oval (adj.))..

meanings.definitionCn: 纵队，列；专栏；圆柱，柱形物

etymology.story: mid-15c., "vertical division of a page," also "a pillar, post," from Old French colombe (12c., Modern French colonne "column, pillar"), from Latin columna "pillar," collateral form of columen "top, summit," from PIE root *kel- (4) "to project, be prominent" (see hill).

morphology.mnemonic: 生命/生物 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1680s, probably from Latin biographia, from Late Greek biographia "description of life," from Greek bio- "life" (see bio-) + graphia "record, account" (see -graphy).
1610s, "fact or manner of proceeding," from French procédure "manner of proceeding" (c.

meanings.definitionCn: 样品,实例,标本,抽样检查
使…完整；使…成整体；求…的积分；表示…的总和

etymology.story: 1630s, "to render (something) whole, bring together the parts of," from Latin integratus, past participle of integrare "make whole," from integer "whole, complete," figuratively, "untainted, upright," literally "untouched," from in- "not" (see in- (1)) + root of tangere "to touch," from PIE root *tag- "to touch, handle" (see tangent (adj.)).
"beyond the violet end of the visible spectrum," 1840, from ultra- + violet.
1831, "official signature or endorsement on a passport," from French visa, from Modern Latin charta visa "verified paper," literally "paper that has been seen," from fem.

1550s, "nearness in place," from Middle French vicinité and directly from Latin vicinitas "of or pertaining to neighbors or a neighborhood," as a noun, "neighborhood, nearness, proximity," from vicinus (adj.) "of the neighborhood, near, neighboring," as a noun "the neighborhood, a neighbor," from vicus "group of houses, village," related to the -wick, -wich in English place names, from PIE *weik- (1) "clan, social unit above the household" (see villa).
representing the sound of the nightingale, 1530s..

late 14c., spydyr, from earlier spiþre, spiþur, spiþer (14c.), from Old English spiðra, from Proto-Germanic *spin-thron- (cognate with Danish spinder), literally "the spinner," from *spen-wo- "to spin" (see spin (v.)) + formative or agential *-thro.

meanings.definitionCn: 领先(于),在(…之前);优先,先于

etymology.story: early 15c., "lead the way; occur before," from Middle French preceder and directly from Latin praecedere "to go before," from prae "before" (see pre-) + cedere "to go" (see cede).

"covering over part of a ship," mid-15c., perhaps a shortening of Middle Low German verdeck (or a related North Sea Germanic word), a nautical word, from ver- "fore" + decken "to cover, put under roof," from Proto-Germanic *thakjan (related to thatch, q.v.).
1530s, originally "playing at bowls," verbal noun from bowl (v.).
early 15c., "relating to the body humors," a native formation from humor (n.), or else from Middle French humoreux "damp," from Old French humor.

morphology.mnemonic: day日 + …的人/处于…状态 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1816, from Medieval Latin differentiatus, past participle of differentiare, from Latin differentia (see difference).
late 14c., "relief from misfortune," see discharge (v.).

morphology.mnemonic: 向前/支持 + 看/观察 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: 1580s, from obsolete French prospectif and directly from Medieval Latin prospectivus "affording a prospect; pertaining to a prospect," from Latin prospect-, past participle stem of prospicere (see prospect (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 减轻；粘贴；涂以灰泥；敷以膏药；使平服
成长的； 成熟的； 成人的

etymology.story: "mature," late 14c., past participle adjective from grow up.

meanings.definitionCn: 首都的,主要都市的,大城市

etymology.story: 1540s, "belonging to an ecclesiastical metropolis," from Late Latin metropolitanus, from Greek metropolites "resident of a city," from metropolis (see metropolitan (n.)).

"refuse, filth," 1580s; earlier "giblets, refuse of a fowl, waste parts of an animal (head, feet, etc.) used for human food" (early 15c., in early use also gabage, garbish, garbidge ), of unknown origin; OED says probably from Anglo-French "like many other words found in early cookery books." In its sense of "waste material, refuse" it has been influenced by and partly confused with garble (q.v.) in its older sense of "remove refuse material from spices;" Middle English had the derived noun garbelage but it is attested only as the action of removing the refuse, not the material itself.

mid-14c., "deserving of or exciting suspicion," from Old French sospecious, from Latin suspiciosus, suspitiosus "exciting suspicion, causing mistrust," also "full of suspicion, ready to suspect," from stem of suspicere (see suspicion).

late 14c., "find fault, lament," from stem of Old French complaindre "to lament" (12c.), from Vulgar Latin *complangere, originally "to beat the breast," from Latin com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + plangere "to strike, beat the breast" (see plague (n.)).

"passionate ardor in pursuit of an objective or course of action," late 14c., from Old French zel (Modern French zèle) and directly from Late Latin zelus "zeal, emulation" (source also of Italian zelo, Spanish celo), a Church word, from Greek zelos "ardor, eager rivalry, emulation," "a noble passion" [Liddell & Scott], but also "jealousy;" prom PIE *ya- "to seek, request, desire." From mid-15c.

meanings.definitionCn: 焦虑；渴望；挂念；令人焦虑的事

etymology.story: 1520s, from Latin anxietatem (nominative anxietas) "anguish, anxiety, solicitude," noun of quality from anxius (see anxious).
1520s, back-formation from renovation, or else from Latin renovatus, past participle of renovare "renew, restore" (see renovation).
late 14c., ajusten, "to correct, remedy;" reborrowed by c.

meanings.definitionCn: 朝气蓬勃的,精力旺盛的

etymology.story: late 13c., dette, from Old French dete, from Latin debitum "thing owed," neuter past participle of debere "to owe," originally, "keep something away from someone," from de- "away" (see de-) + habere "to have" (see habit (n.)).
Old English deman "to judge, condemn, think, compute," from root of dom (see doom (n.)).

mid-14c., from Old French compellir, from Latin compellere "to drive together, drive to one place" (of cattle), "to force or compel" (of persons), from com- "together" (see com-) + pellere "to drive" (see pulse (n.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 有本领的,有能力的;(of)可以…的,能…的

etymology.story: 1560s, from Middle French capable or directly from Late Latin capabilis "receptive; able to grasp or hold," used by theologians, from Latin capax "able to hold much, broad, wide, roomy;" also "receptive, fit for;" adjectival form of capere "to grasp, lay hold, take, catch; undertake; take in, hold; be large enough for; comprehend," from PIE *kap- "to grasp" (source also of Sanskrit kapati "two handfuls;" Greek kaptein "to swallow, gulp down;" Lettish kampiu "seize;" Old Irish cacht "servant-girl," literally "captive;" Welsh caeth "captive, slave;" Gothic haban "have, hold;" Old English hæft "handle," habban "to have, hold," Modern English have).

morphology.mnemonic: 向下/移除/取消 + device for pressing cloth

etymology.story: early 14c., "put down by force," from Old French depresser, from Late Latin depressare, frequentative of Latin deprimere "press down," from de- "down" (see de-) + premere "to press" (see press (v.1)).

meanings.definitionCn: 游行,夸耀n.检阅,阅兵式v.使列队行进
谜语；粗筛；谜一般的人、东西、事情等

etymology.story: 1580s, "account of a gathering or party," from Latin symposium "drinking party, symposium," from Greek symposion "drinking party, convivial gathering of the educated" (related to sympotes "drinking companion"), from assimilated form of syn- "together" (see syn-) + posis "a drinking," from a stem of Aeolic ponen "to drink," cognate with Latin potare "to drink" (see potion).
mid-13c., awaiten, "to wait for," from Old North French awaitier (Old French agaitier) "to lie in wait for, watch, observe," from a- "to" (see ad-) + waitier "to watch" (see wait (v.)).
late 14c., from Anglo-French clyent (c.

Old English synn "moral wrongdoing, injury, mischief, enmity, feud, guilt, crime, offense against God, misdeed," from Proto-Germanic *sun(d)jo- "sin" (source also of Old Saxon sundia, Old Frisian sende, Middle Dutch sonde, Dutch zonde, German Sünde "sin, transgression, trespass, offense," extended forms), probably ultimately "it is true," i.e.

混在一起；使成块状；忍耐；笨重地移动

etymology.story: mid-14c., originally of religion, from French conversion, from Latin conversionem (nominative conversatio), noun of action from past participle stem of convertere (see convert (v.)).

Old English ofen "furnace, oven," from Proto-Germanic *ukhnaz (source also of Old Frisian, Dutch oven, Old High German ovan, German Ofen, Old Norse ofn, Old Swedish oghn, Gothic auhns), from PIE *aukw- "cooking pot" (source also of Sanskrit ukhah "pot, cooking pot," Latin aulla "pot," Greek ipnos), originally, perhaps, "something hollowed out." The oven-bird (1825) so called because of the shape of its nest.

etymology.story: also eye-brow, early 15c., from eye (n.) + brow (q.v.; Old English eagbræw meant "eyelid")..

meanings.definitionCn: （游泳或划船的）划；中风；（打、击等的）一下；冲程；（成功的）举动；尝试；轻抚
（用笔等）画；轻抚；轻挪；敲击；划尾桨；划掉；（打字时）击打键盘

etymology.story: "pass the hand gently over," Old English stracian "to stroke," related to strican "pass over lightly," from Proto-Germanic *straik-, from PIE root *streig- "to stroke, rub, press" (see strigil).

1550s (earlier in Latin form piramis, late 14c.), from French pyramide (Old French piramide "obelisk, stela," 12c.), from Latin pyramides, plural of pyramis "one of the pyramids of Egypt," from Greek pyramis (plural pyramides) "a pyramid," apparently an alteration of Egyptian pimar "pyramid." Financial sense is from 1911.

late 13c., "kingdom," from Old French reaume, probably from roiaume "kingdom," altered (by influence of Latin regalis "regal") from Gallo-Roman *regiminem, accusative form of Latin regimen "system of government, rule" (see regimen).

probably an early Middle English mutual influence or confusion of two old words: The sense in bid farewell is from Old English biddan "to ask, entreat, beg, pray, beseech; order" (class V strong verb, past tense bæd, past participle beden), from Proto-Germanic *bidjan "to pray, entreat" (source also of German bitten "to ask," attested in Old High German from 8c., also Old Saxon bidjan, Old Frisian bidda, Old Norse biðja, Gothic bidjan).

meanings.definitionCn: 提示；促进；激起；（给演员）提白
提示；付款期限；DOS命令：改变DOS系统提示符的风格

etymology.story: 1590s, from French corridor (16c.), from Italian corridore "a gallery," literally "a runner," from correre "to run," from Latin currere (see current (adj.)).

etymology.story: Old English fullfyllan "fill up" (a room, a ship, etc.), "make full; take the place of (something)," from full (adj.), here perhaps with a sense of "completion" + fyllan (see fill (v.), which is ultimately from the same root).

meanings.definitionCn: 先进的；高级的；晚期的；年老的
前进；增加；上涨（advance的过去式和过去分词形式）

"receptacle," Old English binne "basket, manger, crib," probably from Gaulish, from Old Celtic *benna, akin to Welsh benn "a cart," especially one with a woven wicker body.

etymology.story: early 15c., "of or pertaining to machines," from mechanic (adj.) + -al (1); of persons or human actions, "resembling machines, automatic" it is from c.

1550s, "condition of being greater, superiority," from Middle French majorité (16c.), from Medieval Latin majoritatem (nominative majoritas) "majority," from Latin maior "greater" (see major (adj.)).

morphology.mnemonic: faith + 充满…的 + 以…方式/副词化

etymology.story: 1580s, from young + -ster.

立体的；立体声的；立体感觉的

etymology.story: 1823 as a shortening of stereotype (n.); 1876 as a shortening of stereoscope; 1954 as a shortening of stereophonic (adj.); the noun meaning "stereophonic record or tape player" is recorded from 1964..

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French doctrine (12c.) "teaching, doctrine," and directly from Latin doctrina "teaching, body of teachings, learning," from doctor "teacher" (see doctor (n.)) + -ina, fem.

morphology.mnemonic: 判断/法律 + 结果/过程/状态

etymology.story: late 15c., from Middle French paragraphe "division of text" (13c., Old French paragrafe), from Medieval Latin paragraphus "sign for start of a new section of discourse" (the sign looked something like a stylized letter -P-), from Greek paragraphos "short stroke in the margin marking a break in sense," also "a passage so marked," literally "anything written beside," from paragraphein "write by the side," from para- "beside" (see para- (1)) + graphein "to write" (see -graphy)..

Old English cristal "clear ice, clear mineral," from Old French cristal (12c., Modern French crystal), from Latin crystallus "crystal, ice," from Greek krystallos, from kryos "frost," from PIE root *kru(s)- "hard, hard outer surface" (see crust).
late 14c., "persecution," also "action of pursuit," from Anglo-French purseute, from Old French porsuite "a search, pursuit" (14c., Modern French poursuite), from porsivre (see pursue).

late 12c., "person who speaks for God; one who foretells, inspired preacher," from Old French prophete, profete "prophet, soothsayer" (11c., Modern French prophète) and directly from Latin propheta, from Greek prophetes (Doric prophatas) "an interpreter, spokesman," especially of the gods, "inspired preacher or teacher," from pro- "before" (see pro-) + root of phanai "to speak," from PIE *bha- (2) "speak" (see fame (n.)).
early 14c., from reuthe "pity, compassion" (see ruth) + -less.

morphology.mnemonic: in + quire + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: early 15c., enquery, "a judicial examination of facts to determine truth;" mid-15c.

"space between intersecting lines," late 14c., from Old French angle "angle, corner," and directly from Latin angulus "an angle, corner," a diminutive form from PIE root *ang-/*ank- "to bend" (source also of Greek ankylos "bent, crooked," Latin ang(u)ere "to compress in a bend, fold, strangle;" Old Church Slavonic aglu "corner;" Lithuanian anka "loop;" Sanskrit ankah "hook, bent," angam "limb;" Old English ancleo "ankle;" Old High German ango "hook").

morphology.mnemonic: in + 移动/迁移 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: "one who immigrates," 1792, American English, perhaps based on French immigrant, from Latin immigrantem (nominative immigrans), present participle of immigrare "to remove, go into, move in" (see immigrate).

late 14c., "statement, belief, or practice handed down from generation to generation," especially "belief or practice based on Mosaic law," from Old French tradicion "transmission, presentation, handing over" (late 13c.) and directly from Latin traditionem (nominative traditio) "delivery, surrender, a handing down, a giving up," noun of action from past participle stem of tradere "deliver, hand over," from trans- "over" (see trans-) + dare "to give" (see date (n.1)).
mid-15c., from Middle French urgent "pressing, impelling" (14c.), from Latin urgentem (nominative urgens), present participle of urgere "to press hard, urge" (see urge (v.)).

root.meaning: set of correlated principles, facts, ideas, etc.

morphology.mnemonic: set of correlated principles, facts, ideas, etc. + ic

etymology.story: 1670s, "pertaining to a system," from French systématique or directly from Late Latin systematicus, from Greek systematikos "combined in a whole," from systema (genitive systematos); see system.
late 14c., "a board or table to place cups and like objects," from cup (n.) + board (n.1).
mid-15c., from the phrase in like wise "in the same manner" (mid-15c.), from like (adj.) + wise (n.)..

early 14c., "meat from the back and sides of a pig" (originally either fresh or cured, but especially cured), from Old French bacon, from Proto-Germanic *bakkon "back meat" (source also of Old High German bahho, Old Dutch baken "bacon").

meanings.definitionCn: 已婚的,夫妇的;(to)与…结婚的

etymology.story: "formally wedded," late 14c., from past participle of marry (v.)..

meanings.definitionCn: 啜泣，呜咽；（风等）发出呜咽声

etymology.story: Old English mislædan "to mislead," common Germanic compound (compare Middle Low German, Middle Dutch misleiden, Old High German misseleiten, German missleiten, Danish mislede); see mis- (1) + lead (v.).

凌辱，强奸；对…施暴行；激起愤怒

morphology.mnemonic: 分开/二 + device that breaks an electrical circuit

etymology.story: early 15c., from Latin diffusus (see diffuse (v.))..

分离；使分离；劈开；离开；分解

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French contagieus (Modern French contagieux), from Late Latin contagiosus, from Latin contagio (see contact (n.))..

etymology.story: late 14c., "the act of making publicly known," from Old French publicacion (14c.) and directly from Latin publicationem (nominative publicatio) "a making public," noun of action from past participle stem of publicare "make public," from publicus (see public (adj.)).

1660s, from French, short for vin de Champagne 
"wine made in Champagne," former province in northwest France, literally "open country" (see campaign (n.)).

Old English sugu, su "female of the swine," from Proto-Germanic *su- (source also of Old Saxon, Old High German su, German Sau, Dutch zeug, Old Norse syr), from PIE root *su- (source also of Sanskrit sukarah "wild boar, swine;" Avestan hu "wild boar;" Greek hys "swine;" Latin sus "swine," swinus "pertaining to swine;" Old Church Slavonic svinija "swine;" Lettish sivens "young pig;" Welsh hucc, Irish suig "swine; Old Irish socc "snout, plowshare"), possibly imitative of pig noise, a notion reinforced by the fact that Sanskrit sukharah means "maker of (the sound) 'su.' " Related to swine.
Old English cneowlian "to kneel, fall on the knees," from cneow (see knee (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 保护区；禁猎地；加工成的食品

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之前/预先 + 保持/服务

etymology.story: late 14c., from Old French establiss-, present participle stem of establir "cause to stand still, establish, stipulate, set up, erect, build" (12c., Modern French établir), from Latin stabilire "make stable," from stabilis "stable" (see stable (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 相关的；切题的；中肯的；有重大关系的；有意义的，目的明确的

morphology.mnemonic: 提起/相关 + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: "pertinent to the matter at hand," 1550s, from Middle French relevant "depending upon," originally "helpful," from Medieval Latin relevantem (nominative relevans), from stem of Latin relevare "to lessen, lighten" (see relieve).

1690s, as a term in logic, "deductive," from French synthétique (17c.) and directly from Modern Latin syntheticus, from Greek synthetikos "skilled in putting together, constructive," from synthetos "put together, constructed, compounded," past participle of syntithenai "to put together" (see synthesis).

meanings.definitionCn: 宣告，公布；声明；表明；赞扬

etymology.story: late 14c., proclamen, from Latin proclamare "cry or call out," from pro- "forth" (see pro-) + clamare "to cry out" (see claim (v.)).

糖果（等于sweets）；冰糖（等于sugar candy，rock candy）；毒品
新潮的（服饰）；甜言蜜语的

morphology.mnemonic: 在…之前/预先 + domin + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: 1570s, from Middle French prédominant (14c.), from Medieval Latin *praedominantem (nominative praedominans), present participle of *praedominare, from Latin prae- "before" (see pre-) + dominari "to rule" (see dominate).

1580s, "medical prescription," from Middle French récipé (15c.), from Latin recipe "take!," second person imperative singular of recipere "to take" (see receive); word written by physicians at the head of prescriptions.

meanings.definitionCn: 正派的；得体的；相当好的

etymology.story: 1530s, "proper to one's station or rank," also "tasteful," from Middle French décent, or directly from Latin decentem (nominative decens) "becoming, seemly, fitting, proper," present participle of decere "to be fitting or suitable," from PIE *deke-, from root *dek- "to take, accept, to receive, greet, be suitable" (source also of Greek dokein "to appear, seem, think," dekhesthai "to accept;" Sanskrit daśasyati "shows honor, is gracious," dacati "makes offerings, bestows;" Latin docere "to teach," decus "grace, ornament").

Old English bugan "to bend, to bow down, to bend the body in condescension," also "to turn back" (class II strong verb; past tense beag, past participle bogen), from Proto-Germanic *bugon (source also of Dutch buigen, Middle Low German bugen, Old High German biogan, German biegen, Gothic biugan "to bend," Old Norse boginn "bent"), from *beugen, from PIE root *bheug- (3) "to bend," with derivatives referring to bent, pliable, or curved objects (source also of Sanskrit bhujati "bends, thrusts aside;" Old High German boug, Old English beag "a ring").

morphology.mnemonic: run swiftly + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: 1862, from race (n.2) + -ial.

1590s, "slender riding whip," probably from a Flemish or Low German word akin to Middle Dutch swijch "bough, twig," or swutsche, variant of Low German zwukse "long thin stick, switch," from Germanic *swih- (source also of Old High German zwec "wooden peg," German Zweck "aim, design," originally "peg as a target," Zwick "wooden peg"), perhaps connected with PIE root *swei- (2) "to swing, bend, to turn."

The meaning "device for changing the direction of something or making or breaking a connection" is first recorded 1797.

meanings.definitionCn: 助动词；辅助者，辅助物；附属机构

未开化的人；粗鲁的人；残暴成性的人
高度地；非常；非常赞许地

1706, "a cabinet; an ornamental base structure," from French console "a bracket" (16c.), which is of uncertain origin, possibly from Middle French consolateur, literally "one who consoles," word used for carved human figures supporting cornices, shelves or rails in choir stalls.

morphology.mnemonic: 开始/初始 + iat + 有…倾向的

also clock-wise, "in the direction of the hands of a clock," 1879, from clock (n.1) + wise (n.)..

承认；答谢；报偿；告知已收到

1510s, "act of overthrowing," from over- + throw (n.)..
1650s, "demigoddess," from Latin heroine, heroina (plural heroinae) "a female hero, a demigoddess" (such as Medea), from Greek heroine, fem.

meanings.definitionCn: 在内地；向内地；向内陆；在内陆

Old English stol "seat for one person," from Proto-Germanic *stolaz (source also of Old Frisian stol, Old Norse stoll, Old High German stuol, German Stuhl "seat," Gothic stols "high seat, throne"), from PIE *sta-lo-, locative of root *stā- "to stand" (source also of Lithuanian pa-stolas "stand," Old Church Slavonic stolu "stool;" see stet).

meanings.definitionCn: 特产； 特色菜；专业； 专长

etymology.story: "raised open platform at the entrance of a house," 1755, American and Canadian, from Dutch stoep "flight of steps, doorstep, threshold," from Middle Dutch, from Proto-Germanic *stap- "step" (see step (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 一致的，协调的；(话语等)条理清楚的

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + _here + …的人/处于…状态

etymology.story: 1550s, from Middle French cohérent (16c.), from Latin cohaerentem (nominative cohaerens), present participle of cohaerere "cohere," from com- "together" (see co-)  + haerere "to stick" (see hesitation)..

mid-15c., "communication to and fro," ("In early use exclusively with reference to trade" [OED]), from Old French entrecors "exchange, commerce, communication" (12c., Modern French entrecours), from Late Latin intercursus "a running between, intervention," in Medieval Latin "intercommunication," from intercursus, past participle of intercurrere "to run between, intervene, mediate," from Latin inter- "between" (see inter-) + currere "to run" (see current (adj.)).
"place of residence," mid-14c., verbal noun from dwell (v.)..

meanings.definitionCn: 插曲；一段情节；插话；有趣的事件

etymology.story: 1670s, "commentary between two choric songs in a Greek tragedy," also "an incidental narrative or digression within a story, poem, etc.," from French épisode or directly from Greek epeisodion "an episode," literally "an addition," noun use of neuter of epeisodios "coming in besides," from epi "in addition" (see epi-) + eisodos "a coming in, entrance" (from eis "into" + hodos "way").

early 14c., "to estimate the worth of, to appraise," from Old French ponderer "to weigh, poise" (14c., Modern French pondérer) and directly from Latin ponderare "ponder, consider, reflect," literally "to weigh," from pondus (genitive ponderis) "weight" (see pound (n.1)).

morphology.mnemonic: 对/向 + 投/掷 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: mid-15c., "do an injustice to, dishonor," probably a back-formation from injury, or else from Old French injuriier "to damage; offend," from Latin iniuriari "do an injury," from iniuria.

also gild, early 13c., yilde (spelling later influenced by Old Norse gildi "guild, brotherhood"), a semantic fusion of Old English gegield "guild, brotherhood," and gield "service, offering; payment, tribute; compensation," from Proto-Germanic *geldjam "payment, contribution" (source also of Old Frisian geld "money," Old Saxon geld "payment, sacrifice, reward," Old High German gelt "payment, tribute;" see yield (v.)).

1660s, "thrust forth, push out," from Latin exertus/exsertus, past participle of exerere/exserere "thrust out, put forth," from ex- "out" (see ex-) + serere "attach, join; arrange, line up" (see series).

meanings.definitionCn: 丑闻；流言蜚语；诽谤；公愤

etymology.story: 1580s, "discredit caused by irreligious conduct," from Middle French scandale (12c.), from Late Latin scandalum "cause for offense, stumbling block, temptation," from Greek skandalon "a trap or snare laid for an enemy," in New Testament, metaphorically as "a stumbling block, offense;" originally "trap with a springing device," from PIE *skand- "to leap, climb" (see scan (v.); also see slander (n.), which is another form of the same word).

meanings.definitionCn: 混合成的，综合成的n.合成物，复合材料

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 放置 + ite

etymology.story: "situated in the rear, pertaining to the rear, toward the back," late 14c., probably from an unrecorded Old English adjective from hinder (adv.) "behind, back, afterward," but treated as a comparative of hind (adj.).
late 14c., from strength + -en (1).

meanings.definitionCn: 气味；嗅觉；痕迹；察觉能力
闻到；发觉；使充满…的气味；循着遗臭追踪
损伤， 损害， 毁坏

morphology.mnemonic: to injure, hurt, harm + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: late 14c., "harm, damage, loss; a specific injury," from Anglo-French injurie "wrongful action" (Old French injure, 13c.), from Latin iniuria "wrong, an injustice, insult, unlawful violence, assault, damage, harm," noun use of fem.

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + 拥有/保持

etymology.story: 1620s, "document or object produced as evidence in court," from Latin exhibitum, noun use of neuter past participle of exhibere "to display, show" (see exhibition).

meanings.definitionCn: 蘑菇，伞菌；蘑菇形物体；暴发户
蘑菇的；蘑菇形的；迅速生长的
迅速增加；采蘑菇；迅速生长
否定，否认；拒绝给予；拒绝…的要求

etymology.story: early 14c., from Old French denoiir "deny, repudiate, withhold," from Latin denegare "to deny, reject, refuse" (source of Italian dinegarre, Spanish denegar), from de- "away" (see de-) + negare "refuse, say 'no,' " from Old Latin nec "not," from Italic base *nek- "not," from PIE root *ne- "no, not" (see un-).

late 14c., "track cut through a wood," later "long, narrow ditch" (late 15c.), from Old French trenche "a slice, cut, gash, slash; defensive ditch" (13c., Modern French tranche), from trenchier "to cut, carve, slice," possibly from Vulgar Latin *trincare, from Latin truncare "to cut or lop off" (see truncate).

early 15c., "full of misery, causing wretchedness" (of conditions), from Old French miserable "prone to pity, merciful," and directly from Latin miserabilis "pitiable, miserable, deplorable, lamentable," from miserari "to pity, lament, deplore," from miser "wretched" (see miser).

sea-storm of severest intensity, 1550s, a partially deformed adoptation from Spanish huracan (Gonzalo Fernandez de Oviedo y Valdés, "Historia General y Natural de las Indias," 1547-9), furacan (in the works of Pedro Mártir De Anghiera, chaplain to the court of King Ferdinand and Queen Isabella and historian of Spanish explorations), from an Arawakan (W.

mid-14c., "to rejoice" (now always with in), from Old French gloriier "glorify; pride oneself on, boast about," and directly from Latin gloriari which in classical use meant "to boast, vaunt, brag, pride oneself," from gloria (see glory (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 曲折的；锯齿形的；之字形的
使成之字形；使曲折行进
曲折地；之字形地；Z字形地
次要的,二级的;中级的,第二的

etymology.story: late 14c., from Latin secundarius "pertaining to the second class, inferior," from secundus (see second (adj.)).
mid-14c., perhaps from Anglo-French bage or from Anglo-Latin bagis, plural of bagia "emblem," all of unknown origin..

1590s, "a sending abroad," originally of Jesuits, from Latin missionem (nominative missio) "act of sending, a despatching; a release, a setting at liberty; discharge from service, dismissal," noun of action from past participle stem of mittere "to send," oldest form probably *smittere, of unknown origin.
"import or export secretly and contrary to law," 1680s, of Low German or Dutch origin (see smuggler).

meanings.definitionCn: (Sue)人名；(日)末(名)；(法)休；(英)休(女子教名Susan、Susanna的昵称)
进入；接入；到达；享用权；入口
原始的,远古的,早期的;粗糙的,简单的

morphology.mnemonic: 第一/首要 + 有…倾向的

etymology.story: 1640s, from advise (v.) + -able..
mid-15c., from Latin dormitorium "sleeping place," from dormire "to sleep" (see dormant).

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 工作 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: Old English ofercuman "to reach, overtake," also "to conquer, prevail over," from ofer (see over) + cuman "to come" (see come (v.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 总数， 和；金额；算术题

etymology.story: early 14c., "to count, count up, calculate, reckon," from Old French sommer "to count, add up," or directly from Medieval Latin summare, from summa (see sum (n.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 再/重新/回 + 召唤/引用

etymology.story: early 15c., from Old French reciter (12c.) and directly from Latin recitare "read aloud, read out, repeat from memory, declaim," from re- "back, again" (see re-) + citare "to summon" (see cite).
late 14c., from Middle French corant (Modern French courant), from Old French corant (see current (adj.)).

suffix.meaning: thing or animal spoken about before.

morphology.mnemonic: 听 + thing or animal spoken about before.

etymology.story: late 14c., "judge or authority on civil law," from Old French civilien "of the civil law," created from Latin civilis "relating to a citizen, relating to public life, befitting a citizen; popular, affable, courteous" (see civil).

meanings.definitionCn: 声音的；[声] 音频的，[声] 声频的

etymology.story: "sound," especially recorded or transmitted, 1934, abstracted from prefix audio- (in audio-frequency, 1919, etc.), from Latin audire "hear" (see audience)..

meanings.definitionCn: 攻击的；冒犯的；无礼的；讨厌的

morphology.mnemonic: action of attacking + 有…倾向的

meanings.definitionCn: 质地；纹理；结构；本质，实质

etymology.story: type of loose, light indoor footwear, late 15c., agent noun from slip (v.), the notion being of a shoe that is "slipped" onto the foot.

meanings.definitionCn: 发射（导弹、火箭等）；发起，发动；使…下水
发射；发行，投放市场；下水；汽艇

etymology.story: late 14c., from past participle of verb belove (c.

meanings.definitionCn: 确认；确定；证实；批准；使巩固

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 坚固/稳定

etymology.story: mid-13c., confirmyn "to ratify," from Old French confermer (13c., Modern French confirmer) "strengthen, establish, consolidate; affirm by proof or evidence; anoint (a king)," from Latin confirmare "make firm, strengthen, establish," from com-, intensive prefix (see com-), + firmare "to strengthen," from firmus "strong, steadfast" (see firm (adj.)).

"insect," 1620s (earliest reference is to bedbugs), of unknown origin, probably but not certainly from or influenced by Middle English bugge "something frightening, scarecrow" (late 14c.), a meaning obsolete since the "insect" sense arose except in bugbear (1570s) and bugaboo (q.v.).

meanings.definitionCn: 飞驰；急速进行；急急忙忙地说

etymology.story: late 14c., "to decide, resolve; to arrange the time of (a meeting, etc.)," from Anglo-French appointer, Old French apointier "make ready, arrange, settle, place" (12c.), from apointer "duly, fitly," from phrase à point "to the point," from a- "to" (see ad-) + point "point," from Latin punctum (see point (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 广阔的；巨大的；大量的；巨额的

etymology.story: 1570s, "being of great extent or size," from Middle French vaste, from Latin vastus "immense, extensive, huge," also "desolate, unoccupied, empty." The two meanings probably originally attached to two separate words, one with a long -a- one with a short -a-, that merged in early Latin (see waste (v.)).

1580s, "intercept" (obsolete), a back-formation from intervention, or else from Latin intervenire "to come between, intervene; interrupt; stand in the way, oppose, hinder," from inter "between" (see inter-) + venire "to come" (see venue).
1660s (trans.), from Latin ignitus, past participle of ignire "set on fire, make red hot," from ignis "fire" (see igneous).

early 15c., "creed, summary, religious belief," from Late Latin symbolum "creed, token, mark," from Greek symbolon "token, watchword, sign by which one infers; ticket, a permit, license" (the word was applied c.250 by Cyprian of Carthage to the Apostles' Creed, on the notion of the "mark" that distinguishes Christians from pagans), literally "that which is thrown or cast together," from assimilated form of syn- "together" (see syn-) + bole "a throwing, a casting, the stroke of a missile, bolt, beam," from bol-, nominative stem of ballein "to throw" (see ballistics).

early 14c., from Old French declarer "explain, elucidate," or directly from Latin declarare "make clear, reveal, disclose, announce," from de- intensive prefix (see de-) + clarare "clarify," from clarus "clear" (see clear (adj.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 启示；揭露；出乎意料的事；被揭露的真相
可用的， 可得到的；可以见到的， 随时可来的

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 强壮/价值 + able

morphology.mnemonic: 向/朝/在 + 呼叫/声音 + 使…/具有…的

meanings.definitionCn: 限制；使不外出，禁闭n.[pl.]界限，范围

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + to punish by pecuniary penalty

etymology.story: 1520s, "to border on," from Middle French confiner, from confins (n.); see confine (n.).

1550s, "state of being crowded" (now obsolete); 1640s, "fact of occurring often;" from Latin frequentia "an assembling in great numbers, a crowding; crowd, multitude, throng," from frequentem (see frequent).

meanings.definitionCn: 水平的；地平线的；同一阶层的
水平线，水平面；水平位置

etymology.story: 1550s, "relating to or near the horizon," from French horizontal, from Latin horizontem (see horizon).
1590s, from lug (v.) "to drag" + -age; so, literally "what has to be lugged about" (or, in Johnson's definition, "any thing of more weight than value").
"hindrance, disadvantage,"1720, from draw (v.) + back (adv.).

meanings.definitionCn: 导弹的；可投掷的；用以发射导弹的

部门；扇形，扇区；象限仪；函数尺

etymology.story: 1560s, "section of a circle between two radii," from Late Latin sector "section of a circle," in classical Latin "a cutter, one who cuts," from sectus, past participle of secare "to cut" (see section (n.)).

late 12c., "sufferings of Christ on the Cross," from Old French passion "Christ's passion, physical suffering" (10c.), from Late Latin passionem (nominative passio) "suffering, enduring," from past participle stem of Latin pati "to suffer, endure," possibly from PIE root *pe(i)- "to hurt" (see fiend).

meanings.definitionCn: 极端的；极度的；偏激的；尽头的
极端；末端；最大程度；极端的事物
卷起；挤进；用某物舒适地裹住；使有褶裥；收拢；大口吃；使隐藏
食物；船尾突出部；缝摺；抱膝式跳水；活力；鼓声

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + 顺序/等级 + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1660s, "to place in the same rank," from Latin coordinare (see coordination).

meanings.definitionCn: 运动员，体育家；身强力壮的人

etymology.story: early 15c., from Latin athleta "a wrestler, athlete, combatant in public games," from Greek athletes "prizefighter, contestant in the games," agent noun from athlein "to contest for a prize," related to athlos "a contest" and athlon "a prize," which is of unknown origin.

morphology.mnemonic: 星 + 具有…特征/名词化

meanings.definitionCn: 使成管状；把…装管；用管输送

etymology.story: 1610s, from Middle French tube (15c.), from Latin tubus "tube, pipe," of unknown origin.

morphology.mnemonic: 船 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: early 15c., from Old French naval (14c.) and directly from Latin navalis "pertaining to a ship or ships," from navis "ship," from PIE *nau- (2) "boat" (source also of Sanskrit nauh, accusative navam "ship, boat;" Armenian nav "ship;" Greek naus "ship," nautes "sailor;" Old Irish nau "ship;" Welsh noe "a flat vessel;" Old Norse nor "ship").

morphology.mnemonic: 出/向外/前任 + 悬挂/称量/支付

etymology.story: early 15c., from Latin expendere "pay out, weigh out money," from ex- "out" (see ex-) + pendere "to pay, weigh" (see pendant).

morphology.mnemonic: 向下/移除/取消 + animal of vast size + 使…/具有…的

etymology.story: 1550s, "to point out," from Latin demonstratus, past participle of demonstrare "to indicate, describe" (see demonstration).

meanings.definitionCn: 纪念碑,纪念馆,遗迹,不朽的业绩

etymology.story: late 13c., "a sepulchre," from Old French monument "grave, tomb, monument," and directly from Latin monumentum "a monument, memorial structure, statue; votive offering; tomb; memorial record," literally "something that reminds," from monere "to remind, warn" (see monitor (n.)).

meanings.definitionCn: 详细目录,存货,财产清册,总量
鲜艳的;生动的,栩栩如生的

etymology.story: 1630s, from French vivide and perhaps also directly from Latin vividus "spirited, animated, lively, full of life," from vivus "alive," from PIE *gweie- (1) "to live" (see bio-).

演习；谨慎地运用技巧移动， 运动；熟练而巧妙地引导某人

etymology.story: also turn-over, 1650s, "action of turning over," from the verbal phrase; see turn (v.) + over (adv.).

morphology.mnemonic: 死亡 + 与…有关的/形容词

etymology.story: early 15c., "having steps or ridges," from Medieval Latin gradualis, from Latin gradus "step" (see grade (n.)).

etymology.story: mid-15c., in reference to the nature of animals, from Latin brutus (see brute (adj.)) + -al (1).
1610s, from Latin praecludere "to close, shut off; hinder, impede," from prae- "before, ahead" (see pre-) + claudere "to shut" (see close (v.)).
mid-14c., "contaminate, impair the purity of," from Latin corruptus, past participle of corrumpere (see corrupt (adj.)).

morphology.mnemonic: 共同/联合 + sume + 状态/行为/过程

etymology.story: late 14c., "wasting of the body by disease; wasting disease" (replacing Old English yfeladl "the evil disease"), from Old French consumpcion, from Latin consumptionem (nominative consumptio) "a using up, wasting," noun of state from past participle stem of consumere (see consume).

meanings.definitionCn: 科学技术；工业技术；应用科学

morphology.mnemonic: 技术 + 具有…特征/名词化

etymology.story: 1610s, "a discourse or treatise on an art or the arts," from Greek tekhnologia "systematic treatment of an art, craft, or technique," originally referring to grammar, from tekhno- (see techno-) + -logy.
"matter used in vaccination," 1846, from French vaccin, noun use of adjective, from Latin vaccina, fem.

Old English drugað, drugoð "drought, dryness, desert," from Proto-Germanic *drugothaz, from Germanic root *dreug- "dry" (cf high/height) with *-itho, Germanic suffix for forming abstract nouns (see -th (2)).

morphology.mnemonic: dot marking end of a sentence + ic + 与…有关的/形容词

root.meaning: hinged flap on the side of a table

morphology.mnemonic: hinged flap on the side of a table + let

etymology.story: 1787 as a term in botany; 1867 as a term in printing and publication; diminutive of leaf (n.) with -let..

early 15c., "to take notice of," from Middle French advertiss-, present participle stem of a(d)vertir "to warn" (12c.), from Latin advertere "turn toward," from ad- "toward" (see ad-) + vertere "to turn" (see versus).
mid-15c., from Middle French comparatif, from Latin comparativus "pertaining to comparison," from comparat-, past participle stem of comparare (see comparison).
